title: He Jiankui and the CRISPR Twins: Germline Editing, Consent, and the Limits of Scientific Self-Regulation
text:
Who: He Jiankui (b. 1984), a Chinese biophysicist; “Lulu” and “Nana,” twin girls he claimed to have edited as embryos; a third child, sometimes called “Amy,” born in 2019.
What: He used CRISPR-Cas9 to disable the CCR5 gene in human embryos and implanted them, producing the first known live births of gene-edited babies.
Where / When: Shenzhen, China. The experiment ran from 2017 to 2018. He revealed it on November 25, 2018, twenty-four hours before the Second International Summit on Human Gene Editing opened in Hong Kong. He was sentenced to three years in prison in December 2019 and released in 2022.
Why it matters: This is the first known clinical use of human germline editing — changes to DNA that pass to every cell of the resulting person and to their descendants. The case is a study in what a careful, decade-long international near-consensus can and cannot do when one ambitious actor decides not to wait.
Pair with: Genomics & Enhancement

title: He Jiankui and the CRISPR Twins: Germline Editing, Consent, and the Limits of Scientific Self-Regulation
text:
The Case
The world found out on a Sunday night. On November 25, 2018, the technology reporter Antonio Regalado published an article in MIT Technology Review describing a Chinese clinical trial that was, the title said, “creating CRISPR babies.” Within hours the Associated Press confirmed the story: a researcher named He Jiankui had used gene editing on human embryos, implanted them, and a pair of twin girls — he called them Lulu and Nana — had been born about a month earlier. He posted five short YouTube videos the same night. In one of them, against a clean white wall, he explained calmly that he had “edited a gene” to give two healthy newborn girls “the ability to resist HIV” (Greely 2019).
The Second International Summit on Human Gene Editing was scheduled to open in Hong Kong the next morning. He was on the program. He had not told the organizers what he was about to say.

Some vocabulary the story turns on. CRISPR-Cas9 is a programmable tool that scientists use to cut DNA at a chosen spot. A short guide molecule finds the target sequence; the Cas9 enzyme makes the cut; the cell repairs the cut, and the edit takes effect.
A change made to one tissue in one person — say, to a patient’s bone marrow — is a somatic edit. It is not inherited. A change made to an early embryo (or to sperm or egg cells) is a germline edit. It ends up in every cell of the resulting person, including her own eggs or sperm, and so passes to her children, her grandchildren, and onward.
Somatic editing is now standard medicine. The FDA approved the first somatic CRISPR therapy, Casgevy for sickle-cell disease, in December 2023. Germline editing in human embryos meant for live birth has been banned for clinical use in nearly every country with the relevant law. The He experiment is the only known exception.

He Jiankui was 34. He had a physics doctorate from Rice University and a postdoctoral fellowship at Stanford with Stephen Quake, where he had worked on DNA sequencing technology. In 2012 he had returned to China under the Thousand Talents recruitment program and joined the new Southern University of Science and Technology in Shenzhen. He started a sequencing company, Direct Genomics, in 2012, with the Nobel laureate Craig Mello on its scientific advisory board. By 2017 he had decided to do something his sequencing background did not qualify him for. He would use CRISPR to make HIV-resistant children.
His recruitment plan ran through an AIDS support organization in Beijing called Baihualin. Eight Chinese couples agreed to take part. In every case the father was HIV-positive and the mother was not. He paid each couple about 280,000 yuan, roughly $40,000, to cover the procedures. The consent form they signed was twenty-three pages long. Its first sentence described what they were joining as “an AIDS vaccine development project.” It said the goal was to help produce “HIV-resistant infants” (Greely 2019). The form spent more pages on the financial liabilities that would attach if the parents talked to anyone about the project than it spent on what gene editing actually is.
He’s target was the CCR5 gene, which encodes a protein on the surface of certain immune cells. Most strains of HIV use the CCR5 protein as the door through which they enter the cell. About 1% of people of European ancestry are born with two copies of a natural variant of the gene called CCR5Δ32, in which a 32-letter stretch is deleted. People with two copies of CCR5Δ32 are largely resistant to those strains of HIV. He’s plan was to use CRISPR to make a similar deletion in human embryos.

The medical problem was already solved. Couples in which one partner is HIV-positive and the other is not have safely had biological children for years. The standard methods are sperm washing followed by intrauterine insemination or IVF, with antiretroviral therapy for the positive parent. Transmission rates are effectively zero. There was no medical need for any of these eight couples to have their embryos edited (Greely 2019).
The risks were real. CRISPR can cut DNA at unintended sites — called off-target effects — with unknown consequences. Edits in a single-cell embryo can fail to copy to every later cell, producing a mosaic child whose tissues carry different edits. And the protein He was disabling does many things besides serve as a doorway for HIV; CCR5 plays roles in cognition, in resistance to West Nile virus, and possibly in lifespan. The edits would be present in the children’s eggs, and pass to any children they had.

Five of the eight women became pregnant. One miscarried. One withdrew. One carried twins to term. The twins — He called them Lulu and Nana, and the names were almost certainly pseudonyms — were born by emergency C-section in October 2018, premature but healthy. He paid the medical costs and arranged for the family to disappear from public view. The DNA tests he ran on the babies before implantation showed that he had not actually reproduced the natural CCR5Δ32 deletion. He had produced novel variants, with effects no one had ever studied, in two living children. He implanted the embryos anyway.
He flew to Hong Kong on November 26 and walked onto the Summit stage on November 28. The room had spent the previous twenty-four hours absorbing Regalado’s article. He was visibly calm. He apologized for not telling the organizers in advance. He said he was proud of what he had done. He said the parents were “well-educated.” He took questions and answered most of them with confidence that surprised his critics more than his content did.
By that evening the Chinese vice-minister of science and technology had announced an investigation. Within a week, 122 Chinese scientists had signed an open letter calling He’s experiment “madness.” His university said it knew nothing about the work. The hospital ethics committee whose approval He had cited said its signatures had been forged. He was placed under what looked like loose detention at his apartment in Shenzhen for most of 2019.
A second woman in the trial had been pregnant when He spoke in Hong Kong. She gave birth, also in secret, in 2019. That third child — sometimes called Amy in the limited reporting since — appears to be alive and healthy. Almost nothing else about her, Lulu, or Nana has been disclosed.
In December 2019 a Chinese court convicted He of “illegal medical practice” and sentenced him to three years in prison and a three-million-yuan fine. Two collaborators received shorter terms. He was banned for life from human reproductive research. He served his sentence and was released in 2022. He has since opened a small lab in Beijing, given interviews defending the original experiment, and announced plans for “rare disease” gene therapy work (Cyranoski 2020). The international consensus against clinical germline editing has held for now. The question is what, exactly, the consensus is for.

title: He Jiankui and the CRISPR Twins: Germline Editing, Consent, and the Limits of Scientific Self-Regulation
text:
The Argument He Started
He’s experiment broke no clear American law, because it was not done in America. It broke Chinese laws ambiguously enough that a court had to reach for “illegal medical practice.” What it broke unambiguously was a long, careful, near-unanimous statement from the world’s leading scientific bodies that this exact step should not yet be taken. The argument the case started is not just about He. It is about what scientific self-regulation can do when one person decides not to listen.

His Defense
In Hong Kong, He defended what he had done as a serious response to a serious disease. The argument he made, paraphrased, ran something like this.

He Jiankui’s Defense

HIV is a serious disease, especially in families where one parent is already infected.
CRISPR editing of CCR5 can confer resistance to the strains of HIV that use that protein.
The parents understood the procedure and consented to it.
Therefore, this experiment was an ethically acceptable use of gene editing.


Premise 2 is where the defense falls. The edits He actually made were not the natural CCR5Δ32 variant. They were novel deletions and substitutions, never studied in any organism, with unknown effects on immune function and brain development. He knew this before he implanted the embryos. Lulu and Nana may or may not have meaningful HIV resistance. They will, for the rest of their lives, carry edits whose downstream effects no one can predict.


The Risk-Benefit Reply
Henry Greely, in the most thorough early analysis of the case, called it a “reckless ethical disaster” (Greely 2019). The objection is not philosophical at first. It is empirical: there was no benefit on offer, and the risks were real and lifelong.

The Risk-Benefit Reply

Standard reproductive medicine already prevents HIV transmission from infected fathers to their children, at very low cost and near-zero risk.
The CCR5 edits actually made by He did not match the natural protective variant and had never been studied in any organism.
Any unintended edits, off-target effects, or mosaicism in Lulu and Nana are permanent and heritable.
Therefore, the risk-benefit balance of this experiment was unjustifiable on standard research-ethics criteria.


This reply does most of the work needed to condemn what He did. It does not yet say anything special about germline editing. The next move asks whether the case would still have been wrong if the risks had somehow been low — and finds that consent itself does not stretch the way He needed it to stretch.


The Future Person’s Consent
The parents signed a form. Lulu and Nana did not — and could not. Once they exist, they cannot un-consent to the edits they were born with. Any children they have will inherit those edits in turn. The German philosopher Jürgen Habermas had argued, fifteen years before the case, that an asymmetry runs between children who are grown under nature’s contingency and children who are made under someone else’s specific plan: the made child cannot, in principle, retroactively endorse the design choices that produced her (Habermas 2003).

The Future Person’s Consent Reply

Germline edits affect every cell of the resulting person and pass to her descendants.
The persons most affected by a germline edit are the resulting child and her future descendants.
Those persons cannot consent before they exist, and cannot reverse the edit after they exist.
Therefore, parental consent alone is structurally insufficient to authorize a germline edit.


This is where Derek Parfit’s non-identity problem earns its place in the case. Lulu and Nana cannot complain that they were harmed by the edits, because the only alternative was that they not exist. A different embryo would have meant a different person, or no person at all. The wrong does not sit cleanly in “harm to specific children.” It sits in something subtler: in the kind of relationship He set up between himself, the parents, and two future adults who were, in Habermas’s phrase, made into the project of someone else’s life.


The Limits of Self-Regulation
A natural reply to all of this is: well, then, change the rules. But the rules were not the problem. The rules had already been written. They were not law. They were the carefully considered judgment of the relevant scientific community, repeated in detail.
In 2015, an international group of scientists and ethicists meeting in Napa Valley — modeled on the 1975 Asilomar conference on recombinant DNA — published a statement in Science calling for a moratorium on clinical germline editing while the science and the ethics matured. The First International Summit, in Washington in December of that year, concluded that clinical germline editing should not proceed “unless and until (i) the relevant safety and efficacy issues have been resolved … and (ii) there is broad societal consensus.” In February 2017, the U.S. National Academies of Sciences and Medicine issued a 328-page report, Human Genome Editing: Science, Ethics, and Governance, listing ten specific criteria that would need to be met before any heritable germline edit could proceed (National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine 2017). The Nuffield Council on Bioethics, in July 2018, set out a similar framework for the United Kingdom.
He’s experiment, judged against these documents, failed almost every criterion. He had no clinical justification: the families could already have unaffected children. He had no pre-clinical safety data of the kind the Academies required. He had no oversight: his university said it knew nothing. He had no transparency: he confided in a handful of senior academics — including his Stanford postdoctoral supervisor Stephen Quake, the Nobel laureate Craig Mello, and the bioethicist William Hurlbut — and most of them either tried to dissuade him or simply did not inform anyone else when they could not (Greely 2019).

 The cascade of oversight that should have caught He’s experiment, and didn’t. Each box is a body that, on standard accounts of research ethics, would have been expected to know, ask, or stop the work. 



The diagram shows the structure of the failure. Self-regulation works when the relevant community shares norms and enforces them on each other. It assumes the deviant case does not happen, or if it does, that someone in the chain will speak. In He’s case, every box failed in its own way. His university did not look. His hospital ethics committee may have been deceived or simply ignored. The senior academics he confided in tried, individually, to talk him out of it, but did not raise alarms outside their conversations. By the time the international community knew, two children had been born.


What Has and Has Not Followed
The case is now seven years old. The consensus it tested has, so far, held.
In July 2021 the World Health Organization issued a framework for global governance of human genome editing, including recommendations for a global registry of clinical trials and a mechanism for whistleblowers. The Third International Summit at the Francis Crick Institute in March 2023 reaffirmed the moratorium on clinical germline editing while explicitly endorsing the development of somatic CRISPR therapies. In December 2023 the FDA approved Casgevy, the first somatic CRISPR therapy for sickle-cell disease, at a list price of about $2.2 million per patient. The same molecular tool that put He Jiankui in prison is now the technology behind a celebrated treatment.
And He himself, freed from prison in April 2022, has reopened a small Beijing laboratory. His public statements continue to defend the original experiment, framed now around “rare disease” gene therapy rather than HIV resistance (Cyranoski 2020). He has not been able to publish in any major journal, but he is not silent. He is not the only researcher in the world who could try this again. The 2021 WHO framework is a set of recommendations. The 2023 Summit was a meeting. The Academies’ criteria are guidance. None of these documents prevent a second He Jiankui. They only describe what one would, in retrospect, be guilty of.
The sharpened question that has not closed is this: is the international consensus holding because it is sound, or because no one has yet decided to break it again? Self-regulation produced eighteen months of horror in 2018 and one prison sentence in 2019. It has not, since, produced a structure that could stop the next attempt before another child is born.

title: He Jiankui and the CRISPR Twins: Germline Editing, Consent, and the Limits of Scientific Self-Regulation
text:
Discussion Questions

Reconstruct He Jiankui’s Defense and the Risk-Benefit Reply in standard form. They share one premise. Identify it and write the strongest version of that premise you can give.
Parfit’s non-identity problem suggests Lulu and Nana cannot have been harmed by the edits, because their alternative was nonexistence. Yet most readers feel strongly that the experiment was wrong. Where, exactly, does the wrong lie if not in harm to the twins? Use one ethical concept from the dialogue to ground your answer.
He’s consent form described the experiment as “an AIDS vaccine development project.” Suppose he had instead used the phrase “the first heritable human gene-editing experiment.” Would proper informed consent have been possible from the eight couples? Defend your view.
You are a research nurse at a fertility clinic. A patient mentions, in passing, that her husband is a biologist who has been “doing some CRISPR experiments on our IVF embryos at home before transfer.” What do you do, in what order, and what authority — clinical, legal, or professional — backs each step?
The FDA approved Casgevy in December 2023. It uses CRISPR to treat sickle-cell disease through somatic editing of a patient’s own stem cells. He Jiankui used the same molecular tool to alter the germline of twin girls. Drawing on the case, identify the single feature — not “intent,” not “regulation” — that does the moral work of separating these two uses of CRISPR.

title: He Jiankui and the CRISPR Twins: Germline Editing, Consent, and the Limits of Scientific Self-Regulation
text:
Further Reading

Henry T. Greely, “CRISPR’d babies: human germline genome editing in the ‘He Jiankui affair’,” Journal of Law and the Biosciences 6 (2019): 111–183. The fullest and most accessible single account, with extensive primary documents.
Make People Better (Cody Sheehy, dir.), 2022. A documentary built around Antonio Regalado’s reporting, with interviews from several principals.
National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine, Human Genome Editing: Science, Ethics, and Governance (National Academies Press, 2017). The pre-He report whose criteria the experiment violated.
David Cyranoski, “What CRISPR-baby prison sentences mean for research,” Nature 577 (2020): 154–155. Short, useful summary of the legal outcome and what it did and didn’t settle.

title: Kate Cox, Damla Karsan, and Conscience in Post-Dobbs Medicine: Refusal, Provision, and Professional Integrity
text:
Who: Kate Cox, a thirty-one-year-old mother of two from suburban Dallas; Dr. Damla Karsan, her OB-GYN; Ken Paxton, the Attorney General of Texas.
What: In December 2023, Cox became the first pregnant woman since Roe v. Wade was overturned to sue in open court for the right to an abortion. A trial judge granted her request. Within hours, Paxton wrote to three Texas hospitals warning that anyone performing the procedure could still face prosecution. The Texas Supreme Court ruled against her. Karsan, the named physician-plaintiff, would have faced up to life in prison if she had gone ahead.
Where / When: Travis County and the Texas Supreme Court, December 2023. The broader question is national and ongoing.
Why it matters: The case forces a hard question about conscientious refusal and its mirror image, conscientious provision. When a clinician’s medical and moral judgment conflicts with the law of the state in which she practices, what does professional integrity require? Dobbs did not invent that question, but it inverted it.
Pair with: Abortion (The Law and Reproductive Rights)

title: Kate Cox, Damla Karsan, and Conscience in Post-Dobbs Medicine: Refusal, Provision, and Professional Integrity
text:
The Case
In August 2023, Kate Cox saw a positive home pregnancy test and ran into the playroom where her husband Justin was wrestling on the floor with their two toddlers. The Coxes had always wanted a large family — three or four children. They lived in a Dallas-area bungalow with two child-sized lawn chairs in the backyard, two sippy cups in the sink, and an Olaf the snowman in the living room. Neither Kate nor Justin was particularly political. When the Supreme Court issued its 2022 decision in Dobbs v. Jackson Women’s Health Organization, returning the regulation of abortion to the states, the news barely registered with them. Texas’s strict ban, they assumed, would have medical exceptions that would cover anything serious.
At eighteen weeks, Cox’s obstetrician called while she was driving and asked her to pull over. Early screening tests suggested Trisomy 18. This is a chromosomal condition in which a third copy of chromosome 18 produces severe developmental abnormalities. Over the next several weeks of ultrasounds and maternal-fetal-medicine consultations, the diagnosis was confirmed. The fetus had malformations of:

The brain.
The spine.
The neural tube.
The heart.
The skull.
The limbs.

“Each time we had an ultrasound, there was more bad news,” Cox later told Time. The expected outcome was either death in utero or, if the baby survived birth, hospice care and death within hours or days.

Trisomy 18 (Edwards syndrome). Trisomy 18 occurs when a fetus has three copies of chromosome 18 instead of two. Roughly half of fetuses with the condition are stillborn. Among live births, most die within the first week. A small minority of children with mosaic forms live longer, sometimes into adolescence, generally with profound disabilities. There is no curative treatment. Standard care is “comfort care” — keeping the infant warm and free of pain — rather than aggressive intervention.

The diagnosis also created acute risks for Cox herself. Her two previous deliveries had been by Cesarean section. A third C-section, or an induced labor at term following a fetal demise, carried elevated risks:

Uterine rupture.
Hemorrhage.
Hysterectomy — meaning she might never be able to bear another child.

She had already been to the emergency room several times for cramping and elevated vital signs. Her physician, Dr. Damla Karsan, judged that termination of the pregnancy was the medically indicated course.
Texas law at the time forbade nearly all abortions. The statutes permitted a narrow exception when, in the “reasonable medical judgment” of the attending physician, the pregnancy placed the woman at risk of “a serious risk of substantial impairment of a major bodily function.” The exception was poorly defined. Karsan believed Cox qualified. She also believed that if she was wrong — or if a prosecutor disagreed — she could face severe penalties:

Loss of her medical license.
Fines of at least $100,000 per violation.
Up to ninety-nine years in prison.


The Texas medical exception. Texas’s overlapping abortion statutes (the Human Life Protection Act and Senate Bill 8) permit abortion only when, in the physician’s “reasonable medical judgment,” the patient faces a “life-threatening physical condition” or “a serious risk of substantial impairment of a major bodily function.” The statutes:

Do not list qualifying diagnoses.
Do not provide a pre-clearance mechanism.
Require the physician to make the call alone.

A prosecutor may second-guess that call afterward. The physician bears the burden of proving the judgment was reasonable.

On December 5, 2023, with Cox now twenty weeks pregnant, the Center for Reproductive Rights filed Cox v. Texas on behalf of Cox, her husband, and Dr. Karsan. They asked a Travis County district judge for a temporary restraining order — a court order blocking the state from prosecuting Karsan or her hospital if she performed the abortion. Two days later, Judge Maya Guerra Gamble granted it. From the bench, she said: “The idea that Ms. Cox wants desperately to be a parent, and this law might actually cause her to lose that ability, is shocking and would be a genuine miscarriage of justice.”
Within hours, the Texas Attorney General, Ken Paxton, sent letters to three Houston-area hospitals where Karsan held privileges. The letters made three points:

The trial court’s order did not insulate anyone from civil liability under SB 8, which authorizes private lawsuits for at least $100,000 per abortion.
The order also did not insulate anyone from criminal prosecution after the order was lifted, which Paxton was actively seeking.
The hospitals should consider their own exposure.

Paxton then petitioned the Texas Supreme Court for an emergency stay.
By the time the Texas Supreme Court ruled, on December 11, 2023, Kate Cox had already left the state. The court held that Karsan had not met the statutory standard. Her sworn declaration that Cox faced a serious risk of impairment of a major bodily function was, in the court’s view, an opinion — not the demonstration that the statute required. “Only a doctor can exercise ‘reasonable medical judgment’ to decide whether a pregnant woman ‘has a life-threatening physical condition,’” the court wrote. “A woman who meets the medical-necessity exception need not seek a court order. … But the law does not permit a court to grant a pre-authorization” (Supreme Court of Texas 2023). Cox obtained the abortion in New Mexico.
The figure who interests us in what follows is not the patient but the physician. Damla Karsan had not been compelled to take Cox’s case. She could have referred Cox out of state — many Texas OB-GYNs did — and avoided the criminal exposure entirely. She chose instead to do three risky things:

Put her name on the lawsuit.
Swear under oath that the medical-exception standard was met.
Publicly disagree with a state law she had to live under.

She was not refusing to participate in a procedure she found morally objectionable. She was insisting on participating in a procedure she believed she was morally obligated to provide. The question her case raises is whether the profession of medicine has room for that kind of conscience too.

title: Kate Cox, Damla Karsan, and Conscience in Post-Dobbs Medicine: Refusal, Provision, and Professional Integrity
text:
The Argument Karsan Started
The classical debate about conscience in medicine was built around the figure of the refuser. Three examples:

A Catholic pharmacist who would not dispense Plan B.
An OB-GYN who would not perform elective abortions.
A nurse who would not assist with physician-assisted dying.

The debate asked when, if ever, the law should protect such refusals. Karsan’s case raises a stranger version of the question: when, if ever, should the law protect provision? The philosophical materials built for the first question travel awkwardly into the second.

The integrity argument for conscientious refusal
The foundational defense of conscience clauses, given its sharpest form by the bioethicist Mark Wicclair, holds that being asked to perform an act one believes to be deeply wrong is being asked to damage one’s own moral integrity. Moral integrity is itself a basic human good that medicine should respect (Wicclair 2000). A pharmacist who believes emergency contraception ends a human life is not, on this view, merely expressing a preference. She is being asked to act against her core identity.

The Integrity Argument for Refusal

Moral integrity — the consistency between a person’s deeply held moral commitments and her actions — is a basic component of human well-being.
Compelling a clinician to perform an act she sincerely believes to be seriously wrong damages that integrity.
The state and the profession should not damage a clinician’s integrity unless doing so is necessary to prevent serious harm to others.
Therefore, clinicians have a defeasible right to refuse to participate in legal procedures they find morally abhorrent.


The disputed premise is the third, which depends on what “serious harm to others” amounts to. If a refusal merely inconveniences a patient who has other options, the right to refuse holds. If a refusal denies a patient timely access to legal, indicated care — particularly in a region with no alternative provider — the right runs out. That is where the principal reply lives.


Stahl and Emanuel’s reply: physicians are not conscripts
The bioethicists Ronit Stahl and Ezekiel Emanuel argue that the comparison to military conscientious objection breaks down at the point of voluntariness. Soldiers are drafted. Physicians are licensed. Medicine is a profession one enters voluntarily. The entry deal goes like this: a clinician accepts fiduciary obligations to patients in exchange for state-granted privileges. To accept the license and then refuse the obligations is to want the benefit without the burden (Stahl and Emanuel 2017).

The Stahl-Emanuel Reply

Medicine is a public profession whose members voluntarily accept fiduciary obligations to patients in exchange for state-granted privileges.
Those obligations include the duty to provide legal, professionally indicated care that the patient requests, regardless of the clinician’s personal moral views.
Clinicians who cannot in conscience meet those obligations should leave the relevant specialty, not import their personal morality into the patient encounter.
Therefore, conscience clauses that exempt clinicians from providing legal, indicated care are illegitimate.


The soft spot here is the third premise. It works smoothly for a young medical student choosing a specialty. It works clumsily for a thirty-year veteran of obstetrics whose state legislature changes the rules underneath her. The argument also threatens to swallow more than its authors may have intended. The same logic would seem to compel participation in:

MAID (medical aid in dying).
Capital punishment medical clearances.
Any other lawful practice the state designates as professionally indicated.



Brock’s counter-reply: the conventional compromise
The philosopher Dan Brock proposed, well before Dobbs, what he called the “conventional compromise.” A clinician may refuse personally, but must meet three conditions:

Disclose the refusal in advance.
Refer the patient to another qualified provider.
Not abandon the patient mid-care.

The refusing pharmacist hands the prescription to a colleague. The refusing OB-GYN provides the name of a clinic across town. Brock then anticipated and rejected the “complicity” objection — that referring is itself moral participation — by arguing that the moral weight of a referral is too thin to constitute the kind of agency that integrity protects (Brock 2008).
The conventional compromise was, for two decades, the working consensus of American medical ethics. It held because it assumed a background condition: that a willing provider was somewhere. In a country where abortion was legal everywhere and contraception was available at any pharmacy, the refusing clinician could always point to a door. Dobbs removed the assumption. In a state with no in-state legal provider, the obligation to “refer” collapses into a duty to direct the patient to break the law, leave the state, or wait.


The reframing: conscientious provision
This is where the case stops being about refusal and starts being about its mirror. The classical defenders of conscientious refusal — Wicclair, Brock, even the dissenters Stahl and Emanuel — were arguing about a different setting. In their setting, the contested care was legal, and the contested clinician was the one declining to provide it. Lisa Harris and her co-authors at the Hastings Center Report describe the post-Dobbs clinician as living under a “sword of Damocles.” The contested care is now criminalized. The contested clinician is the one who provides it (Harris et al. 2024). The integrity argument has not gone away. It has changed direction.
The sharpened version of the inversion comes from Isa Ryan, Ashish Premkumar, and Katie Watson in the AMA Journal of Ethics (Ryan et al. 2022). They draw a distinction the classical literature mostly elides:

Personal morality — the convictions one holds as a private individual.
Role morality — the ethical obligations one takes on by accepting a professional role.

The pre-Dobbs conscience clause is a personal-morality protection. The pharmacist’s private view of emergency contraception is shielded from the role’s demands. Ryan and her co-authors argue that the post-Dobbs landscape now demands the role-morality analogue. Codes of professional ethics should protect clinicians who provide medically indicated care that their state has criminalized. That provision is what the role itself requires. In their words: “Codes of medical ethics should be updated to state that, where abortion is illegal, breaking the law to provide care for reasons of conscience is not unethical.”

The Argument for Conscientious Provision

Personal morality grounds conscientious refusal: a clinician may decline care she sincerely believes is wrong, on the same integrity grounds the refusal tradition recognizes.
Role morality grounds professional obligation: by accepting the role of obstetrician or emergency physician, a clinician takes on duties to her patients that are not reducible to her private moral views.
When state law criminalizes care that role morality requires, role-based conscience generates the same kind of integrity claim as personal-based conscience does — pointed toward provision rather than refusal.
Therefore, the profession should protect conscientious provision on par with conscientious refusal, including, where necessary, by recognizing that providing standard-of-care medicine in defiance of an unjust law is itself an ethical act.


A defender of the asymmetry will press on the third premise. Refusal, the objection runs, is a negative claim — leave me out of it — that can be honored without anyone breaking the law. Provision is a positive claim that, where care is illegal, requires law-breaking. That is too much to ask the profession to underwrite. Ryan and her co-authors anticipate the objection. They argue that the asymmetry is itself an artifact of the prior legal regime, not a deep feature of conscience. What the profession underwrites is not law-breaking as such. It is the role’s commitment to patient welfare when the law fails it.
The case that haunts this move is not American. In October 2012, Savita Halappanavar, a thirty-one-year-old dentist living in Galway, was admitted to University Hospital Galway at seventeen weeks of pregnancy with an inevitable miscarriage. Her membranes had ruptured. The fetus was not viable. Irish law at the time prohibited abortion unless the woman’s life was at risk. The medical team waited several days for the fetal heartbeat to stop on its own. Halappanavar developed sepsis. By the time the pregnancy was terminated, the infection was uncontrollable. She died on October 28, 2012. The case galvanized Irish public opinion and led to the 2018 referendum that repealed the constitutional ban. Damla Karsan, in Houston in December 2023, was practicing in a legal environment that asked of her what Halappanavar’s physicians had been asked of in 2012 — to wait until a patient was sick enough.


The move that hasn’t ended
What follows from the inversion is structural, not symmetrical. Pre-Dobbs conscience clauses protected individual clinicians from the demands of the profession. The new conscience question runs through institutions and jurisdictions — entities that classical bioethics did not theorize as conscience-bearers at all. Three current pressure points show the shape of the problem:

Catholic hospital systems. Roughly one in six U.S. acute-care hospital beds is now in a Catholic system, and in some states the share is much higher. Catholic hospital directives prohibit sterilization, most forms of contraception, and abortion even in cases like Cox’s. Clinicians who work in those systems are bound by institutional conscience whether or not they share its commitments.
State shield laws. A growing patchwork of state shield laws — New York, Massachusetts, California, and others — now protect clinicians who mail mifepristone into states that ban it. This poses the constitutional question of whether a state may reach across its borders to prosecute a doctor whose conduct is legal where she stands.
EMTALA conflicts. The federal Emergency Medical Treatment and Labor Act, which requires hospitals to stabilize patients in emergencies, has come into direct conflict with state bans. The Supreme Court’s 2024 ruling in Moyle v. United States sent the question back for further litigation without resolving it.

These are not problems Wicclair or Brock anticipated. Their framework was built for the individual clinician with a private moral commitment. It is now being asked to mediate between four very different parties:

State legislatures.
Federal statutes.
Religious health systems.
Clinicians whose oaths now conflict with their licenses.

The sharpened open question is no longer whether conscience matters in medicine. It is whose conscience the law should recognize when the conscience of a state, a hospital, and a physician all point in different directions.

title: Kate Cox, Damla Karsan, and Conscience in Post-Dobbs Medicine: Refusal, Provision, and Professional Integrity
text:
Discussion Questions

Write out the Integrity Argument for Refusal and the Argument for Conscientious Provision in your own words — premises first, then the conclusion. The two arguments share one premise. Identify it. Then say whether the post-Dobbs legal environment changes which side that shared premise now supports.
Stahl and Emanuel argue that clinicians who cannot meet a profession’s obligations should leave the specialty. Does that response work for Damla Karsan — who completed her training years before Texas changed its abortion law? Give the strongest version of Stahl and Emanuel’s reply to Karsan. Then give the strongest version of Karsan’s reply back.
Brock’s conventional compromise says a clinician may refuse to provide care she objects to, as long as she refers the patient to someone who will. Brock’s rule depends on one assumption about the world that has to be true for the rule to work. What is that assumption? Describe a present-day case where the assumption no longer holds. Does the compromise still work in some modified form, or does it have to be abandoned?
You are a labor-and-delivery nurse in a Catholic hospital in a state with a near-total abortion ban. An ER patient is admitted at seventeen weeks. Her membranes have ruptured. The fetus still has a heartbeat, likely to stop on its own in a day or two. Her white-blood-cell count is rising — an early sign of infection. Hospital policy and state law both require waiting. Your professional judgment tells you the patient is heading toward sepsis. What do you do? Which argument from the case is doing the work in your answer?
Catholic hospital systems describe themselves as institutions with a conscience. Many state legislatures describe their abortion statutes as expressions of the moral conscience of the people who voted for them. The integrity argument was built for individual people with consciences. Does it carry over to an institution or to a state? Pick one and defend your answer.

title: Kate Cox, Damla Karsan, and Conscience in Post-Dobbs Medicine: Refusal, Provision, and Professional Integrity

title: The Tuskegee and Guatemala Syphilis Studies: Research Ethics, Whistleblowing, and Medical Distrust
text:
Who: About 600 Black men of Macon County, Alabama — 399 with latent syphilis, 201 uninfected controls — and, later, more than 1,300 Guatemalans deliberately infected by the same U.S. agency.
What: The U.S. Public Health Service watched untreated syphilis progress to autopsy for 40 years (1932–1972). The agency withheld penicillin and deceived the men into believing they were being treated. A parallel PHS project in Guatemala (1946–1948) infected prisoners, soldiers, and psychiatric patients on purpose.
Where / When: Tuskegee/Macon County, Alabama, 1932–1972; Guatemala, 1946–1948. Exposed by a whistleblower and the press in 1972. The Guatemala records were found in 2003.
Why it matters: Tuskegee is widely called the single most important event in the rise of American bioethics. It produced the modern rules — informed consent, the IRB, the Belmont principles — and a debate, still unsettled, about whether rules were ever the thing that was missing, and about the medical distrust the study left behind.
Pair with: History & the Making of Research Ethics

title: The Tuskegee and Guatemala Syphilis Studies: Research Ethics, Whistleblowing, and Medical Distrust
text:
The Case
In 1932, the United States Public Health Service (PHS) — the federal agency that would become the CDC — began what its own scientists titled the Tuskegee Study of Untreated Syphilis in the Negro Male. The premise was simple. To its designers, it was scientifically attractive: find people with untreated syphilis and observe what the disease does to a human body if nothing is done, all the way to autopsy.
They chose Macon County, Alabama. Most residents were Black. Most were poor. Almost none had access to a doctor. That was the point. The men recruited — about 600 in all — were sharecroppers working white-owned land in a system of debt peonage. Of the 600:

399 had latent syphilis.
201 served as healthy controls.

The PHS enlisted the respected Tuskegee Institute and a trusted Black public-health nurse, Eunice Rivers, whose constancy kept the men coming back for decades. The men were never told they had syphilis. They were told they had “bad blood,” a vague local phrase, and that the government doctors were treating them for it.

What untreated syphilis does. Syphilis is a bacterial infection. After an early stage it can go latent — silent for years — and then, in roughly a third of untreated cases, attack the heart, blood vessels, brain, and spinal cord. The consequences the PHS’s own venereal-disease workers printed on a matchbook to scare people into testing were blunt and accurate: “Blindness, heart injury, insanity, death.” None of that was named to the Tuskegee men.

The deception was not passive. To collect the data they wanted, investigators did several specific things:

They performed painful diagnostic spinal taps and called them a free “spinal shot” — a treatment.
One PHS officer wrote that the men might refuse the procedure if they understood it was only diagnostic. Its real purpose, he said, should “be kept from them as far as possible.”
Because the study’s final data point was the autopsy, the PHS offered burial insurance. This was a powerful inducement in a poor rural community where a proper funeral mattered. Families would surrender the bodies in return.
One investigator wrote that the team had “no further interest in these patients until they die.”
When a mobile treatment unit later swept the county offering free penicillin to everyone, PHS staff quietly flagged their enrollees to local doctors with the instruction that the man was under study and not to be treated.







Figure 1: A Public Health Service worker draws blood from a study participant during an annual “roundup,” Milstead, Macon County, 1953. Nurse Eunice Rivers stands at left.




National Archives and Records Administration. Public domain. Reproduced in (Tobin 2022).

Penicillin became the standard cure for syphilis in the mid-1940s. The men were not given it. PHS scientists argued that the arrival of a cure made the study more valuable, not less. It was a never-to-be-repeated chance to see the full natural course of a disease that would soon no longer run its course anywhere else. The study generated about fifteen papers in reputable medical journals over thirty-seven years. The titles alone announced what it was — one 1964 article was called “The Tuskegee Study of Untreated Syphilis; the 30th Year of Observation.” A 1955 paper reported that more than thirty percent of the autopsied men had died directly of syphilis. No physician, anywhere, published a letter of objection.
It ended only because one PHS employee would not let it go. By the most-cited later reckonings:

Dozens of the men had died of syphilis or its complications.
Dozens of their wives had been infected.
Children had been born with congenital syphilis.

In 1973, after the story broke, a class-action suit produced an out-of-court settlement. The government eventually provided lifetime medical care to survivors and affected families. In 1997 — a quarter century after the study stopped — President Clinton apologized: what the government did “was shameful,” a study “so clearly racist.” No scientist was ever prosecuted.
The case is usually taught as a failure of consent. The questions it actually raises are harder than that, and they are still open.

title: The Tuskegee and Guatemala Syphilis Studies: Research Ethics, Whistleblowing, and Medical Distrust
text:
The Argument Tuskegee Started
Few cases have generated as much agreement that something monstrous happened and as much disagreement about what the monstrous thing was. The moves below trace that argument as a connected exchange — each answering the one before.

“We were missing rules”
The official lesson is institutional. Tuskegee, on this view, happened because the United States had no system requiring that subjects consent and no body to review research before it ran. So the country built one:

The 1974 National Research Act.
Institutional review boards (IRBs) to approve and monitor studies.
Federal informed-consent regulations.
In 1979, the Belmont Report’s three principles — respect for persons, beneficence, and justice.


Informed consent, the IRB, and Belmont. Informed consent means a competent person is told the truth about a study’s risks, benefits, and purpose and freely agrees to take part. An IRB is a committee that must approve human research before it begins and can stop it. The Belmont Report distilled the post-Tuskegee consensus into three duties:

Respect persons as autonomous agents.
Do not harm and maximize benefit.
Distribute research’s burdens and benefits fairly.

These are the spine of every research-ethics rulebook in use today (National Commission for the Protection of Human Subjects of Biomedical and Behavioral Research 1979).


The Regulatory-Gap Argument

If a system lacks independent review and consent regulations, then research abuses like Tuskegee are inevitable.
Modern research systems now have independent review and consent regulations.
Therefore, research abuses like Tuskegee are no longer possible in modern research systems.


Premise 1 is where this breaks. It assumes the gap was the absence of rules. The next move denies that.


“The rules existed — and were ignored”
The Nuremberg Code was written in 1947 after Nazi doctors were tried for experimenting on prisoners. It opens with the sentence “The voluntary consent of the human subject is absolutely essential.” It existed for the last twenty-five years of Tuskegee. American researchers of the era reportedly dismissed it as “a good code for barbarians” — something for Nazis, not for them. Rules were not missing. They were not felt to apply.
Then there is Guatemala. While researching Surgeon General Thomas Parran’s papers in 2003, the historian Susan Reverby opened the donated files of a PHS doctor named John Cutler and found something worse than Tuskegee (Reverby 2011). Between 1946 and 1948, the same Public Health Service had deliberately infected more than 1,300 Guatemalans — prisoners, soldiers, and patients in a psychiatric hospital — with syphilis, gonorrhea, and chancroid, to test prevention. When infection through arranged contact proved unreliable, investigators inoculated subjects directly. At least eighty-three deaths were recorded. One psychiatric patient, after months of induced disease, was dying when a researcher infected her further. She died days later.
The decisive fact about Guatemala is not its cruelty but its respectability. Consider the chain of approval:

The principal investigator, John Mahoney, was arguably the most honored American physician-scientist of his day. He won a Lasker Award the year the experiments began.
The proposal was reviewed and funded by an NIH study section staffed by physicians from the country’s leading medical schools.
The Surgeon General was kept informed and enthusiastic. He is recorded remarking that “we couldn’t do such an experiment in this country.”

Nothing failed in the chain of command. Everyone with authority approved.








G



sg

U.S. Surgeon General
(kept informed, approving)



div

PHS Venereal Disease Division
(directors: Clark, Vonderlehr, Heller)



sg->div





nih

NIH study section
(reviewed & funded Guatemala)



sg->nih





pi

Senior investigators
(Mahoney, Cutler, Wenger)



div->pi





nih->pi





site

On-site team &
Nurse Eunice Rivers
(kept the men enrolled)



pi->site





local

Local doctors instructed:
enrollee “not to be treated”



pi->local





subj

The men / the Guatemalan subjects



site->subj





local->subj





bux

Peter Buxtun
(PHS interviewer, no authority)



bux->div


  objects, 1966–1968






Figure 2: The PHS chain of command in the Tuskegee and Guatemala studies. The point of the diagram is what it does not contain: a broken link. Approval and knowledge flowed through every legitimate level of authority — the Surgeon General, the funding review, the senior investigators, the trusted on-site nurse — down to instructing local doctors that enrolled men were ‘not to be treated.’ The one person who finally stopped Tuskegee, Peter Buxtun, sits outside this structure entirely (dashed). A rule is enforced by the people inside the boxes; that is why ‘add a rule’ did not, by itself, answer the problem.






The Conscience Argument

Rules only prevent abuse if researchers believe the rules apply to them and their subjects.
The PHS researchers did not believe existing ethical rules applied to their subjects.
Therefore, the failure was a failure of institutional and individual conscience, not a lack of rules.


That individual was Peter Buxtun. The PHS hired him in 1965 as a venereal-disease interviewer in San Francisco. He was a Czech-born social worker whose family had fled to America in 1937. He learned of the study from a coworker, obtained a folder of internal “roundup” reports, and recognized the shape of what he was reading. He had read the Nuremberg trial record. His path inside the agency went like this:

He filed formal objections in 1966 and again in 1968.
The CDC summoned him to Atlanta, and a senior official berated him.
A blue-ribbon panel was convened in 1969. Its dominant voice argued, “You will never have another study like this; take advantage of it.” Only one member, Gene Stollerman, urged that the men be treated as patients rather than as a data set.
The panel voted to continue. Buxtun asked the CDC, in writing, “What is the ethical thing to do?” He received no answer.
He took the documents to the press. The story broke in July 1972.







Figure 3: Peter Buxtun, the PHS social worker whose complaints, ignored inside the agency for years, finally reached the press in 1972.




Public domain. Reproduced in (Tobin 2022).

Why did it take one outsider seven years, when no insider acted in forty? Buxtun’s own answer was wry: “It’s tough being a whistleblower when you don’t even know you’re a whistleblower.” The stronger pressure to weave in here is from social psychology. Stanley Milgram’s obedience experiments — and a later study modeled on them, in which university students were asked to recruit others into an obviously dangerous sham experiment — found that most people, given a legitimate-seeming authority, comply and look away. They do this even when dissent is safe, anonymous, and easy (Elliott 2018). Milgram called this surrender the “agentic state”: we recast ourselves as instruments of the authority and let our conscience go quiet. The Tuskegee physicians were not monsters by Milgram’s lights. They were ordinary people inside a structure that made silence feel normal.


“But heroes are not a policy”
If the Conscience Argument is right, it leaves a problem. You cannot run a research system on the hope that each abuse will produce its own Buxtun — especially since the system punishes the ones it gets.

The Institutional-Design Reply

Relying on individual conscience only works if whistleblowers can safely come forward.
Under current institutional designs, whistleblowers who come forward are routinely punished.
Therefore, preventing future research abuses requires structural institutional reform, not just appeals to personal courage.


The constructive half of this comes, again, from Milgram. Obedience dropped sharply under three specific conditions:

When the authority figure was made less imposing.
When the victim was made more present.
And — most powerfully — when even one other person in the room dissented.

The design implication is concrete:

Deflate the prestige of the lead researcher.
Make subjects visible rather than abstract.
Protect real (not cosmetic) channels for objection.
Make it possible for objectors to find each other.

The recurring real-world successes — the New Zealand “unfortunate experiment,” for instance — came from teams of clinicians acting together, not solitary martyrs. An anonymous “ethics hotline,” the usual institutional answer, is the thing the evidence trusts least.


Who was chosen, and why
There is a wrong here that none of the moves above quite names. The Tuskegee men were not random. They were selected because they were poor, Black, unschooled, and without doctors. They were chosen for the very powerlessness that made them easy to deceive and unlikely to be believed. A PHS internal phrasing reduced them to a single compound label, a string of demographic adjectives ending in “men.” That is not a consent failure or an oversight failure. It is a justice failure: a vulnerable group made to carry a burden for a science that would never have been done to the powerful.

Vulnerability and fair subject selection. Kenneth Kipnis argued that “vulnerable” is not a list of groups but a set of pressures a participant is under — economic, social, deferential, institutional — that compromise a genuinely free choice (Kipnis 2001). The Belmont principle of justice demands fair subject selection. Research must not load its risks onto those least able to refuse, nor (the mirror error) exclude groups from research’s benefits. Tuskegee is the textbook case of wrongful exploitation of the vulnerable. Modern ethics also watches for wrongful exclusion.


The Justice Reframing

In Tuskegee, subjects were selected specifically because their poverty and race made them unable to refuse or complain.
Selecting subjects because of their vulnerability is a form of exploitation that violates the principle of justice.
Therefore, the deepest wrong of Tuskegee was a violation of justice, which cannot be fixed merely by improving consent procedures.


This is not safely historical. Research today still gravitates toward people under exactly Kipnis’s pressures:

The institutionalized.
The incarcerated.
The cognitively impaired.
The poor.

That is why “fair subject selection” remains an active demand and not a settled box to check.


The argument that hasn’t ended
There is a final move, and it is the one with the most current stakes. The standard account says: Tuskegee is why many Black Americans distrust medicine and research, so the remedy is outreach, apology, and education to repair the trust the study broke. After 1972, the empirical legacy is, in fact, measurable. Marcella Alsan and Marianne Wanamaker found that disclosure of the study was followed by reduced medical contact among older Black men and worse health outcomes — a real and lasting cost (Alsan and Wanamaker 2018).
But Vanessa Northington Gamble and Susan Reverby press a sharp objection to the standard account. Black medical distrust did not begin with Tuskegee and is not exhausted by it. It tracks a long history and, more importantly, present mistreatment — documented disparities in pain treatment and outcomes, structural barriers in care now (Gamble 1997). To explain today’s distrust by pointing only to a study that ended in 1972 can become a way of locating the problem safely in the past. It can cast Black patients as irrationally haunted rather than accurately observant. (Reverby also notes a precise irony: the Tuskegee men were not distrustful refusers. They trusted the government doctors, which is exactly how they were used.) The COVID-19 vaccination gap became the live test of this dispute: was the right response to recite Tuskegee and reassure, or to address the present-day inequities that make wariness a reasonable inference?
What actually moved the gap is instructive. Early 2021 messaging that leaned on history-and-reassurance — that was then, the rules are different now, the vaccine is safe — tended to land poorly. It answered a question many Black patients were not asking. A review of equity strategies found that the interventions that raised uptake were structural and relational rather than rhetorical:

Vaccine sites placed inside trusted Black churches and barbershops.
Outreach delivered by community health workers, clergy, and Black clinicians rather than by the institutions themselves (Dada et al. 2022).

The recurring mechanism is concordance — patients are more likely to accept advice, including vaccination, from clinicians who share their background or have earned standing in their community. A randomized field experiment in Oakland found that Black men assigned to a Black physician agreed to substantially more preventive services, including invasive screening, than those assigned to a non-Black physician — a gap that no recitation of history opens or closes (Alsan et al. 2019). The lesson tracks Reverby’s point precisely: the operative variable was not how vividly the past was explained but how much present trustworthiness the patient could actually observe in the room.
That gives a clinician something to do rather than something to recite. It does not, however, settle the argument. It arguably concedes the critique’s premise — trust follows present conditions, not historical correction — while leaving the standard view’s strongest reply intact: history is part of why those present conditions are read the way they are, and a patient who invokes Tuskegee is often naming a pattern, not a date — distrust as a response to everyday racism, not only to one archived study (Bajaj and Stanford 2021). The dispute is which description should drive the response.

The Distrust Dispute

If historical events like Tuskegee are the primary cause of medical distrust, then distrust should be solved by historical education and apology.
Medical distrust is primarily caused by ongoing, present-day systemic medical racism and disparities.
Therefore, medical distrust can only be solved by changing present-day medical practices, not just explaining the past.


Fifty years on, that is where the argument rests — not at a verdict, but at a question that a nurse, a researcher, and a public-health officer each have to answer the next time someone says, reasonably, that they are not sure they trust the people offering to help them.

title: The Tuskegee and Guatemala Syphilis Studies: Research Ethics, Whistleblowing, and Medical Distrust
text:
Discussion Questions

Write out The Regulatory-Gap Argument and The Conscience Argument in your own words — premises first, then the conclusion. They disagree about one premise. Identify it. Then explain what the Guatemala study — run by a respected lead researcher, reviewed by NIH, approved by the U.S. Surgeon General — tells us about which side has the better case.
Nurse Eunice Rivers was trusted by the men in the study because she was caring, consistent, and one of their own. That trust is part of what kept them enrolled for decades. Imagine you are a nurse who has just realized that the “treatment” you have been giving is a deception. What do you owe these particular patients that the institution does not? What, concretely, do you do — knowing that nurses who reported similar abuses often lost their jobs?
The Justice Reframing says the deepest wrong of Tuskegee was who the researchers chose as subjects, not the missing consent forms. Imagine a future study with perfect informed consent — but where the volunteers are poor, uninsured, and badly need the money. Would the consent fix Tuskegee’s wrong, or just move it somewhere else? Use the idea of fair subject selection to defend your answer.
The Institutional-Design Reply says hospitals can do specific things to keep individual dissent from being silenced. Pick a hospital you know. Name one concrete change — not an ethics hotline — that would make a dissenting nurse or resident less alone. Drawing on Milgram’s obedience studies, explain why your change would actually work.
A patient eligible for a new vaccine tells you she does not trust it. She mentions Tuskegee. The case describes two ways to respond: reassure her and explain the science, or acknowledge that present conditions still give some patients real reasons to distrust. Give the strongest version of each response. Then say which you would actually give her, and why.

title: The Tuskegee and Guatemala Syphilis Studies: Research Ethics, Whistleblowing, and Medical Distrust
text:
Further Reading

James H. Jones, Bad Blood: The Tuskegee Syphilis Experiment (Free Press, 1981; rev. 1993) (Jones 1981) — the definitive history.
Susan M. Reverby, Examining Tuskegee: The Infamous Syphilis Study and Its Legacy (University of North Carolina Press, 2009) (Reverby 2009).

title: Lecture 6 — Genomics and Enhancement
text:
You and your partner are doing IVF. Six embryos make it to the testing stage. The clinic offers a new add-on for $50,000. They will read each embryo’s whole genome and rank them on predicted IQ, height, and risk for common diseases. You can pick the embryo at the top of the list. The doctor stresses that it is legal, voluntary, and currently used by other couples in the clinic.
Write or discuss:

Is this medical treatment, disease prevention, or something else? Does the label matter?
The expected IQ gain from picking the top embryo is about 2 points. Does that change your answer?
If you would not pay $50,000 for this, but it became free tomorrow, would you take it then?



The scenario is not hypothetical. US fertility clinics began offering whole-embryo polygenic scoring around 2020. The first business model is in slides 35–38. The point of the opener is to surface “what counts as treatment?” before naming it.










Note



The thought question turns on a distinction we will spend the lecture testing.

Treatment restores normal function — an antibiotic for an infection, insulin for diabetes.
Prevention keeps you out of harm — a vaccine, a statin.
Enhancement improves you beyond the normal range — sharper memory, taller body, longer life.

The line between these sounds clear. It is not. Most of today is about why the line keeps collapsing — and what is left to argue about once it does.






By the end of Part B, you will be able to:

State three accounts of the treatment-enhancement distinction and explain why each one keeps collapsing.
Tell somatic from germline editing, and explain why this does most of the moral work in the gene-editing debate.
Describe what Casgevy (FDA 2023) does, and why somatic gene therapy is much less contested than germline editing.
Compare three positions on enhancement: Savulescu’s procreative beneficence, Sandel’s giftedness argument, and Habermas on making vs. growing.
Explain the non-identity problem and apply it to a real case.
Describe what polygenic embryo selection is doing in US fertility clinics today, and what it can and cannot deliver.
