apology



F->G






 The Tuskegee study ran for forty years. No internal review stopped it; a single whistleblower and the press did.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
6.4 Why It Lasted

The subjects were poor, Black, and politically powerless, and racism made their suffering easy for officials to discount.
The study was bureaucratically routine — renewed and re-funded for forty years without anyone with power asking whether it should continue.
Professional deference meant the doubts that were raised internally went nowhere.
There was no external body with the authority to stop it, and the internal warnings that did come were dismissed for years.
The damage to public trust — especially in Black communities — was profound, and self-policing was finished: the 1974 National Research Act and the Belmont Report followed directly.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
section: 6.5 The Whistleblower Nobody Wanted to Hear
text:
6.5 The Whistleblower Nobody Wanted to Hear

Peter Buxtun, a Public Health Service venereal-disease interviewer, learned of the study soon after joining in 1965.
He filed formal ethical objections with the CDC in 1966, and pressed them again in 1968.
Rather than stop the study, a CDC blue-ribbon panel reviewed it in 1969 and decided it should continue.
Buxtun was junior and powerless, and the institution simply outlasted his complaints for years.
Only in 1972, after being rebuffed repeatedly, did he give the documents to journalist Jean Heller, whose Associated Press story finally forced the study to end.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
6.6 Thought Question

What was the deepest wrong in Tuskegee?

The deception?
The withholding of treatment?
The racist choice of subjects?
The 40 years of institutional indifference?

You can argue for more than one answer, but which wrong seems most central?

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
6.7 Make It Contemporary


In 1997 the U.S. government formally apologized for Tuskegee — 25 years after it ended.
The justice worry has partly flipped: women and minority groups have often been left out of trials, so treatments are less well tested on them.

Is excluding a group from research a justice failure of the same family as Tuskegee, or a different kind of wrong?

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
6.8 After You’ve Discussed






Note



Two payoffs worth the time:

Map each wrong to a principle. Deception → respect for persons; withheld penicillin → beneficence; choosing poor Black men → justice. The 40 years of silence maps to none of them cleanly — codes do not legislate moral courage.
Justice runs both ways. Tuskegee was wrongful inclusion under exploitation. Modern ethics also worries about wrongful exclusion — fair selection means neither dumping risk on the vulnerable nor denying them benefit.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
7.1 The Birth of “Bioethics”

Van Rensselaer Potter coined “bioethics” in 1970, calling it a “bridge to the future” between scientific knowledge and human values (Potter 1970).
The field grew quickly because medicine now faced public, not merely professional, moral questions.
New institutions gave it a home: the Hastings Center (1969) and Georgetown’s Kennedy Institute of Ethics (1971).
High-profile cases — Tuskegee, and soon Karen Ann Quinlan — gave the field urgency and an audience.
Medicine needed a shared public moral language, not just internal etiquette among physicians.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
section: 7.2 From Scandal to Statute: The 1974 Act
text:
7.2 From Scandal to Statute: The 1974 Act

The exposure of Tuskegee in 1972 created political pressure Congress could not ignore.
The National Research Act (1974) created the National Commission for the Protection of Human Subjects.
It required IRB review for federally funded human research for the first time.
It also charged the Commission with naming the ethical principles that should govern all such research.
The goal was a durable framework, not merely punishment for past abuse.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
7.3 The Belmont Report (1979)

The Belmont Report was written by that Commission and drafted at a 1976 retreat at the Smithsonian’s Belmont Conference Center (National Commission for the Protection of Human Subjects of Biomedical and Behavioral Research 1979).
It is deliberately brief — a framework, not a checklist and not a criminal code.
It names three principles: respect for persons, beneficence, and justice.
Its decisive move is pairing each principle with a concrete protection rather than leaving it abstract.
Those pairings became the backbone of all U.S. human-research regulation (the “Common Rule”).

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
7.4 Belmont’s Three Principles









Principle
Plain meaning
The protection it became
The scandal that exposed the need




Respect for persons
People are agents; protect those with reduced autonomy
Informed consent
Willowbrook & Tuskegee deception


Beneficence
Secure welfare; minimize harm; justify every risk
Risk–benefit assessment
Nazi & Willowbrook harm


Justice
Burdens and benefits must be distributed fairly
Fair subject selection
Tuskegee’s targeting of the poor and Black

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
7.5 Three Foundational Documents Compared









Document
Origin
Main emphasis
Best one-line summary




Nuremberg Code
War-crimes tribunal
Voluntary consent
Persons are not raw material


Helsinki
Medical profession
Research governance by physicians
Research needs disciplined review


Belmont
U.S. national commission
Respect, beneficence, justice
Turn principles into oversight

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
7.6 Bioethics Timeline







timeline

 A slide-sized timeline of the key milestones in this lecture. The sequence matters: atrocity produces a code, ordinary abuses expose deeper problems, and oversight emerges only after repeated failure.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
8.1 Two Kinds of Oversight

An IRB (Institutional Review Board) reviews proposed human-subjects research before it begins, asking whether risks are minimized and justified, subjects are selected fairly, and consent is adequate.
A clinical ethics committee works during care, advising on active bedside conflicts — capacity, surrogates, end-of-life decisions, and team disagreement.
Both are deliberately multidisciplinary, because the lesson of this lecture is that one person’s conscience is an unreliable safeguard.
Hospital ethics committees spread after the 1976 Karen Ann Quinlan case; the 1962 Seattle “God committee,” which rationed dialysis, was an earlier and troubling forerunner.
The common thread is structural review: not “trust the good doctor” but “make the reasoning answerable to others.”

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
8.2 How an IRB Thinks







irb


A

Protocol
submitted



B

Scientific
value?



A->B





C

Risks
minimized?



B->C


yes

fair?

strong enough?

 An IRB turns Belmont-style principles into review questions. Each gate reflects a lesson learned from earlier research abuses.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
section: 8.3 Why This Still Matters at the Bedside
text:
8.3 Why This Still Matters at the Bedside

The reforms that began in research ethics reshaped ordinary clinical ethics too.
Patients came to be seen as agents who decide, not passive recipients who are managed.
Consent, the right to refuse, truth-telling, and confidentiality moved from the margins to the center of care.
The governing question changed from “What should the doctor do?” to “How should clinicians decide with patients?”
That question is exactly where Part B begins.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
8.4 The Rulebook You Just Built

Together, these are why research today requires documented consent and independent review.







Scandal
What was missing
What it became




Carrie Buck (1927)
Your body is yours
The body-autonomy idea the law had skipped


Nuremberg (1947)
The subject’s free “yes”
Voluntary consent


Willowbrook & Beecher (1956–66)
An outside check
Independent review (IRBs)


Tuskegee (1932–72)
Honesty, treatment, fair selection
Belmont: respect, beneficence, justice

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
8.5 Wrapping Up Part A

Medical ethics moved from professional paternalism toward explicit respect for persons because abuse made the older model impossible to defend. Nuremberg named consent. Willowbrook and Beecher showed abuse can look respectable. Tuskegee exposed racism, deception, and 40 years of institutional failure. Belmont turned these lessons into a framework that still governs research and echoes through bedside care.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
8.6 Review Questions
Write a brief answer to each, or use them as discussion prompts.


Recall
How did Nuremberg, Willowbrook and Beecher, and Tuskegee each add a different safeguard to the modern research-ethics rulebook?


Apply
A hospital wants its IRB for a new drug study to be made up entirely of physicians from the sponsoring department because “they understand the science best.” Using the history in this lecture, explain why self-policing is not enough and what safeguards an adequate review process needs.


Debate
Do health systems and governments owe material repair for past medical abuses such as forced sterilization and Tuskegee, or are apology, memorialization, and better oversight enough?

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
8.7 Key Terms

Paternalism — overriding a person’s choices for what is judged to be their own good.
Eugenics — the early-20th-century project of “improving” the population by controlling who reproduces; underwrote forced sterilization laws.
Nuremberg Code (1947) — the ten-point statement issued after the Nazi doctors’ trial; first to require voluntary consent of the human subject.
Declaration of Helsinki — the World Medical Association’s research-ethics statement (1964, repeatedly revised), addressing consent, risk, and obligations to subjects.
Willowbrook hepatitis study — research at a NY institution for children with intellectual disabilities, criticized for coerced consent and deliberate infection.
Beecher’s exposé (1966) — Henry Beecher’s NEJM article cataloguing 22 unethical studies in mainstream U.S. medicine.
Tuskegee Study (1932–1972) — USPHS study of untreated syphilis in Black men in Alabama; subjects deceived and denied penicillin after it became standard.
Belmont Report (1979) — National Commission report naming the three principles of research ethics: respect for persons, beneficence, justice.
IRB (Institutional Review Board) — the committee that reviews and approves human-subjects research at an institution.
Bioethics — the field that emerged in the 1970s in response to these scandals and to new biomedical capacities.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
8.8 References


Beecher, Henry K. 1966. “Ethics and Clinical Research.” New England Journal of Medicine 274 (24): 1354–60. https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJM196606162742405.


Jonsen, Albert R. 1998. The Birth of Bioethics. Oxford University Press.


National Commission for the Protection of Human Subjects of Biomedical and Behavioral Research. 1979. The Belmont Report: Ethical Principles and Guidelines for the Protection of Human Subjects of Research. U.S. Department of Health, Education, and Welfare. https://www.hhs.gov/ohrp/regulations-and-policy/belmont-report/index.html.


Nuremberg Military Tribunals. 1947. The Nuremberg Code. Trials of War Criminals before the Nuremberg Military Tribunals under Control Council Law No. 10.


Potter, Van Rensselaer. 1970. “Bioethics, the Science of Survival.” Perspectives in Biology and Medicine 14 (1): 127–53.


Supreme Court of the United States. 1927. Buck v. Bell, 274 U.S. 200 (1927). U.S. Supreme Court decision.


U.S. National Library of Medicine. 2002. Greek Medicine: The Hippocratic Oath. History of Medicine Division. https://www.nlm.nih.gov/hmd/greek/greek_oath.html.


World Medical Association. 1964. “Declaration of Helsinki: Ethical Principles for Medical Research Involving Human Subjects.” JAMA.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: A Trial Offer at the Worst Time
text:
A Trial Offer at the Worst Time

You have metastatic cancer. Your oncologist says:

“The standard drug has stopped working.”
“There is a study you could join. I need to be clear: it is a randomized trial.”
“One group gets the current standard treatment. The other gets the new drug.”
“I do not get to choose your group, and neither do you.”
“I hope it helps you. But the study is designed to answer a question, not to tailor a plan just for you.”


Does this sound like care, research, or both?
What would you still want to know before saying yes?
If your doctor is the one recruiting you, what is most likely to confuse you?

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
After You’ve Discussed






Note


Three starting points matter here:

Research can happen inside ordinary care without becoming ordinary care. Same clinic, different goal.
Randomization is the clue. Once assignment is by chance, the clinician is no longer choosing only on what seems best for this patient.
Hope is not the same as a promise. Ethical recruitment does not kill hope. It also cannot quietly sell the study as personal treatment.





The opening move should make students feel how easily research can sound like an ordinary treatment recommendation. The point is not to make them anti-research. It is to make them notice the difference in purpose.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Today

By the end of Part A, you will be able to:

Tell clinical care apart from clinical research, and say why the difference changes consent and oversight
Explain randomization, control, placebo, and blinding by walking through the 1954 polio trial
Tell a clinical endpoint from a surrogate, and judge whether a result could be chance
Summarize what trial phases I–IV are for
Explain the therapeutic misconception and why it matters at the bedside
Use the opioid-evidence story to say what a given study can and cannot support

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
One Crucial Difference

Clinical care aims to help the patient in front of you
Clinical research aims to produce knowledge that applies to many patients (Emanuel et al. 2000)
Both can help a patient
But direct patient benefit is not what makes an activity research
That difference changes consent, oversight, and what risks are acceptable

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: The Same Room Can Hide the Difference
text:
The Same Room Can Hide the Difference


Why it feels like care

Same hospital
Same white coats
Same lab draws and imaging
Same reassuring language of “options”
Often the same physician


Why it is not the same

The protocol fixes what can happen
A coin flip may assign the arm
The main goal is learning, not tailoring
The results are meant to guide future care
Independent review must happen before it begins

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Why Research Exists

Without research, medicine would freeze in place
New drugs, devices, and procedures must be tested before they become standard
Even old treatments need comparison, follow-up, and correction
Ethical research is not the enemy of patient care
Ethical research is one reason patient care can improve at all

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: Research vs. Clinical Care at a Glance
text:
Research vs. Clinical Care at a Glance








Question
Clinical care
Clinical research




Main goal
Help this patient now
Produce reliable knowledge


Who should benefit first?
This patient
Future patients, and sometimes current ones


Who sets the plan?
Clinician and patient
The protocol, eligibility rules, and study design


What governs it?
Professional standards, licensure, ordinary consent
IRB review, protocol rules, research consent (US Department of Health and Human Services 2018)


What counts as success?
Good care for this person
Valid evidence that answers the research question

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
The Physician-Investigator Tension

A clinician may also be a physician-investigator
That can be efficient and helpful
It can also blur whether the advice is coming from the doctor or the researcher
Modern research ethics treats this tension as real and common, not rare (World Medical Association 2024)
That is one reason independent review matters

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Why Randomize?

If doctors picked the groups, healthier and sicker patients could cluster without anyone meaning to (National Cancer Institute 2024)
Randomization assigns people by chance
That keeps the comparison scientifically fair
It also means the clinician is no longer hand-picking a treatment for this patient
So randomization is ethically important, not just useful in statistics

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: When Is It OK to Randomize a Sick Person?
text:
When Is It OK to Randomize a Sick Person?

The honest answer needs a concept: clinical equipoise
It means the expert community is genuinely unsure which arm is better
If we already knew, randomizing would be wrong — we’d be sending some patients to the worse option on purpose
Equipoise is the moral license for a trial
This is why “I’d just give everyone the new drug” misses the point: we do not yet know it is better

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: One Map of a Parallel-Group Trial
text:
One Map of a Parallel-Group Trial







rct


A

People with the
condition



B

Eligibility check
and consent



A->B





C

Randomize



B->C





D

Arm 1
new treatment



C->D





E

Arm 2
standard care or
control



C->E





F

Follow-up
and outcome data



D->F





E->F





G

F->G






 A plain-language map of a parallel-group randomized trial. The key ethical moment is the middle: once people consent and are randomized, no one chooses the arm just for that patient’s benefit.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
A Real Trial: Polio, 1954

In 1952 the United States recorded about 58,000 polio cases, most of them children. It was the most feared childhood disease in the country.
Jonas Salk’s killed-virus injected vaccine looked promising. But “promising” vaccines had failed before. Some had even harmed children.
So the country ran the largest medical experiment in history. About 1.8 million schoolchildren — the “Polio Pioneers” — took part. The March of Dimes organized it. Thomas Francis Jr. led the science.
The trial’s job was not to deliver a cure. It was to prove whether there was one.
Every part from the last few slides — randomize, control, blind, compare — was used on these children. The design choices decided whether anyone could believe the result (Francis et al. 1955).

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Two Designs, One Trial
The 1954 trial actually ran two designs at once — and that is the lesson:









Placebo-controlled areas
Observed-control areas




Who got vaccine
Grades 1–3 randomized to vaccine or saline
Only 2nd-grade volunteers vaccinated


The controls
Randomized peers given a saline shot
Grades 1 and 3, simply observed


Blinding
Double-blind — no one knew who got what
Not blinded


How trustworthy
High — groups comparable, judgment unbiased
Weaker — volunteers differ; diagnosis can be swayed



Same disease, same year, same vaccine. The design, not the vaccine, is what made one half of the trial believable.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
What the Control Group Gets

Sometimes the control group gets current standard care
Sometimes it gets standard care plus a placebo
Sometimes, if no proven treatment exists, it may get placebo or no active treatment (National Cancer Institute 2024; World Medical Association 2024)
Control does not mean “ignored”
Everyone in the study is still watched under a protocol

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Placebo Does Not Always Mean “Nothing”

A placebo can do different jobs.

If no proven treatment exists, placebo may stand in for no active treatment.
If a proven treatment already exists, placebo is often added on top of standard care so no one is denied basic treatment.
The ethical question is not just “placebo or no placebo?” It is what participants are missing by being in that arm (National Cancer Institute 2024; World Medical Association 2024).

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
What Blinding Actually Does

Blinding means some people in the trial do not know which arm a participant is in
Single-blind usually means the participant does not know
Double-blind usually means the participant and the treating team do not know
The point is to reduce expectation effects and biased judgment
“Double-blind” is about cleaner evidence, not secrecy for its own sake

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Why Blinding Mattered in 1954

Diagnosing mild polio is partly a judgment call. A borderline weak limb can be read either way. In the 1954 trial’s observed-control areas, the examining doctors knew which children had been vaccinated. A doctor who hoped the vaccine worked could, without any dishonesty, lean toward “not polio” in a vaccinated child.
That is exactly why the placebo-controlled areas were double-blind. No one at the bedside knew. Hope could not tip the count.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: Who Watches the Trial While It Runs?
text:
Who Watches the Trial While It Runs?

A trial is not left alone until it ends
An independent data safety monitoring board (DSMB) reads the data as it comes in
It can stop a trial early for harm — one arm is clearly hurting people
It can also stop early for benefit — one arm is so clearly better that continuing the other is no longer fair
Equipoise has a deadline. Once the evidence is in, the trial must end

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
The Same Machinery, Today

The 2020 COVID-19 vaccine trials used the 1954 logic at modern scale. Tens of thousands of volunteers. Randomized. Double-blind. Placebo-controlled.
The primary endpoint was a clinical one — lab-confirmed symptomatic COVID-19. Not just an antibody level in the blood.
An independent monitoring board read the data at planned checkpoints.
When the effect was large and the safety data was good enough, the FDA granted Emergency Use Authorization. Full approval came later, after more follow-up.
Seventy years apart, the polio and COVID trials are the same instrument. Both used it carefully because the stakes were enormous.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: What Are We Even Measuring? Endpoints
text:
What Are We Even Measuring? Endpoints

A clinical endpoint is something a patient can feel or would obviously care about: living longer, avoiding a stroke, walking without pain.
A surrogate endpoint is a stand-in. It is measured because it is faster or cheaper — a lab number, a scan finding, a blood pressure reading.
Surrogates speed research up. But a treatment can move the surrogate and still not help patients. It can even harm them.
The classic warning is the CAST trial. Drugs that “fixed” an abnormal heart rhythm on the monitor actually increased deaths.
So the first question to ask of any trial is: did patients actually do better, or did a number just move?

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Could It Just Be Chance?

Any two groups of people differ a little by luck, even if the treatment does nothing.
Statistics ask one question: how likely is a difference this big if the treatment really had no effect?
“Statistically significant” roughly means “probably not just luck.” It does not mean large, important, or certain.
Small trials are easily fooled by chance. That is why large samples and independent replication matter so much.
A single significant result is a lead worth following. It is not a verdict to act on alone.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Honest Trials Can Still Mislead

A result can mislead even when no one lied or faked anything.
Who got into the trial limits who the answer applies to. Narrow eligibility rules can leave out the very patients you treat.
People who drop out are usually different from those who finish. So looking only at the “completers” quietly flatters the treatment.
Intention-to-treat analysis is the standard guard against that. It counts everyone in the group they were randomized to, even if they quit.
The disciplined question is always: who is missing from this result, and which way would that bias it?

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
When a Letter Becomes “Evidence”

In 1980, a five-sentence letter to the editor reported that, among hospitalized patients given opioids, addiction was rare (Porter and Jick 1980).
It was not a trial. No randomization. No control. No long-term or outpatient follow-up.
Over the next two decades it was cited hundreds of times — as if it had proven that long-term outpatient opioids rarely cause addiction.
The lesson is exact. The type of study and who was in it cap what any result can support.
How that misreading was promoted is a research-integrity story we return to later. Here the point is simple: a letter is not an RCT.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Thought Question

A new drug’s Phase III trial is reported as “positive.”

It significantly improved a surrogate marker on a scan.
The trial was small. About 25% of participants dropped out.
The analysis included only those who completed.


Name the three weaknesses you can already see.
What would you want to know before telling a patient this drug “works”?
Is “the FDA might approve it” the same as “it helps patients”?

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Make It Contemporary

Regulators sometimes grant accelerated approval based on a surrogate endpoint. A confirming trial is promised later.

Patients with no other option get the drug years sooner.
Some confirming trials later show no real benefit. A few are never finished.

Is faster access worth weaker evidence? Who should decide? And what should happen if the confirming trial fails?

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
After You’ve Discussed






Note


Three distinctions to carry forward:

Statistically significant ≠ clinically meaningful. A real but tiny effect on a surrogate can be true and still not worth having.
Regulatory approval ≠ proven patient benefit. Approval is a decision under uncertainty, not a guarantee.
The standing questions are always the same: what was measured, in whom, and who is missing from the result?

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Four Phases, Four Questions








Phase
The main question
Typical scale




I
Is it safe? What dose can people tolerate?
Small


II
Does it seem to work?
Larger


III
Is it better than the current standard?
Large, often randomized


IV
What happens after approval in the real world?
Very large, longer-term

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
One Simple Phase Map







phases


A

Phase I
small group
safety and dose



B

Phase II
more participants
early evidence of effect



A->B





C

Phase III
large comparison
against standard care



B->C





D

Phase IV
after approval
long-term follow-up



C->D






 A plain-language phase map. The main ethical point is that ‘clinical trial’ covers activities with very different goals, risks, and likely benefits.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: What Each Phase Is Really Asking
text:
What Each Phase Is Really Asking

Phase I is small. Often 15–30 people in cancer. It asks what dose is tolerable and what harms appear. Direct benefit is not the point. That is what makes it ethically hard (National Cancer Institute 2024).
Phase II enrolls more people. It asks whether the treatment seems to work. Safety is still watched closely. Many promising drugs fail right here.
Phase III is the large, usually randomized comparison against the best existing option. This is where placebo and control debates bite hardest. Its result becomes the new standard.
Phase IV runs after approval. It catches rare harms and real-world performance that tightly controlled trials miss.
“Clinical trial” is therefore not one thing. The same words cover wildly different risks, hopes, and likely benefits.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Why Phases Matter Ethically

Students often hear “clinical trial” and imagine one thing
But Phase I and Phase IV do not ask the same question or offer the same likely benefits
Consent should match the phase, not rely on a generic script
Risks, hopes, and expectations shift from phase to phase
So the ethical explanation has to be specific

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
The Therapeutic Misconception

Therapeutic misconception happens when participants think the study is being chosen mainly for their personal medical benefit (Appelbaum et al. 1982)
The mistake is not just “I hope this helps”
It is more like “my doctor is picking this because it is best for me”
That is often false once protocol rules and randomization control the options
A person can sign a form and still misunderstand the basic situation

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Why It Happens So Easily

Recruitment often comes from a trusted clinician
Serious illness makes hope urgent
Research visits look like treatment visits
Words like “option” and “opportunity” blur the categories
Many people hear “new” and assume “better”

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: A Research Consent Is Not a Treatment Consent
text:
A Research Consent Is Not a Treatment Consent

Valid consent has four elements. Research adds requirements on top of them.
It must say, in plain words, “this is research” — not treatment chosen for you
It must disclose randomization. A chance process, not your clinician, may pick your arm
It must state the goal is knowledge. Direct benefit is uncertain or unlikely
It must make withdrawal at any time, without penalty, explicit
The nurse is often the one who notices a form was signed but not understood

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
The Dangerous Sentence


“This study may help you” can be true. “This study is being chosen because it is the best treatment for you” is often false.


The distinction is what the therapeutic misconception tries to protect (Appelbaum et al. 1982; Emanuel et al. 2000).

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: Early-Phase Trials and Thin Personal Benefit
text:
Early-Phase Trials and Thin Personal Benefit

Healthy volunteers and desperate patients join studies for very different reasons
In Phase I, researchers mainly ask what dose is tolerable and what harms appear
Some participants may benefit. Many will not.
Payment, hope, and lack of alternatives can all cloud judgment
Ethical recruitment has to say this plainly

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Thought Question

You are a research nurse enrolling patients in a first-in-human cancer trial.

A patient says, “If you are offering this, you must think it gives me the best chance.”
The oncologist who is also the principal investigator is standing beside you.


What is the most important misunderstanding to correct?
How blunt should you be about the low chance of direct benefit?
If the patient says, “I know the odds are low, but I want to try anyway,” has the misconception been fixed?

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Make It Contemporary

The same structure appears outside cancer too:

A family enrolls a child in a gene-therapy study because no approved treatment exists.
A healthy adult joins a Phase I trial mainly because the payment covers two months of rent.

Both may be voluntary. But are both equally informed and equally free?

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
After You’ve Discussed






Note


Three payoffs to insist on:

Hope is allowed. The goal is not to turn participants cynical. It is to keep hope from masquerading as evidence.
Misunderstanding is not the same as irrationality. A person may fully understand that a study is not personal care and still choose it.
The hardest part is relational. When a trusted clinician recruits for a study, the burden to separate care from research falls more heavily on the professional, not less (World Medical Association 2024).

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
The Main Distinction

Trials are ethically hard because they borrow the setting of care but not its single-minded goal
Randomization, blinding, control groups, and phases are scientific tools
The same tools let you read a result: what was measured, could it be chance, who is missing
Those tools are exactly why research needs its own ethics
Part B turns from the structure of trials to the rulebook meant to protect the people inside them

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Wrapping Up Part A

Clinical research is not ordinary care with extra paperwork. Its purpose is to produce reliable knowledge. That is why it uses randomization, control groups, blinding, and staged phases. You can see all of these in one real instrument: the 1954 polio trial. The same tools showed up again in the COVID-19 vaccine trials.
Those tools also teach you to read a study. What did it measure — a clinical endpoint or a surrogate? Could the result be chance? Who dropped out or was left out?
The key task in Part A is learning to say, clearly and without panic: this is research, not just treatment by another name — and here is how good its evidence actually is.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Review Questions
Write a brief answer to each, or use them as discussion prompts.


Recall
What is clinical equipoise, and why is it the standard ethical condition for randomizing patients between two arms of a trial?


Apply
A community oncologist enrolls her own patients in an industry-sponsored Phase III trial of a new chemo drug. She continues to manage their routine care. Identify two specific ways her dual role as physician-investigator could distort either the science or the patients’ decision to enroll. Propose one structural safeguard for each.


Debate
When a Phase III trial shows a clear survival benefit at an interim look, the data safety monitoring board may stop the trial early and offer the better arm to everyone. Critics argue this often happens too soon — before long-term harms are visible — and that finishing the trial would better serve future patients. When should “stop early for benefit” win, and when should “let the science finish” win?

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Key Terms

Clinical care — what a clinician does to benefit this patient.
Clinical research — what an investigator does to produce knowledge about a class of patients.
Physician-investigator — a clinician who is both at once; the source of the structural conflict at the heart of research ethics.
Clinical equipoise — real uncertainty in the expert community about which trial arm is better; the standard reason for randomizing.
Randomization — assigning subjects to study arms by chance, to keep known and unknown differences balanced.
Blinding — hiding the arm assignment from subjects (single-blind) and/or from clinicians and assessors (double-blind), to prevent expectation effects.
Phases I–IV — first-in-human safety (I), small efficacy and dose-finding (II), pivotal efficacy and safety (III), post-marketing surveillance (IV).
Clinical vs. surrogate endpoint — outcomes patients feel (death, MI, stroke) versus measurable stand-ins (LDL, tumor shrinkage) that may or may not track them.
Data safety monitoring board (DSMB) — an independent panel that reads unblinded interim data and may stop a trial for harm, benefit, or futility.
Therapeutic misconception — the patient’s mistaken belief that decisions in a trial are being made for their personal benefit rather than to answer a research question.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
References


Appelbaum, Paul S., Loren H. Roth, and Charles Lidz. 1982. “The Therapeutic Misconception: Informed Consent in Psychiatric Research.” International Journal of Law and Psychiatry 5 (3-4): 319–29. https://doi.org/10.1016/0160-2527(82)90026-7.


Emanuel, Ezekiel J., David Wendler, and Christine Grady. 2000. “What Makes Clinical Research Ethical?” JAMA 283 (20): 2701–11. https://doi.org/10.1001/jama.283.20.2701.


Francis, Thomas et al. 1955. “An Evaluation of the 1954 Poliomyelitis Vaccine Trials: Summary Report.” American Journal of Public Health 45 (5): 1–63.


National Cancer Institute. 2024. How Do Clinical Trials Work? National Cancer Institute. https://www.cancer.gov/research/participate/clinical-trials/how-trials-work.


Porter, Jane, and Hershel Jick. 1980. “Addiction Rare in Patients Treated with Narcotics.” New England Journal of Medicine 302 (2): 123. https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJM198001103020221.


US Department of Health and Human Services. 2018. Federal Policy for the Protection of Human Subjects (“the Common Rule”). Code of Federal Regulations, Title 45, Part 46. https://www.hhs.gov/ohrp/regulations-and-policy/regulations/common-rule/index.html.


World Medical Association. 2024. WMA Declaration of Helsinki – Ethical Principles for Medical Research Involving Human Participants. Adopted by the 75th WMA General Assembly, Helsinki, Finland. https://www.wma.net/policies-post/wma-declaration-of-helsinki/.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
You have metastatic cancer. Your oncologist says:

“The standard drug has stopped working.”
“There is a study you could join. I need to be clear: it is a randomized trial.”
“One group gets the current standard treatment. The other gets the new drug.”
“I do not get to choose your group, and neither do you.”
“I hope it helps you. But the study is designed to answer a question, not to tailor a plan just for you.”


Does this sound like care, research, or both?
What would you still want to know before saying yes?
If your doctor is the one recruiting you, what is most likely to confuse you?











Note



Three starting points matter here:

Research can happen inside ordinary care without becoming ordinary care. Same clinic, different goal.
Randomization is the clue. Once assignment is by chance, the clinician is no longer choosing only on what seems best for this patient.
Hope is not the same as a promise. Ethical recruitment does not kill hope. It also cannot quietly sell the study as personal treatment.




The opening move should make students feel how easily research can sound like an ordinary treatment recommendation. The point is not to make them anti-research. It is to make them notice the difference in purpose.





By the end of Part A, you will be able to:

Tell clinical care apart from clinical research, and say why the difference changes consent and oversight
Explain randomization, control, placebo, and blinding by walking through the 1954 polio trial
Tell a clinical endpoint from a surrogate, and judge whether a result could be chance
Summarize what trial phases I–IV are for
Explain the therapeutic misconception and why it matters at the bedside
Use the opioid-evidence story to say what a given study can and cannot support

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
You have metastatic cancer. Your oncologist says:

“The standard drug has stopped working.”
“There is a study you could join. I need to be clear: it is a randomized trial.”
“One group gets the current standard treatment. The other gets the new drug.”
“I do not get to choose your group, and neither do you.”
“I hope it helps you. But the study is designed to answer a question, not to tailor a plan just for you.”


Does this sound like care, research, or both?
What would you still want to know before saying yes?
If your doctor is the one recruiting you, what is most likely to confuse you?

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Note



Three starting points matter here:

Research can happen inside ordinary care without becoming ordinary care. Same clinic, different goal.
Randomization is the clue. Once assignment is by chance, the clinician is no longer choosing only on what seems best for this patient.
Hope is not the same as a promise. Ethical recruitment does not kill hope. It also cannot quietly sell the study as personal treatment.




The opening move should make students feel how easily research can sound like an ordinary treatment recommendation. The point is not to make them anti-research. It is to make them notice the difference in purpose.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
By the end of Part A, you will be able to:

Tell clinical care apart from clinical research, and say why the difference changes consent and oversight
Explain randomization, control, placebo, and blinding by walking through the 1954 polio trial
Tell a clinical endpoint from a surrogate, and judge whether a result could be chance
Summarize what trial phases I–IV are for
Explain the therapeutic misconception and why it matters at the bedside
Use the opioid-evidence story to say what a given study can and cannot support

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
2.1 One Crucial Difference

Clinical care aims to help the patient in front of you
Clinical research aims to produce knowledge that applies to many patients (Emanuel et al. 2000)
Both can help a patient
But direct patient benefit is not what makes an activity research
That difference changes consent, oversight, and what risks are acceptable

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: 2.2 The Same Room Can Hide the Difference
text:
2.2 The Same Room Can Hide the Difference


Why it feels like care

Same hospital
Same white coats
Same lab draws and imaging
Same reassuring language of “options”
Often the same physician


Why it is not the same

The protocol fixes what can happen
A coin flip may assign the arm
The main goal is learning, not tailoring
The results are meant to guide future care
Independent review must happen before it begins

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
2.3 Why Research Exists

Without research, medicine would freeze in place
New drugs, devices, and procedures must be tested before they become standard
Even old treatments need comparison, follow-up, and correction
Ethical research is not the enemy of patient care
Ethical research is one reason patient care can improve at all

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: 2.4 Research vs. Clinical Care at a Glance
text:
2.4 Research vs. Clinical Care at a Glance








Question
Clinical care
Clinical research




Main goal
Help this patient now
Produce reliable knowledge


Who should benefit first?
This patient
Future patients, and sometimes current ones


Who sets the plan?
Clinician and patient
The protocol, eligibility rules, and study design


What governs it?
Professional standards, licensure, ordinary consent
IRB review, protocol rules, research consent (US Department of Health and Human Services 2018)


What counts as success?
Good care for this person
Valid evidence that answers the research question

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
2.5 The Physician-Investigator Tension

A clinician may also be a physician-investigator
That can be efficient and helpful
It can also blur whether the advice is coming from the doctor or the researcher
Modern research ethics treats this tension as real and common, not rare (World Medical Association 2024)
That is one reason independent review matters

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
3.1 Why Randomize?

If doctors picked the groups, healthier and sicker patients could cluster without anyone meaning to (National Cancer Institute 2024)
Randomization assigns people by chance
That keeps the comparison scientifically fair
It also means the clinician is no longer hand-picking a treatment for this patient
So randomization is ethically important, not just useful in statistics

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: 3.2 When Is It OK to Randomize a Sick Person?
text:
3.2 When Is It OK to Randomize a Sick Person?

The honest answer needs a concept: clinical equipoise
It means the expert community is genuinely unsure which arm is better
If we already knew, randomizing would be wrong — we’d be sending some patients to the worse option on purpose
Equipoise is the moral license for a trial
This is why “I’d just give everyone the new drug” misses the point: we do not yet know it is better

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: 3.3 One Map of a Parallel-Group Trial
text:
3.3 One Map of a Parallel-Group Trial







rct


A

People with the
condition



B

Eligibility check
and consent



A->B





C

Randomize



B->C





D

Arm 1
new treatment



C->D





E

Arm 2
standard care or
control



C->E





F

Follow-up
and outcome data



D->F





E->F





G

Compare results



F->G






 A plain-language map of a parallel-group randomized trial. The key ethical moment is the middle: once people consent and are randomized, no one chooses the arm just for that patient’s benefit.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
3.4 A Real Trial: Polio, 1954

In 1952 the United States recorded about 58,000 polio cases, most of them children. It was the most feared childhood disease in the country.
Jonas Salk’s killed-virus injected vaccine looked promising. But “promising” vaccines had failed before. Some had even harmed children.
So the country ran the largest medical experiment in history. About 1.8 million schoolchildren — the “Polio Pioneers” — took part. The March of Dimes organized it. Thomas Francis Jr. led the science.
The trial’s job was not to deliver a cure. It was to prove whether there was one.
Every part from the last few slides — randomize, control, blind, compare — was used on these children. The design choices decided whether anyone could believe the result (Francis et al. 1955).

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
3.5 Two Designs, One Trial
The 1954 trial actually ran two designs at once — and that is the lesson:









Placebo-controlled areas
Observed-control areas




Who got vaccine
Grades 1–3 randomized to vaccine or saline
Only 2nd-grade volunteers vaccinated


The controls
Randomized peers given a saline shot
Grades 1 and 3, simply observed


Blinding
Double-blind — no one knew who got what
Not blinded


How trustworthy
High — groups comparable, judgment unbiased
Weaker — volunteers differ; diagnosis can be swayed



Same disease, same year, same vaccine. The design, not the vaccine, is what made one half of the trial believable.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
3.6 What the Control Group Gets

Sometimes the control group gets current standard care
Sometimes it gets standard care plus a placebo
Sometimes, if no proven treatment exists, it may get placebo or no active treatment (National Cancer Institute 2024; World Medical Association 2024)
Control does not mean “ignored”
Everyone in the study is still watched under a protocol

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
3.7 Placebo Does Not Always Mean “Nothing”

A placebo can do different jobs.

If no proven treatment exists, placebo may stand in for no active treatment.
If a proven treatment already exists, placebo is often added on top of standard care so no one is denied basic treatment.
The ethical question is not just “placebo or no placebo?” It is what participants are missing by being in that arm (National Cancer Institute 2024; World Medical Association 2024).

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
3.8 What Blinding Actually Does

Blinding means some people in the trial do not know which arm a participant is in
Single-blind usually means the participant does not know
Double-blind usually means the participant and the treating team do not know
The point is to reduce expectation effects and biased judgment
“Double-blind” is about cleaner evidence, not secrecy for its own sake

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
3.9 Why Blinding Mattered in 1954

Diagnosing mild polio is partly a judgment call. A borderline weak limb can be read either way. In the 1954 trial’s observed-control areas, the examining doctors knew which children had been vaccinated. A doctor who hoped the vaccine worked could, without any dishonesty, lean toward “not polio” in a vaccinated child.
That is exactly why the placebo-controlled areas were double-blind. No one at the bedside knew. Hope could not tip the count.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: 3.10 Who Watches the Trial While It Runs?
text:
3.10 Who Watches the Trial While It Runs?

A trial is not left alone until it ends
An independent data safety monitoring board (DSMB) reads the data as it comes in
It can stop a trial early for harm — one arm is clearly hurting people
It can also stop early for benefit — one arm is so clearly better that continuing the other is no longer fair
Equipoise has a deadline. Once the evidence is in, the trial must end

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
3.11 The Same Machinery, Today

The 2020 COVID-19 vaccine trials used the 1954 logic at modern scale. Tens of thousands of volunteers. Randomized. Double-blind. Placebo-controlled.
The primary endpoint was a clinical one — lab-confirmed symptomatic COVID-19. Not just an antibody level in the blood.
An independent monitoring board read the data at planned checkpoints.
When the effect was large and the safety data was good enough, the FDA granted Emergency Use Authorization. Full approval came later, after more follow-up.
Seventy years apart, the polio and COVID trials are the same instrument. Both used it carefully because the stakes were enormous.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: 4.1 What Are We Even Measuring? Endpoints
text:
4.1 What Are We Even Measuring? Endpoints

A clinical endpoint is something a patient can feel or would obviously care about: living longer, avoiding a stroke, walking without pain.
A surrogate endpoint is a stand-in. It is measured because it is faster or cheaper — a lab number, a scan finding, a blood pressure reading.
Surrogates speed research up. But a treatment can move the surrogate and still not help patients. It can even harm them.
The classic warning is the CAST trial. Drugs that “fixed” an abnormal heart rhythm on the monitor actually increased deaths.
So the first question to ask of any trial is: did patients actually do better, or did a number just move?

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
4.2 Could It Just Be Chance?

Any two groups of people differ a little by luck, even if the treatment does nothing.
Statistics ask one question: how likely is a difference this big if the treatment really had no effect?
“Statistically significant” roughly means “probably not just luck.” It does not mean large, important, or certain.
Small trials are easily fooled by chance. That is why large samples and independent replication matter so much.
A single significant result is a lead worth following. It is not a verdict to act on alone.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
4.3 Honest Trials Can Still Mislead

A result can mislead even when no one lied or faked anything.
Who got into the trial limits who the answer applies to. Narrow eligibility rules can leave out the very patients you treat.
People who drop out are usually different from those who finish. So looking only at the “completers” quietly flatters the treatment.
Intention-to-treat analysis is the standard guard against that. It counts everyone in the group they were randomized to, even if they quit.
The disciplined question is always: who is missing from this result, and which way would that bias it?

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
4.4 When a Letter Becomes “Evidence”

In 1980, a five-sentence letter to the editor reported that, among hospitalized patients given opioids, addiction was rare (Porter and Jick 1980).
It was not a trial. No randomization. No control. No long-term or outpatient follow-up.
Over the next two decades it was cited hundreds of times — as if it had proven that long-term outpatient opioids rarely cause addiction.
The lesson is exact. The type of study and who was in it cap what any result can support.
How that misreading was promoted is a research-integrity story we return to later. Here the point is simple: a letter is not an RCT.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
4.5 Thought Question

A new drug’s Phase III trial is reported as “positive.”

It significantly improved a surrogate marker on a scan.
The trial was small. About 25% of participants dropped out.
The analysis included only those who completed.


Name the three weaknesses you can already see.
What would you want to know before telling a patient this drug “works”?
Is “the FDA might approve it” the same as “it helps patients”?

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
4.6 Make It Contemporary

Regulators sometimes grant accelerated approval based on a surrogate endpoint. A confirming trial is promised later.

Patients with no other option get the drug years sooner.
Some confirming trials later show no real benefit. A few are never finished.

Is faster access worth weaker evidence? Who should decide? And what should happen if the confirming trial fails?

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
4.7 After You’ve Discussed






Note



Three distinctions to carry forward:

Statistically significant ≠ clinically meaningful. A real but tiny effect on a surrogate can be true and still not worth having.
Regulatory approval ≠ proven patient benefit. Approval is a decision under uncertainty, not a guarantee.
The standing questions are always the same: what was measured, in whom, and who is missing from the result?

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
5.1 Four Phases, Four Questions








Phase
The main question
Typical scale




I
Is it safe? What dose can people tolerate?
Small


II
Does it seem to work?
Larger


III
Is it better than the current standard?
Large, often randomized


IV
What happens after approval in the real world?
Very large, longer-term

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
5.2 One Simple Phase Map







phases


A

Phase I
small group
safety and dose



B

Phase II
more participants
early evidence of effect



A->B





C

Phase III
large comparison
against standard care



B->C





D

Phase IV
after approval
long-term follow-up



C->D






 A plain-language phase map. The main ethical point is that ‘clinical trial’ covers activities with very different goals, risks, and likely benefits.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: 5.3 What Each Phase Is Really Asking
text:
5.3 What Each Phase Is Really Asking

Phase I is small. Often 15–30 people in cancer. It asks what dose is tolerable and what harms appear. Direct benefit is not the point. That is what makes it ethically hard (National Cancer Institute 2024).
Phase II enrolls more people. It asks whether the treatment seems to work. Safety is still watched closely. Many promising drugs fail right here.
Phase III is the large, usually randomized comparison against the best existing option. This is where placebo and control debates bite hardest. Its result becomes the new standard.
Phase IV runs after approval. It catches rare harms and real-world performance that tightly controlled trials miss.
“Clinical trial” is therefore not one thing. The same words cover wildly different risks, hopes, and likely benefits.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
5.4 Why Phases Matter Ethically

Students often hear “clinical trial” and imagine one thing
But Phase I and Phase IV do not ask the same question or offer the same likely benefits
Consent should match the phase, not rely on a generic script
Risks, hopes, and expectations shift from phase to phase
So the ethical explanation has to be specific

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
6.1 The Therapeutic Misconception

Therapeutic misconception happens when participants think the study is being chosen mainly for their personal medical benefit (Appelbaum et al. 1982)
The mistake is not just “I hope this helps”
It is more like “my doctor is picking this because it is best for me”
That is often false once protocol rules and randomization control the options
A person can sign a form and still misunderstand the basic situation

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
6.2 Why It Happens So Easily

Recruitment often comes from a trusted clinician
Serious illness makes hope urgent
Research visits look like treatment visits
Words like “option” and “opportunity” blur the categories
Many people hear “new” and assume “better”

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: 6.3 A Research Consent Is Not a Treatment Consent
text:
6.3 A Research Consent Is Not a Treatment Consent

Valid consent has four elements. Research adds requirements on top of them.
It must say, in plain words, “this is research” — not treatment chosen for you
It must disclose randomization. A chance process, not your clinician, may pick your arm
It must state the goal is knowledge. Direct benefit is uncertain or unlikely
It must make withdrawal at any time, without penalty, explicit
The nurse is often the one who notices a form was signed but not understood

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
6.4 The Dangerous Sentence


“This study may help you” can be true. “This study is being chosen because it is the best treatment for you” is often false.


The distinction is what the therapeutic misconception tries to protect (Appelbaum et al. 1982; Emanuel et al. 2000).

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: 6.5 Early-Phase Trials and Thin Personal Benefit
text:
6.5 Early-Phase Trials and Thin Personal Benefit

Healthy volunteers and desperate patients join studies for very different reasons
In Phase I, researchers mainly ask what dose is tolerable and what harms appear
Some participants may benefit. Many will not.
Payment, hope, and lack of alternatives can all cloud judgment
Ethical recruitment has to say this plainly

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
6.6 Thought Question

You are a research nurse enrolling patients in a first-in-human cancer trial.

A patient says, “If you are offering this, you must think it gives me the best chance.”
The oncologist who is also the principal investigator is standing beside you.


What is the most important misunderstanding to correct?
How blunt should you be about the low chance of direct benefit?
If the patient says, “I know the odds are low, but I want to try anyway,” has the misconception been fixed?

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
6.7 Make It Contemporary

The same structure appears outside cancer too:

A family enrolls a child in a gene-therapy study because no approved treatment exists.
A healthy adult joins a Phase I trial mainly because the payment covers two months of rent.

Both may be voluntary. But are both equally informed and equally free?

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
6.8 After You’ve Discussed






Note



Three payoffs to insist on:

Hope is allowed. The goal is not to turn participants cynical. It is to keep hope from masquerading as evidence.
Misunderstanding is not the same as irrationality. A person may fully understand that a study is not personal care and still choose it.
The hardest part is relational. When a trusted clinician recruits for a study, the burden to separate care from research falls more heavily on the professional, not less (World Medical Association 2024).

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
7.1 The Main Distinction

Trials are ethically hard because they borrow the setting of care but not its single-minded goal
Randomization, blinding, control groups, and phases are scientific tools
The same tools let you read a result: what was measured, could it be chance, who is missing
Those tools are exactly why research needs its own ethics
Part B turns from the structure of trials to the rulebook meant to protect the people inside them

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
7.2 Wrapping Up Part A

Clinical research is not ordinary care with extra paperwork. Its purpose is to produce reliable knowledge. That is why it uses randomization, control groups, blinding, and staged phases. You can see all of these in one real instrument: the 1954 polio trial. The same tools showed up again in the COVID-19 vaccine trials.
Those tools also teach you to read a study. What did it measure — a clinical endpoint or a surrogate? Could the result be chance? Who dropped out or was left out?
The key task in Part A is learning to say, clearly and without panic: this is research, not just treatment by another name — and here is how good its evidence actually is.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
7.3 Review Questions
Write a brief answer to each, or use them as discussion prompts.


Recall
What is clinical equipoise, and why is it the standard ethical condition for randomizing patients between two arms of a trial?


Apply
A community oncologist enrolls her own patients in an industry-sponsored Phase III trial of a new chemo drug. She continues to manage their routine care. Identify two specific ways her dual role as physician-investigator could distort either the science or the patients’ decision to enroll. Propose one structural safeguard for each.


Debate
When a Phase III trial shows a clear survival benefit at an interim look, the data safety monitoring board may stop the trial early and offer the better arm to everyone. Critics argue this often happens too soon — before long-term harms are visible — and that finishing the trial would better serve future patients. When should “stop early for benefit” win, and when should “let the science finish” win?

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
7.4 Key Terms

Clinical care — what a clinician does to benefit this patient.
Clinical research — what an investigator does to produce knowledge about a class of patients.
Physician-investigator — a clinician who is both at once; the source of the structural conflict at the heart of research ethics.
Clinical equipoise — real uncertainty in the expert community about which trial arm is better; the standard reason for randomizing.
Randomization — assigning subjects to study arms by chance, to keep known and unknown differences balanced.
Blinding — hiding the arm assignment from subjects (single-blind) and/or from clinicians and assessors (double-blind), to prevent expectation effects.
Phases I–IV — first-in-human safety (I), small efficacy and dose-finding (II), pivotal efficacy and safety (III), post-marketing surveillance (IV).
Clinical vs. surrogate endpoint — outcomes patients feel (death, MI, stroke) versus measurable stand-ins (LDL, tumor shrinkage) that may or may not track them.
Data safety monitoring board (DSMB) — an independent panel that reads unblinded interim data and may stop a trial for harm, benefit, or futility.
Therapeutic misconception — the patient’s mistaken belief that decisions in a trial are being made for their personal benefit rather than to answer a research question.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
7.5 References


Appelbaum, Paul S., Loren H. Roth, and Charles Lidz. 1982. “The Therapeutic Misconception: Informed Consent in Psychiatric Research.” International Journal of Law and Psychiatry 5 (3-4): 319–29. https://doi.org/10.1016/0160-2527(82)90026-7.


Emanuel, Ezekiel J., David Wendler, and Christine Grady. 2000. “What Makes Clinical Research Ethical?” JAMA 283 (20): 2701–11. https://doi.org/10.1001/jama.283.20.2701.


Francis, Thomas et al. 1955. “An Evaluation of the 1954 Poliomyelitis Vaccine Trials: Summary Report.” American Journal of Public Health 45 (5): 1–63.


National Cancer Institute. 2024. How Do Clinical Trials Work? National Cancer Institute. https://www.cancer.gov/research/participate/clinical-trials/how-trials-work.


Porter, Jane, and Hershel Jick. 1980. “Addiction Rare in Patients Treated with Narcotics.” New England Journal of Medicine 302 (2): 123. https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJM198001103020221.


US Department of Health and Human Services. 2018. Federal Policy for the Protection of Human Subjects (“the Common Rule”). Code of Federal Regulations, Title 45, Part 46. https://www.hhs.gov/ohrp/regulations-and-policy/regulations/common-rule/index.html.


World Medical Association. 2024. WMA Declaration of Helsinki – Ethical Principles for Medical Research Involving Human Participants. Adopted by the 75th WMA General Assembly, Helsinki, Finland. https://www.wma.net/policies-post/wma-declaration-of-helsinki/.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
You Wake Up Connected

You wake up in a hospital bed. In the bed beside you is a famous, unconscious violinist. The Society of Music Lovers has discovered that you alone have the right blood type to keep his failing kidneys filtering. Overnight they kidnapped you, sedated you, and plugged his circulatory system into yours. The hospital director apologizes. The violinist will die if you unplug. He needs you for nine months.
Write or discuss:

Are you morally required to stay plugged in for nine months?
If you unplug, have you killed him — or simply refused to save him?
Where, exactly, does this scenario stop resembling a pregnancy?



This is Judith Jarvis Thomson’s 1971 case. Don’t formalize it yet — let students react. We’ll come back and reconstruct it as an explicit argument later in the lecture.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
After You’ve Discussed






Note


Three things to hold onto from that scene:

A right to life is not automatically a right to use someone else’s body. These are two different rights, and people slide between them.
There’s a moral difference between “killing” and “letting die.” Where you draw that line will decide many other cases in this course.
Today’s job is to learn a tool, not to settle the question. By the end you will be able to point at the specific sentence in any abortion argument that you think is wrong. That sentence is where the real disagreement lives.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Today

By the end of Part A, you will be able to:

Rewrite an everyday argument in standard form (numbered premises → conclusion) and distinguish validity from soundness
State Don Marquis’s future-like-ours argument and the strongest objection to it
Apply Mary Anne Warren’s five marks of personhood to borderline cases (zygote, newborn, adult with advanced dementia, dolphin, AI)
Reconstruct Judith Jarvis Thomson’s violinist case as a formal argument and locate at least two disanalogies critics press against it
Explain how Rosalind Hursthouse’s virtue-ethics approach reframes the abortion question away from the status of the fetus
Identify, for any abortion argument, the load-bearing premise — the one premise the disagreement actually turns on

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
What Standard Form Looks Like
In philosophy, we make arguments visible by stacking the premises in a numbered list and setting the conclusion below a line. A toy example, far from abortion:


A Simple Argument


All registered nurses in Minnesota are licensed by the Board of Nursing.
Jamie is a registered nurse working in Rochester, Minnesota.
Therefore, Jamie is licensed by the Board of Nursing.


Once an argument is in this shape, disagreement becomes precise: you no longer reject “the whole thing,” you reject a specific premise, or you reject the inference from premises to conclusion.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Validity vs. Soundness


Valid
An argument is valid when the conclusion follows from the premises — if the premises were all true, the conclusion would have to be true.
Validity is about the shape of the argument, not the truth of any individual sentence.



Sound
An argument is sound when it is valid and all its premises are actually true.
Most real bioethics arguments are valid; the fight is almost always over whether one of the premises is actually true. That is what to look for.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
section: Two Traps to Watch For — The Equivocation Trap
text:
Two Traps to Watch For — The Equivocation Trap
The word “human” has two very different senses, and arguments about abortion routinely slide between them without anyone noticing.


Biological sense. “Human” = a living member of the species Homo sapiens. A zygote, a fingernail clipping, a tumor of human tissue — all human in this sense.



Moral sense. “Human” = a being with the kind of mental and moral life that grounds a right to life. An adult patient, plausibly a newborn — human in this sense.


The trap. “The fetus is human” is true in the biological sense — but the conclusion “it is wrong to kill the fetus” only follows if “human” means the moral sense. Sliding between the two senses inside a single argument is the fallacy of equivocation, and it is the single most common error in abortion debates on both sides.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
section: Two Traps to Watch For — Begging the Question
text:
Two Traps to Watch For — Begging the Question
An argument begs the question when one of its premises already assumes the very conclusion it is trying to establish. The argument doesn’t prove anything; it just restates.


Anti-abortion version:

Abortion is the murder of an innocent person.
Murder is wrong.
∴ Abortion is wrong.

The word “murder” has already done all the work — it means wrongful killing of a person. Premise 1 is the conclusion.



Pro-choice version:

The fetus is just a clump of tissue in the woman’s body.
People can do as they wish with tissue in their own bodies.
∴ Abortion is permissible.

“Just a clump of tissue” assumes the fetus has no moral status — which is the very point in dispute.


Once students see equivocation and question-begging, they hear them everywhere — these are transferable skills for the rest of the course.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Activity 1 — Reconstruct It

Here is a passage in the style philosophers actually write in. Pull it apart into standard form on paper or in a text box.

“Look — every one of us was once a fetus. If our mothers had ended those pregnancies, none of us would be here to argue about it. The fetus you are talking about already has a future ahead of it, the same kind of future you and I value in our own lives. To cut that future off is no different in principle from cutting yours off. That is why abortion is wrong.”

Write or discuss:

What is the conclusion of this passage? (One sentence.)
What are the premises — the claims the author needs you to accept to get to that conclusion? (Number them 1, 2, 3.)
Which premise do you find least secure, and why?



This is a paraphrase of Marquis. We’ll formalize it carefully in the next section — students should be primed to see it again.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
A Short Biological Primer

A zygote is the single cell formed at fertilization, when sperm and egg fuse.
An embryo is the developing organism from implantation (~2 weeks) through the eighth week, when the major organ systems first form.
A fetus is the developing human from the ninth week of gestation until birth.
Viability is the point at which a fetus could plausibly survive outside the womb with intensive medical support — currently around 22–24 weeks.


Gestational age vs. fertilization age. Clinicians count pregnancy from the first day of the last menstrual period (LMP), about two weeks before fertilization. So a “12-week pregnancy” describes a roughly 10-week-old embryo or early fetus.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Timeline of Fetal Development


Adapted from clinically standard developmental timelines.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
section: Trimester at a Glance — What Patients Are Actually Experiencing
text:
Trimester at a Glance — What Patients Are Actually Experiencing









Trimester
Weeks (LMP)
What is developing
What care typically looks like




First
0–13
Implantation; neural tube closes (~wk 4); cardiac activity detectable (~wk 6); limb buds; basic organ systems
Confirmation of pregnancy, prenatal vitamins, first ultrasound, ~80% of US abortions occur here


Second
14–27
Quickening (felt movement, ~16–22 wk); anatomy scan (~20 wk); viability threshold approaches (~22–24 wk)
Anatomy ultrasound; diagnostic testing for fetal anomalies; most pregnancy losses here are not elective


Third
28–40
Rapid weight gain; lung maturation; final neurological wiring; positioning for birth
Frequent prenatal visits; delivery planning; elective abortion is extremely rare and almost always for fetal anomaly or maternal life




Why this matters for nursing. Patients use clinical week counts, not fertilization counts. A “six-week pregnancy” is a roughly four-week-old embryo, smaller than a grain of rice. Some recent state laws are written to take effect at clinical week 6 — before many people realize they are pregnant.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
How Abortion Is Actually Performed


Medication abortion (up to ~10–11 wk; ~63% of US abortions in 2023). Often at home.

Mifepristone blocks progesterone, ending the pregnancy.
Misoprostol, taken 24–48 hours later, causes the uterus to contract and expel the pregnancy.




Procedural abortion in clinics (any gestational age; ~37% of US abortions).

Aspiration / vacuum (most first-trimester procedures): the uterine contents are removed through a thin tube; takes ~5–10 minutes.
Dilation and evacuation (D&E) (second trimester): cervix dilated; instruments and suction used to empty the uterus.



The pre-2022 picture is shifting. After Dobbs (2022), state-by-state law has become the dominant variable in what is actually available to a given patient. Part B picks this up.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Who Actually Has Abortions



About 1 in 4 US women will have an abortion by age 45.
~60% of those obtaining abortions are already mothers.
The modal patient is in her 20s, low-income, and identifies as a Christian.
The most-cited reasons are economic instability, timing, and responsibilities to existing children.
Roughly 80% of US abortions occur in the first trimester; <1% occur after 21 weeks, almost always for severe fetal anomaly or maternal life.










Note


Why these numbers matter. Public images of abortion often imagine a single, young, childless patient making a quick decision. The data describe a different population: women already raising children, deciding from inside the constraints of an existing family.





Figures from Guttmacher Institute and CDC Abortion Surveillance, 2020–2023.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
What Makes Something Matter Morally?
To have moral status is to count, in your own right, in moral reasoning — to be the kind of thing it can be wrong to harm, not just the kind of thing it can be wrong to damage.


Biological humanity. Being a living member of the species Homo sapiens. A zygote qualifies; a chimpanzee does not.
Sentience. Being able to feel — pleasure, pain, fear. Early fetuses do not; late-term fetuses and most animals do.



Personhood. Having the psychological features (consciousness, reasoning, self-awareness) that make someone a who, not just a what.
Potentiality. Being the kind of thing that, left to develop, will become one of the above.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
section: Why the Criterion You Pick Decides Almost Everything
text:
Why the Criterion You Pick Decides Almost Everything
Each candidate criterion delivers a different verdict on the early fetus. | Criterion | Does the early fetus qualify? | Which argument it lines up with | |—|:—:|—| | Biological humanity | Yes — Homo sapiens from fertilization | The conservative right-to-life argument | | Sentience | No — no functional pain pathways until ~24 wk | Most pro-choice arguments, esp. early abortion | | Personhood (psychological) | No — no consciousness, reasoning, or self-awareness | Warren’s argument | | Potentiality (a future like ours) | Yes — will develop into a full person if not killed | Marquis’s argument |

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Argument 1 — The Future-Like-Ours Argument


Marquis: Abortion Deprives a Fetus of a Future Like Ours


Don Marquis, “Why Abortion Is Immoral” (1989)


What makes killing an adult human being seriously wrong is that it deprives the victim of all the experiences, projects, activities, and relationships that would otherwise have made up their future.
Whatever it is that makes killing wrong in adults is what makes it wrong in any case.
A standard human fetus, if not killed, has a future containing experiences, projects, activities, and relationships of the same general kind — a future like ours.
Therefore, killing a standard human fetus is seriously wrong, for the same reason killing an adult is.


(Marquis 1989)

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
section: The Strange Company Marquis’s Principle Keeps
text:
The Strange Company Marquis’s Principle Keeps
Marquis’s principle — killing is wrong when it deprives a being of a valuable future — has consequences that surprise people on both sides of the debate.



Contraception is not wrong. There is no being yet to be deprived; sperm + egg = no subject of a future.
Removing life support from a permanently comatose patient may not be wrong — there is no valuable future to lose.
Abortion of an anencephalic fetus (no brain development possible) is permissible — no future of the relevant kind.





Most abortions for socioeconomic reasons are seriously wrong — the fetus’s future doesn’t care about the mother’s circumstances.
Abortion for fetal Down syndrome is wrong — those futures are valuable too.
Killing many animals may be wrong if they have futures of value (Marquis himself is cautious here).

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Marquis: Unpacking “a Future Like Ours”
Marquis’s argument is unusual: it does not depend on the fetus being a “person” right now, or on having a soul, or on a heartbeat. It depends only on the fetus having a valuable future.
That move has two consequences worth noticing:

It explains why killing a temporarily unconscious adult is wrong: their future is still there, waiting for them, even though they are not currently conscious. The same logic, Marquis says, covers the fetus.
It does not automatically cover a permanently comatose patient, or a fertilized egg in a clinic freezer that no one will implant — because those entities arguably have no future to be deprived of.

The premise doing the heavy lifting is P3 — that the fetus’s future is “of the same general kind” as yours. That is where critics push.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Objection to Marquis


The Identity Objection (targets P3)


A common reply in the secondary literature (Harman 2025, sec. 2.2)


For losing a future to be a harm to me, the future being lost must be mine — it must belong to a continuous psychological subject who can be deprived.
A zygote or early embryo has no psychological life at all — no memories, no plans, no point of view from which a future could be “lost.”
So the early embryo is not the subject of any future; talk of “its future” is more like talk of the future of a sperm-and-egg pair before they meet.
Therefore, even granting Marquis’s general principle, it does not yet show that early abortion deprives anyone of a future.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Activity 2a — Whose Future Counts?

Marquis’s argument stands or falls on who counts as having “a future like ours.”
Write or discuss. For each entity below, does Marquis’s argument apply? Why or why not?

A 26-year-old in a coma expected to wake up in three weeks.
A 70-year-old with advanced Alzheimer’s, no memory of yesterday, who will likely die within the year.
A 6-week embryo conceived through IVF that the couple has decided not to implant.
A frozen embryo that will be donated for stem-cell research with no chance of implantation.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Argument 2 — The Personhood Argument
