Warren: A Fetus Is Not Yet a Person


Mary Anne Warren, “On the Moral and Legal Status of Abortion” (1973)


The right to life belongs to persons — beings with a recognizable mental and moral life — not to every member of the biological species Homo sapiens.
To be a person in this morally relevant sense is to possess (some significant set of) consciousness, reasoning, self-motivated activity, the capacity to communicate, and self-awareness.
A fetus, at any stage of development, possesses none of these traits to any meaningful degree.
Therefore, a fetus is not a person and does not yet have the right to life that persons have.


(Warren 1973)

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Warren, in Her Own Words


“A fetus, even a fully developed one, is considerably less personlike than is the average mature mammal, indeed the average fish. And I think that a rational person must conclude that if the right to life of a fetus is to be based upon its resemblance to a person, then it cannot be said to have any more right to life then, let us say, a newborn guppy.”


Mary Anne Warren, “On the Moral and Legal Status of Abortion” (1973), §III.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Warren: The Five Criteria
Warren argues that a fetus might have some moral value, but is not yet a person, since it lacks most of the following:







Criterion
Plain-language gloss




Consciousness
Aware of things; can feel pain or pleasure


Reasoning
Can solve new and reasonably complex problems


Self-motivated activity
Acts on its own, not just by reflex or external cue


Capacity to communicate
Can convey many kinds of messages, on many topics


Self-awareness
Has a concept of itself as a continuing subject



Critics argue that newborn infants also fail most of these tests, yet killing them is plainly murder.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Objection to Warren


The Infanticide Objection (targets P2 and P3)


A standard reply, pressed in many forms since Warren’s paper


A newborn infant lacks all five of Warren’s marks of personhood to any greater degree than a third-trimester fetus does.
So if Warren’s criteria are correct, killing a newborn is not killing a person, and is therefore not a violation of the right to life.
But killing a newborn is obviously a grave moral wrong.
Therefore, either Warren’s criteria are wrong, or Warren’s account is missing something — full moral status cannot rest on present psychological capacities alone.



Warren’s own response (in a 1982 postscript) is that infanticide is wrong because others now want and can care for the infant — which moves the moral weight off the infant’s own properties and onto social facts. Many readers find this reply unsatisfying.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Activity 2b — Personhood Scoreboard

Score each entity on each of Warren’s five criteria using 0 (none), ½ (some), or 1 (clearly present). Then write a one-sentence verdict: is this entity a person, on Warren’s account?











Entity
Conscious
Reasons
Self-motivated
Communicates
Self-aware




Zygote (day 1)







Fetus, 24 weeks







Newborn infant







Adult dolphin







Adult with advanced dementia







Large language model (ChatGPT)








Write or discuss. Which row was hardest? What does that suggest about Warren’s criteria?

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Argument 3 — The Bodily Autonomy Argument
You met this case at the very start of class. Now we will formalize it.


Thomson: A Right to Life Is Not a Right to Use Another’s Body


Judith Jarvis Thomson, “A Defense of Abortion” (1971)


Even granting that the fetus is a person with a full right to life, a right to life does not include the right to use another person’s body without that person’s ongoing consent.
A pregnant person who has not consented to the pregnancy (e.g., rape, contraceptive failure) has not granted the fetus the use of her body.
Withdrawing the use of one’s body from another is not unjust killing; it is the refusal to extend an extraordinary form of aid.
Therefore, in at least some cases, abortion does not violate any right of the fetus and is morally permissible.


(Thomson 1971)

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Thomson, in Her Own Words


“I propose, then, that we grant that the fetus is a person from the moment of conception.… I am inclined to think also that we shall probably have to agree that the fetus has already become a human person well before birth.… [But] having a right to life does not guarantee having either a right to be given the use of or a right to be allowed continued use of another person’s body — even if one needs it for life itself.”


Judith Jarvis Thomson, “A Defense of Abortion” (1971), §§1, 4.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
section: Thomson: The Move That Shocked Everyone
text:
Thomson: The Move That Shocked Everyone
The brilliance of Thomson’s argument is the phrase “even granting” in premise 1. Nearly every prior debate had assumed that if the fetus is a person, abortion is obviously wrong. Thomson said: fine, grant it — and abortion is still sometimes permissible.
This reframes the entire debate. The question is no longer only “Is the fetus a person?” It becomes: “What does a right to life actually entitle you to?”

A right to life is narrower than people assume. Thomson points out that you cannot, for instance, demand that Henry Fonda fly across the country to put his cool hand on your fevered brow — even if it is the only thing that will save your life. Your right to life does not give you a right to whatever you need to keep living.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Objection to Thomson


The Responsibility Objection (targets P2)


A standard reply (Harman 2025, sec. 1.2)


If you voluntarily engage in an activity knowing it has a foreseeable chance of producing a dependent being, you have implicitly taken on responsibility for that being.
Consensual sex is such an activity, even with contraception: pregnancy is a known, foreseeable possibility.
Therefore, in the typical case of pregnancy from consensual sex, P2 is false — the pregnant person has implicitly granted the fetus the use of her body, the way the violinist analogy does not assume.
So Thomson’s argument, even if sound in the rape case, does not generalize to most actual abortions.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Activity 3 — Disanalogy Hunt

An analogy is only as strong as its weakest joint. Thomson’s case is built on a structural likeness between being plugged into the violinist and being pregnant.
Write or discuss. List three concrete disanalogies between Thomson’s violinist case and a typical pregnancy. For each one, answer:

What is the difference?
Does the difference make the violinist case more like an easy case (where unplugging seems obviously fine) or less like an easy case?
Does the difference, in your judgment, defeat Thomson’s argument, or only limit the range of pregnancies it covers?

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Argument 4 — The Virtue-Ethics Argument


Hursthouse: The Question Is About Character, Not Status


Rosalind Hursthouse, “Virtue Theory and Abortion” (1991)


An action is morally right if and only if it is what a fully virtuous person — one with and exercising the virtues such as honesty, courage, justice, and practical wisdom — would characteristically do in those circumstances.
Whether having an abortion is what a virtuous person would do depends on the agent’s motives, circumstances, and what the choice expresses about her character.
Some abortions plainly express vices — callousness, vanity, irresponsibility, refusal to face the seriousness of what is happening.
Other abortions plainly express virtues — clear-eyed acknowledgment of a serious matter, care for existing children or dependents, honesty about one’s own limits.
Therefore, whether a particular abortion is morally right or wrong is not settled by the metaphysical status of the fetus alone; it turns on the character and circumstances of the choice.


(Hursthouse 1991)

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Hursthouse, in Her Own Words


“To think of abortion as nothing but the killing of something that does not matter, or as nothing but the exercise of some right or rights one has, or as the incidental means to some desirable state of affairs, is to do something callous and light-minded, the sort of thing that no virtuous and wise person would do. It is to have the wrong attitude not only to fetuses, but more generally to human life and death, parenthood, and family relationships.”


Rosalind Hursthouse, “Virtue Theory and Abortion” (1991), §III.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
section: Hursthouse: What a Virtuous Person Notices
text:
Hursthouse: What a Virtuous Person Notices
Hursthouse’s move is to change the subject of the question. Marquis, Warren, and Thomson all ask: what kind of thing is the fetus, and what follows? Hursthouse asks: what kind of person decides this way, in these circumstances?

Two things she insists on. First, even if abortion is sometimes permissible, the loss of a potential human life is a grave matter — to treat it as trivial is itself a moral failing. Second, character is judged not just on the act but on the motivations and attitudes around it: the same outward act, done flippantly or done with sorrow, is not morally the same.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Objection to Hursthouse


The Action-Guidance Objection (targets P1)


A standard line of criticism of virtue ethics generally


A pregnant person facing this decision needs guidance: a way to tell what to do now, not a description of what some idealized virtuous person would do.
“Do what a virtuous person would do” is unhelpful — if I knew what the virtuous person would do, I would not need the theory; and if I do not know, the theory does not tell me.
Hursthouse offers vocabulary (callous, light-minded, courageous) rather than rules, but two thoughtful people can apply that vocabulary to the same case and reach opposite verdicts.
Therefore, virtue ethics may describe what we admire in decisions about abortion, but it does not actually guide the decision itself.



Hursthouse’s own reply is that no theory escapes this problem — utilitarians have to predict consequences they cannot really know; Kantians have to interpret maxims. Ethics is just tough!

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Activity 4 — A Different Question

Hursthouse refuses to settle abortion at the level of the fetus’s status. Instead, she asks what the decision expresses about a person.
Write or discuss. Consider three pregnant people, each at 10 weeks, each choosing to terminate:

A: a 16-year-old who became pregnant the first time she had sex, terrified to tell her parents, sees no way to continue school.
B: a 32-year-old who already has two young children, is barely paying rent, and knows another child would mean none of them eat enough.
C: a 26-year-old who would have to cancel a long-planned European vacation if she carried to term.


On Hursthouse’s view, does her judgment of the decision differ across the three cases? Why?
Does it differ in ways that Marquis, Warren, or Thomson could even talk about?
Is anything important missed by Hursthouse’s focus on character?

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
The Landscape So Far
Four arguments, side by side. Notice that the disagreement almost always lives at one specific premise — and once you see which one, it becomes something you can reason about.









Argument
Verdict
Load-bearing premise
What you’d have to deny




Marquis (FLO)
Most abortion is seriously wrong
P3: the fetus has a future “like ours”
That a being with no current psychology is the subject of a future


Warren (personhood)
Early-fetus abortion is permissible
P2: personhood is defined by these five marks
That consciousness-style criteria are the right ones (also worry: infants)


Thomson (autonomy)
Abortion is permissible even if the fetus is a person
P3: refusing body-use is not killing
That there’s a meaningful difference between killing and refusing aid


Hursthouse (virtue)
“It depends” — on motives and circumstances
P1: rightness is what the virtuous person would do
That morality reduces to character and not to rules or rights

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Activity 5 — Circle the Weakest Premise

For each of the four arguments above, name the single premise you find weakest and explain in one sentence why.
Write or discuss.

Marquis: The premise I would push back on is __________ because __________.
Warren: The premise I would push back on is __________ because __________.
Thomson: The premise I would push back on is __________ because __________.
Hursthouse: The premise I would push back on is __________ because __________.

Then: notice the pattern. Do your four “weakest” premises share something in common? That common thing is your deepest moral commitment in this debate.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Where the Real Disagreement Lives






Note


Notice what just happened. We did not ask you to decide whether abortion is right or wrong. We asked you to point at the sentence in each argument you find weakest. That sentence is where the actual disagreement between thoughtful people sits.
This is a transferable move. In end-of-life debates, in research-ethics debates, in resource-allocation debates — the same trick will work. Find the argument. Put it in standard form. Locate the load-bearing premise. Then you have a disagreement worth having.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Mini-Recap

We learned a tool — standard form — and used it on four classical arguments. Marquis grounds the wrongness of abortion in a future like ours; Warren denies that the fetus is yet a person; Thomson grants personhood and still defends bodily autonomy; Hursthouse refuses to settle the question at the level of the fetus at all and looks instead to character. In Part B we ask a different question: should the law track any of these arguments?

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Review Questions
Write a brief answer to each, or use them as discussion prompts.


Recall
State Marquis’s future-like-ours argument in standard form (three or four premises). Then identify the single premise that is doing the most work — and explain in one sentence why critics target it.


Apply
A 28-year-old patient at 11 weeks gestation tells you she is terminating because prenatal testing has revealed Tay-Sachs disease, which is uniformly fatal in early childhood. Reconstruct her reasoning as an argument in standard form, then identify which of our four frameworks (Marquis, Warren, Thomson, Hursthouse) would most readily support her decision and which would most strongly resist it.


Debate
Some critics charge that Thomson’s violinist case proves too much: if it justifies abortion, it also seems to justify a parent unplugging from a sick child who depends on continued bodily aid. Is there a principled difference between the two cases? Defend your answer using the standard-form tools from today.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Key Terms

Standard form — an argument written as numbered premises and a marked conclusion, so the inference is visible.
Valid / sound — valid = conclusion follows from premises; sound = valid plus all premises true.
Moral status — the property of mattering, morally, in one’s own right.
Personhood (Warren’s sense) — having a significant set of consciousness, reasoning, self-motivated activity, communication, self-awareness.
Future like ours (Marquis) — a future containing the kinds of experiences and projects that make adult human futures valuable.
Bodily autonomy (Thomson) — the right to determine what may be done with and to one’s body, even when another’s life depends on it.
Virtue ethics (Hursthouse) — the view that right action is what a fully virtuous person would characteristically do.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Further Reading
Accessible follow-ups beyond the works cited.

Beckwith, Francis J. Defending Life: A Moral and Legal Case Against Abortion Choice. Cambridge University Press, 2007.
Boonin, David. A Defense of Abortion. Cambridge University Press, 2003.
Kamm, Frances M. Creation and Abortion. Oxford University Press, 1992.
Little, Margaret Olivia. “Abortion, Intimacy, and the Duty to Gestate.” Ethical Theory and Moral Practice 2 (1999): 295–312.
Harman, Elizabeth. “The Ethics of Abortion.” Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy (2025). (Harman 2025)
Gordon, John-Stewart. “Abortion.” Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. (Gordon, n.d.)

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
References


Gordon, John-Stewart. n.d. Abortion. Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/abortion/.


Harman, Elizabeth. 2025. “The Ethics of Abortion.” In The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Summer 2025, edited by Edward N. Zalta and Uri Nodelman. Metaphysics Research Lab, Stanford University. https://plato.stanford.edu/archives/sum2025/entries/abortion-ethics/.


Hursthouse, Rosalind. 1991. “Virtue Theory and Abortion.” Philosophy & Public Affairs 20 (3): 223–46. https://www.jstor.org/stable/2265432.


Marquis, Don. 1989. “Why Abortion Is Immoral.” The Journal of Philosophy 86 (4): 183–202. https://doi.org/10.2307/2026961.


Thomson, Judith Jarvis. 1971. “A Defense of Abortion.” Philosophy & Public Affairs 1 (1): 47–66. https://www.jstor.org/stable/2265091.


Warren, Mary Anne. 1973. “On the Moral and Legal Status of Abortion.” The Monist 57 (1): 43–61. https://doi.org/10.5840/monist197357133.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
You wake up in a hospital bed. In the bed beside you is a famous, unconscious violinist. The Society of Music Lovers has discovered that you alone have the right blood type to keep his failing kidneys filtering. Overnight they kidnapped you, sedated you, and plugged his circulatory system into yours. The hospital director apologizes. The violinist will die if you unplug. He needs you for nine months.
Write or discuss:

Are you morally required to stay plugged in for nine months?
If you unplug, have you killed him — or simply refused to save him?
Where, exactly, does this scenario stop resembling a pregnancy?



This is Judith Jarvis Thomson’s 1971 case. Don’t formalize it yet — let students react. We’ll come back and reconstruct it as an explicit argument later in the lecture.










Note



Three things to hold onto from that scene:

A right to life is not automatically a right to use someone else’s body. These are two different rights, and people slide between them.
There’s a moral difference between “killing” and “letting die.” Where you draw that line will decide many other cases in this course.
Today’s job is to learn a tool, not to settle the question. By the end you will be able to point at the specific sentence in any abortion argument that you think is wrong. That sentence is where the real disagreement lives.







By the end of Part A, you will be able to:

Rewrite an everyday argument in standard form (numbered premises → conclusion) and distinguish validity from soundness
State Don Marquis’s future-like-ours argument and the strongest objection to it
Apply Mary Anne Warren’s five marks of personhood to borderline cases (zygote, newborn, adult with advanced dementia, dolphin, AI)
Reconstruct Judith Jarvis Thomson’s violinist case as a formal argument and locate at least two disanalogies critics press against it
Explain how Rosalind Hursthouse’s virtue-ethics approach reframes the abortion question away from the status of the fetus
Identify, for any abortion argument, the load-bearing premise — the one premise the disagreement actually turns on

You wake up in a hospital bed. In the bed beside you is a famous, unconscious violinist. The Society of Music Lovers has discovered that you alone have the right blood type to keep his failing kidneys filtering. Overnight they kidnapped you, sedated you, and plugged his circulatory system into yours. The hospital director apologizes. The violinist will die if you unplug. He needs you for nine months.
Write or discuss:

Are you morally required to stay plugged in for nine months?
If you unplug, have you killed him — or simply refused to save him?
Where, exactly, does this scenario stop resembling a pregnancy?



This is Judith Jarvis Thomson’s 1971 case. Don’t formalize it yet — let students react. We’ll come back and reconstruct it as an explicit argument later in the lecture.

Note

By the end of Part A, you will be able to:

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
2.1 What Standard Form Looks Like
In philosophy, we make arguments visible by stacking the premises in a numbered list and setting the conclusion below a line. A toy example, far from abortion:


A Simple Argument


All registered nurses in Minnesota are licensed by the Board of Nursing.
Jamie is a registered nurse working in Rochester, Minnesota.
Therefore, Jamie is licensed by the Board of Nursing.


Once an argument is in this shape, disagreement becomes precise: you no longer reject “the whole thing,” you reject a specific premise, or you reject the inference from premises to conclusion.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
2.2 Validity vs. Soundness



2.2.1 Valid
An argument is valid when the conclusion follows from the premises — if the premises were all true, the conclusion would have to be true.
Validity is about the shape of the argument, not the truth of any individual sentence.





2.2.2 Sound
An argument is sound when it is valid and all its premises are actually true.
Most real bioethics arguments are valid; the fight is almost always over whether one of the premises is actually true. That is what to look for.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
section: 2.3 Two Traps to Watch For — The Equivocation Trap
text:
2.3 Two Traps to Watch For — The Equivocation Trap
The word “human” has two very different senses, and arguments about abortion routinely slide between them without anyone noticing.


Biological sense. “Human” = a living member of the species Homo sapiens. A zygote, a fingernail clipping, a tumor of human tissue — all human in this sense.



Moral sense. “Human” = a being with the kind of mental and moral life that grounds a right to life. An adult patient, plausibly a newborn — human in this sense.



The trap. “The fetus is human” is true in the biological sense — but the conclusion “it is wrong to kill the fetus” only follows if “human” means the moral sense. Sliding between the two senses inside a single argument is the fallacy of equivocation, and it is the single most common error in abortion debates on both sides.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
section: 2.4 Two Traps to Watch For — Begging the Question
text:
2.4 Two Traps to Watch For — Begging the Question
An argument begs the question when one of its premises already assumes the very conclusion it is trying to establish. The argument doesn’t prove anything; it just restates.


Anti-abortion version:

Abortion is the murder of an innocent person.
Murder is wrong.
∴ Abortion is wrong.

The word “murder” has already done all the work — it means wrongful killing of a person. Premise 1 is the conclusion.



Pro-choice version:

The fetus is just a clump of tissue in the woman’s body.
People can do as they wish with tissue in their own bodies.
∴ Abortion is permissible.

“Just a clump of tissue” assumes the fetus has no moral status — which is the very point in dispute.



Once students see equivocation and question-begging, they hear them everywhere — these are transferable skills for the rest of the course.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
2.5 Activity 1 — Reconstruct It

Here is a passage in the style philosophers actually write in. Pull it apart into standard form on paper or in a text box.

“Look — every one of us was once a fetus. If our mothers had ended those pregnancies, none of us would be here to argue about it. The fetus you are talking about already has a future ahead of it, the same kind of future you and I value in our own lives. To cut that future off is no different in principle from cutting yours off. That is why abortion is wrong.”

Write or discuss:

What is the conclusion of this passage? (One sentence.)
What are the premises — the claims the author needs you to accept to get to that conclusion? (Number them 1, 2, 3.)
Which premise do you find least secure, and why?



This is a paraphrase of Marquis. We’ll formalize it carefully in the next section — students should be primed to see it again.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
3.1 A Short Biological Primer

A zygote is the single cell formed at fertilization, when sperm and egg fuse.
An embryo is the developing organism from implantation (~2 weeks) through the eighth week, when the major organ systems first form.
A fetus is the developing human from the ninth week of gestation until birth.
Viability is the point at which a fetus could plausibly survive outside the womb with intensive medical support — currently around 22–24 weeks.


Gestational age vs. fertilization age. Clinicians count pregnancy from the first day of the last menstrual period (LMP), about two weeks before fertilization. So a “12-week pregnancy” describes a roughly 10-week-old embryo or early fetus.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
3.2 Timeline of Fetal Development


Adapted from clinically standard developmental timelines.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
section: 3.3 Trimester at a Glance — What Patients Are Actually Experiencing
text:
3.3 Trimester at a Glance — What Patients Are Actually Experiencing









Trimester
Weeks (LMP)
What is developing
What care typically looks like




First
0–13
Implantation; neural tube closes (~wk 4); cardiac activity detectable (~wk 6); limb buds; basic organ systems
Confirmation of pregnancy, prenatal vitamins, first ultrasound, ~80% of US abortions occur here


Second
14–27
Quickening (felt movement, ~16–22 wk); anatomy scan (~20 wk); viability threshold approaches (~22–24 wk)
Anatomy ultrasound; diagnostic testing for fetal anomalies; most pregnancy losses here are not elective


Third
28–40
Rapid weight gain; lung maturation; final neurological wiring; positioning for birth
Frequent prenatal visits; delivery planning; elective abortion is extremely rare and almost always for fetal anomaly or maternal life




Why this matters for nursing. Patients use clinical week counts, not fertilization counts. A “six-week pregnancy” is a roughly four-week-old embryo, smaller than a grain of rice. Some recent state laws are written to take effect at clinical week 6 — before many people realize they are pregnant.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
3.4 How Abortion Is Actually Performed


Medication abortion (up to ~10–11 wk; ~63% of US abortions in 2023). Often at home.

Mifepristone blocks progesterone, ending the pregnancy.
Misoprostol, taken 24–48 hours later, causes the uterus to contract and expel the pregnancy.




Procedural abortion in clinics (any gestational age; ~37% of US abortions).

Aspiration / vacuum (most first-trimester procedures): the uterine contents are removed through a thin tube; takes ~5–10 minutes.
Dilation and evacuation (D&E) (second trimester): cervix dilated; instruments and suction used to empty the uterus.




The pre-2022 picture is shifting. After Dobbs (2022), state-by-state law has become the dominant variable in what is actually available to a given patient. Part B picks this up.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
3.5 Who Actually Has Abortions



About 1 in 4 US women will have an abortion by age 45.
~60% of those obtaining abortions are already mothers.
The modal patient is in her 20s, low-income, and identifies as a Christian.
The most-cited reasons are economic instability, timing, and responsibilities to existing children.
Roughly 80% of US abortions occur in the first trimester; <1% occur after 21 weeks, almost always for severe fetal anomaly or maternal life.










Note



Why these numbers matter. Public images of abortion often imagine a single, young, childless patient making a quick decision. The data describe a different population: women already raising children, deciding from inside the constraints of an existing family.





Figures from Guttmacher Institute and CDC Abortion Surveillance, 2020–2023.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
3.6 What Makes Something Matter Morally?
To have moral status is to count, in your own right, in moral reasoning — to be the kind of thing it can be wrong to harm, not just the kind of thing it can be wrong to damage.


Biological humanity. Being a living member of the species Homo sapiens. A zygote qualifies; a chimpanzee does not.
Sentience. Being able to feel — pleasure, pain, fear. Early fetuses do not; late-term fetuses and most animals do.



Personhood. Having the psychological features (consciousness, reasoning, self-awareness) that make someone a who, not just a what.
Potentiality. Being the kind of thing that, left to develop, will become one of the above.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
section: 3.7 Why the Criterion You Pick Decides Almost Everything
text:
3.7 Why the Criterion You Pick Decides Almost Everything
Each candidate criterion delivers a different verdict on the early fetus. | Criterion | Does the early fetus qualify? | Which argument it lines up with | |—|:—:|—| | Biological humanity | Yes — Homo sapiens from fertilization | The conservative right-to-life argument | | Sentience | No — no functional pain pathways until ~24 wk | Most pro-choice arguments, esp. early abortion | | Personhood (psychological) | No — no consciousness, reasoning, or self-awareness | Warren’s argument | | Potentiality (a future like ours) | Yes — will develop into a full person if not killed | Marquis’s argument |

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
4.1 Argument 1 — The Future-Like-Ours Argument


Marquis: Abortion Deprives a Fetus of a Future Like Ours


Don Marquis, “Why Abortion Is Immoral” (1989)


What makes killing an adult human being seriously wrong is that it deprives the victim of all the experiences, projects, activities, and relationships that would otherwise have made up their future.
Whatever it is that makes killing wrong in adults is what makes it wrong in any case.
A standard human fetus, if not killed, has a future containing experiences, projects, activities, and relationships of the same general kind — a future like ours.
Therefore, killing a standard human fetus is seriously wrong, for the same reason killing an adult is.


(Marquis 1989)

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
section: 4.2 The Strange Company Marquis’s Principle Keeps
text:
4.2 The Strange Company Marquis’s Principle Keeps
Marquis’s principle — killing is wrong when it deprives a being of a valuable future — has consequences that surprise people on both sides of the debate.



Contraception is not wrong. There is no being yet to be deprived; sperm + egg = no subject of a future.
Removing life support from a permanently comatose patient may not be wrong — there is no valuable future to lose.
Abortion of an anencephalic fetus (no brain development possible) is permissible — no future of the relevant kind.





Most abortions for socioeconomic reasons are seriously wrong — the fetus’s future doesn’t care about the mother’s circumstances.
Abortion for fetal Down syndrome is wrong — those futures are valuable too.
Killing many animals may be wrong if they have futures of value (Marquis himself is cautious here).

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
4.3 Marquis: Unpacking “a Future Like Ours”
Marquis’s argument is unusual: it does not depend on the fetus being a “person” right now, or on having a soul, or on a heartbeat. It depends only on the fetus having a valuable future.
That move has two consequences worth noticing:

It explains why killing a temporarily unconscious adult is wrong: their future is still there, waiting for them, even though they are not currently conscious. The same logic, Marquis says, covers the fetus.
It does not automatically cover a permanently comatose patient, or a fertilized egg in a clinic freezer that no one will implant — because those entities arguably have no future to be deprived of.

The premise doing the heavy lifting is P3 — that the fetus’s future is “of the same general kind” as yours. That is where critics push.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
4.4 Objection to Marquis


The Identity Objection (targets P3)


A common reply in the secondary literature (Harman 2025, sec. 2.2)


For losing a future to be a harm to me, the future being lost must be mine — it must belong to a continuous psychological subject who can be deprived.
A zygote or early embryo has no psychological life at all — no memories, no plans, no point of view from which a future could be “lost.”
So the early embryo is not the subject of any future; talk of “its future” is more like talk of the future of a sperm-and-egg pair before they meet.
Therefore, even granting Marquis’s general principle, it does not yet show that early abortion deprives anyone of a future.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
4.5 Activity 2a — Whose Future Counts?

Marquis’s argument stands or falls on who counts as having “a future like ours.”
Write or discuss. For each entity below, does Marquis’s argument apply? Why or why not?

A 26-year-old in a coma expected to wake up in three weeks.
A 70-year-old with advanced Alzheimer’s, no memory of yesterday, who will likely die within the year.
A 6-week embryo conceived through IVF that the couple has decided not to implant.
A frozen embryo that will be donated for stem-cell research with no chance of implantation.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
4.6 Argument 2 — The Personhood Argument


Warren: A Fetus Is Not Yet a Person


Mary Anne Warren, “On the Moral and Legal Status of Abortion” (1973)


The right to life belongs to persons — beings with a recognizable mental and moral life — not to every member of the biological species Homo sapiens.
To be a person in this morally relevant sense is to possess (some significant set of) consciousness, reasoning, self-motivated activity, the capacity to communicate, and self-awareness.
A fetus, at any stage of development, possesses none of these traits to any meaningful degree.
Therefore, a fetus is not a person and does not yet have the right to life that persons have.


(Warren 1973)

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
4.7 Warren, in Her Own Words


“A fetus, even a fully developed one, is considerably less personlike than is the average mature mammal, indeed the average fish. And I think that a rational person must conclude that if the right to life of a fetus is to be based upon its resemblance to a person, then it cannot be said to have any more right to life then, let us say, a newborn guppy.”


Mary Anne Warren, “On the Moral and Legal Status of Abortion” (1973), §III.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
4.8 Warren: The Five Criteria
Warren argues that a fetus might have some moral value, but is not yet a person, since it lacks most of the following:







Criterion
Plain-language gloss




Consciousness
Aware of things; can feel pain or pleasure


Reasoning
Can solve new and reasonably complex problems


Self-motivated activity
Acts on its own, not just by reflex or external cue


Capacity to communicate
Can convey many kinds of messages, on many topics


Self-awareness
Has a concept of itself as a continuing subject



Critics argue that newborn infants also fail most of these tests, yet killing them is plainly murder.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
4.9 Objection to Warren


The Infanticide Objection (targets P2 and P3)


A standard reply, pressed in many forms since Warren’s paper


A newborn infant lacks all five of Warren’s marks of personhood to any greater degree than a third-trimester fetus does.
So if Warren’s criteria are correct, killing a newborn is not killing a person, and is therefore not a violation of the right to life.
But killing a newborn is obviously a grave moral wrong.
Therefore, either Warren’s criteria are wrong, or Warren’s account is missing something — full moral status cannot rest on present psychological capacities alone.



Warren’s own response (in a 1982 postscript) is that infanticide is wrong because others now want and can care for the infant — which moves the moral weight off the infant’s own properties and onto social facts. Many readers find this reply unsatisfying.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
4.10 Activity 2b — Personhood Scoreboard

Score each entity on each of Warren’s five criteria using 0 (none), ½ (some), or 1 (clearly present). Then write a one-sentence verdict: is this entity a person, on Warren’s account?











Entity
Conscious
Reasons
Self-motivated
Communicates
Self-aware




Zygote (day 1)







Fetus, 24 weeks







Newborn infant







Adult dolphin







Adult with advanced dementia







Large language model (ChatGPT)








Write or discuss. Which row was hardest? What does that suggest about Warren’s criteria?

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
4.11 Argument 3 — The Bodily Autonomy Argument
You met this case at the very start of class. Now we will formalize it.


Thomson: A Right to Life Is Not a Right to Use Another’s Body


Judith Jarvis Thomson, “A Defense of Abortion” (1971)


Even granting that the fetus is a person with a full right to life, a right to life does not include the right to use another person’s body without that person’s ongoing consent.
A pregnant person who has not consented to the pregnancy (e.g., rape, contraceptive failure) has not granted the fetus the use of her body.
Withdrawing the use of one’s body from another is not unjust killing; it is the refusal to extend an extraordinary form of aid.
Therefore, in at least some cases, abortion does not violate any right of the fetus and is morally permissible.


(Thomson 1971)

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
4.12 Thomson, in Her Own Words


“I propose, then, that we grant that the fetus is a person from the moment of conception.… I am inclined to think also that we shall probably have to agree that the fetus has already become a human person well before birth.… [But] having a right to life does not guarantee having either a right to be given the use of or a right to be allowed continued use of another person’s body — even if one needs it for life itself.”


Judith Jarvis Thomson, “A Defense of Abortion” (1971), §§1, 4.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
section: 4.13 Thomson: The Move That Shocked Everyone
text:
4.13 Thomson: The Move That Shocked Everyone
The brilliance of Thomson’s argument is the phrase “even granting” in premise 1. Nearly every prior debate had assumed that if the fetus is a person, abortion is obviously wrong. Thomson said: fine, grant it — and abortion is still sometimes permissible.
This reframes the entire debate. The question is no longer only “Is the fetus a person?” It becomes: “What does a right to life actually entitle you to?”

A right to life is narrower than people assume. Thomson points out that you cannot, for instance, demand that Henry Fonda fly across the country to put his cool hand on your fevered brow — even if it is the only thing that will save your life. Your right to life does not give you a right to whatever you need to keep living.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
4.14 Objection to Thomson


The Responsibility Objection (targets P2)


A standard reply (Harman 2025, sec. 1.2)


If you voluntarily engage in an activity knowing it has a foreseeable chance of producing a dependent being, you have implicitly taken on responsibility for that being.
Consensual sex is such an activity, even with contraception: pregnancy is a known, foreseeable possibility.
Therefore, in the typical case of pregnancy from consensual sex, P2 is false — the pregnant person has implicitly granted the fetus the use of her body, the way the violinist analogy does not assume.
So Thomson’s argument, even if sound in the rape case, does not generalize to most actual abortions.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
4.15 Activity 3 — Disanalogy Hunt

An analogy is only as strong as its weakest joint. Thomson’s case is built on a structural likeness between being plugged into the violinist and being pregnant.
Write or discuss. List three concrete disanalogies between Thomson’s violinist case and a typical pregnancy. For each one, answer:

What is the difference?
Does the difference make the violinist case more like an easy case (where unplugging seems obviously fine) or less like an easy case?
Does the difference, in your judgment, defeat Thomson’s argument, or only limit the range of pregnancies it covers?

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
4.16 Argument 4 — The Virtue-Ethics Argument


Hursthouse: The Question Is About Character, Not Status


Rosalind Hursthouse, “Virtue Theory and Abortion” (1991)


An action is morally right if and only if it is what a fully virtuous person — one with and exercising the virtues such as honesty, courage, justice, and practical wisdom — would characteristically do in those circumstances.
Whether having an abortion is what a virtuous person would do depends on the agent’s motives, circumstances, and what the choice expresses about her character.
Some abortions plainly express vices — callousness, vanity, irresponsibility, refusal to face the seriousness of what is happening.
Other abortions plainly express virtues — clear-eyed acknowledgment of a serious matter, care for existing children or dependents, honesty about one’s own limits.
Therefore, whether a particular abortion is morally right or wrong is not settled by the metaphysical status of the fetus alone; it turns on the character and circumstances of the choice.


(Hursthouse 1991)

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
4.17 Hursthouse, in Her Own Words


“To think of abortion as nothing but the killing of something that does not matter, or as nothing but the exercise of some right or rights one has, or as the incidental means to some desirable state of affairs, is to do something callous and light-minded, the sort of thing that no virtuous and wise person would do. It is to have the wrong attitude not only to fetuses, but more generally to human life and death, parenthood, and family relationships.”


Rosalind Hursthouse, “Virtue Theory and Abortion” (1991), §III.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
section: 4.18 Hursthouse: What a Virtuous Person Notices
text:
4.18 Hursthouse: What a Virtuous Person Notices
Hursthouse’s move is to change the subject of the question. Marquis, Warren, and Thomson all ask: what kind of thing is the fetus, and what follows? Hursthouse asks: what kind of person decides this way, in these circumstances?

Two things she insists on. First, even if abortion is sometimes permissible, the loss of a potential human life is a grave matter — to treat it as trivial is itself a moral failing. Second, character is judged not just on the act but on the motivations and attitudes around it: the same outward act, done flippantly or done with sorrow, is not morally the same.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
4.19 Objection to Hursthouse


The Action-Guidance Objection (targets P1)


A standard line of criticism of virtue ethics generally


A pregnant person facing this decision needs guidance: a way to tell what to do now, not a description of what some idealized virtuous person would do.
“Do what a virtuous person would do” is unhelpful — if I knew what the virtuous person would do, I would not need the theory; and if I do not know, the theory does not tell me.
Hursthouse offers vocabulary (callous, light-minded, courageous) rather than rules, but two thoughtful people can apply that vocabulary to the same case and reach opposite verdicts.
Therefore, virtue ethics may describe what we admire in decisions about abortion, but it does not actually guide the decision itself.



Hursthouse’s own reply is that no theory escapes this problem — utilitarians have to predict consequences they cannot really know; Kantians have to interpret maxims. Ethics is just tough!

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
4.20 Activity 4 — A Different Question

Hursthouse refuses to settle abortion at the level of the fetus’s status. Instead, she asks what the decision expresses about a person.
Write or discuss. Consider three pregnant people, each at 10 weeks, each choosing to terminate:

A: a 16-year-old who became pregnant the first time she had sex, terrified to tell her parents, sees no way to continue school.
B: a 32-year-old who already has two young children, is barely paying rent, and knows another child would mean none of them eat enough.
C: a 26-year-old who would have to cancel a long-planned European vacation if she carried to term.


On Hursthouse’s view, does her judgment of the decision differ across the three cases? Why?
Does it differ in ways that Marquis, Warren, or Thomson could even talk about?
Is anything important missed by Hursthouse’s focus on character?

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
5.1 The Landscape So Far
Four arguments, side by side. Notice that the disagreement almost always lives at one specific premise — and once you see which one, it becomes something you can reason about.









Argument
Verdict
Load-bearing premise
What you’d have to deny




Marquis (FLO)
Most abortion is seriously wrong
P3: the fetus has a future “like ours”
That a being with no current psychology is the subject of a future


Warren (personhood)
Early-fetus abortion is permissible
P2: personhood is defined by these five marks
That consciousness-style criteria are the right ones (also worry: infants)


Thomson (autonomy)
Abortion is permissible even if the fetus is a person
P3: refusing body-use is not killing
That there’s a meaningful difference between killing and refusing aid


Hursthouse (virtue)
“It depends” — on motives and circumstances
P1: rightness is what the virtuous person would do
That morality reduces to character and not to rules or rights

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
5.2 Activity 5 — Circle the Weakest Premise

For each of the four arguments above, name the single premise you find weakest and explain in one sentence why.
Write or discuss.

Marquis: The premise I would push back on is __________ because __________.
Warren: The premise I would push back on is __________ because __________.
Thomson: The premise I would push back on is __________ because __________.
Hursthouse: The premise I would push back on is __________ because __________.

Then: notice the pattern. Do your four “weakest” premises share something in common? That common thing is your deepest moral commitment in this debate.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
5.3 Where the Real Disagreement Lives






Note



Notice what just happened. We did not ask you to decide whether abortion is right or wrong. We asked you to point at the sentence in each argument you find weakest. That sentence is where the actual disagreement between thoughtful people sits.
This is a transferable move. In end-of-life debates, in research-ethics debates, in resource-allocation debates — the same trick will work. Find the argument. Put it in standard form. Locate the load-bearing premise. Then you have a disagreement worth having.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
5.4 Mini-Recap

We learned a tool — standard form — and used it on four classical arguments. Marquis grounds the wrongness of abortion in a future like ours; Warren denies that the fetus is yet a person; Thomson grants personhood and still defends bodily autonomy; Hursthouse refuses to settle the question at the level of the fetus at all and looks instead to character. In Part B we ask a different question: should the law track any of these arguments?

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
5.5 Review Questions
Write a brief answer to each, or use them as discussion prompts.


Recall
State Marquis’s future-like-ours argument in standard form (three or four premises). Then identify the single premise that is doing the most work — and explain in one sentence why critics target it.


Apply
A 28-year-old patient at 11 weeks gestation tells you she is terminating because prenatal testing has revealed Tay-Sachs disease, which is uniformly fatal in early childhood. Reconstruct her reasoning as an argument in standard form, then identify which of our four frameworks (Marquis, Warren, Thomson, Hursthouse) would most readily support her decision and which would most strongly resist it.


Debate
Some critics charge that Thomson’s violinist case proves too much: if it justifies abortion, it also seems to justify a parent unplugging from a sick child who depends on continued bodily aid. Is there a principled difference between the two cases? Defend your answer using the standard-form tools from today.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
5.6 Key Terms

Standard form — an argument written as numbered premises and a marked conclusion, so the inference is visible.
Valid / sound — valid = conclusion follows from premises; sound = valid plus all premises true.
Moral status — the property of mattering, morally, in one’s own right.
Personhood (Warren’s sense) — having a significant set of consciousness, reasoning, self-motivated activity, communication, self-awareness.
Future like ours (Marquis) — a future containing the kinds of experiences and projects that make adult human futures valuable.
Bodily autonomy (Thomson) — the right to determine what may be done with and to one’s body, even when another’s life depends on it.
Virtue ethics (Hursthouse) — the view that right action is what a fully virtuous person would characteristically do.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
5.7 Further Reading
Accessible follow-ups beyond the works cited.

Beckwith, Francis J. Defending Life: A Moral and Legal Case Against Abortion Choice. Cambridge University Press, 2007.
Boonin, David. A Defense of Abortion. Cambridge University Press, 2003.
Kamm, Frances M. Creation and Abortion. Oxford University Press, 1992.
Little, Margaret Olivia. “Abortion, Intimacy, and the Duty to Gestate.” Ethical Theory and Moral Practice 2 (1999): 295–312.
Harman, Elizabeth. “The Ethics of Abortion.” Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy (2025). (Harman 2025)
Gordon, John-Stewart. “Abortion.” Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. (Gordon, n.d.)

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
5.8 References


Gordon, John-Stewart. n.d. Abortion. Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/abortion/.


Harman, Elizabeth. 2025. “The Ethics of Abortion.” In The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Summer 2025, edited by Edward N. Zalta and Uri Nodelman. Metaphysics Research Lab, Stanford University. https://plato.stanford.edu/archives/sum2025/entries/abortion-ethics/.


Hursthouse, Rosalind. 1991. “Virtue Theory and Abortion.” Philosophy & Public Affairs 20 (3): 223–46. https://www.jstor.org/stable/2265432.


Marquis, Don. 1989. “Why Abortion Is Immoral.” The Journal of Philosophy 86 (4): 183–202. https://doi.org/10.2307/2026961.


Thomson, Judith Jarvis. 1971. “A Defense of Abortion.” Philosophy & Public Affairs 1 (1): 47–66. https://www.jstor.org/stable/2265091.


Warren, Mary Anne. 1973. “On the Moral and Legal Status of Abortion.” The Monist 57 (1): 43–61. https://doi.org/10.5840/monist197357133.

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
text:
A Patient at Twenty-Two Weeks

A 22-week-pregnant woman is admitted to the ICU with severe bacterial pneumonia. She is awake and oriented. The team recommends intubation and IV antibiotics; without intubation, she will likely die within seventy-two hours. She refuses, citing fetal effects of the sedation, and says she will take her chances.
Write or discuss:

Is her refusal morally identical to the refusal of a non-pregnant patient in the same clinical state?
Does the answer change if she becomes briefly delirious from oxygen deprivation overnight?
If the team disagrees with her decision, what — short of going to court — are the team’s legitimate options?



Don’t formalize. Let students react. We’ll come back to the legal contours of cases like this when we discuss In re A.C. and Burton v. State.

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
text:
After You’ve Discussed






Note



The standard model of autonomy was built for one bounded patient. Pregnancy is the first case where some argue there is more than one — and even that framing is contested.
The clinical instinct to “override” a refusing pregnant patient has a long history of going badly for both the patient and the future baby the override was meant to protect.
Today’s job is to learn a framework precise enough to distinguish a refusal we should accept, a refusal we should keep trying to persuade against, and a refusal the law may override.

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
text:
Today

By the end of Part A, you will be able to:

State Mill’s harm principle and apply its standard test for justified paternalism to a contemporary drug-policy case.
Distinguish liberal autonomy from relational autonomy, and explain why pregnancy is the paradigm case where the distinction matters.
Describe what Ferguson v. City of Charleston (2001) and In re A.C. (1990) held, and what each refused to decide.
Explain why pregnant women were historically excluded from clinical trials, and why the 2018 PRGLAC report reframed that exclusion as itself a harm.
Apply the framework to one substance-use scenario you will see again in clinical practice.

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
text:
Liberal Autonomy: One-Slide Recap
The standard model of autonomy rests on a handful of claims that are easy to state and hard to dislodge.

The patient is the author of her own care.
Valid informed consent requires disclosure, understanding, voluntariness, and capacity.
A competent adult may refuse medically recommended treatment, for any reason or no reason.
The clinician’s job in the face of a refusal is persuasion, not coercion.

That set has done enormous work since Nuremberg. It also, in a growing list of cases, runs out.

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
section: Where the Model Works: A Single Adult Patient
text:
Where the Model Works: A Single Adult Patient


A fifty-year-old man with stage IV pancreatic cancer is told he has a 40% one-year survival chance with aggressive chemotherapy. He declines; he prefers to spend his remaining time gardening. His oncologist tries twice to talk him into the protocol, then helps him enroll in hospice.
Nothing about that scene troubles anyone in the room. The standard model handles it perfectly.



What the standard model assumes:

One patient with one set of interests.
A choice that primarily affects the chooser.
A clinician who disagrees but defers.
A legal system that backs the deferral.

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
section: Where It Strains: The Pregnant Patient
text:
Where It Strains: The Pregnant Patient


What changes:

The body in front of you contains, on some accounts, a second locus of moral concern.
The choice the patient makes can — depending on the case — be argued to affect a future person who cannot yet refuse on their own behalf.
The clinician’s professional norms about non-maleficence may pull in two directions at once.




What does not change (according to the modern professional consensus):

The pregnant person remains the patient with the right to refuse.
Her competence is presumed unless specifically rebutted.
Her body is not a public resource for the protection of a future person.

The fight is over how to honor both lists at once.

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
text:
Paternalism, Defined
Paternalism is acting on someone’s behalf, against their stated preferences, for what one takes to be their own good. The metaphor is exact: a parent overriding a child for the child’s sake.
Almost no one thinks all paternalism is wrong. We accept it toward small children, toward people in psychotic crisis, toward an unconscious patient in the ED. The hard question is where it stops.
The framework below is not specific to pregnancy. It is what we need before the pregnancy-specific cases can be evaluated honestly.

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
text:
Mill’s Harm Principle

“The only purpose for which power can be rightfully exercised over any member of a civilised community, against his will, is to prevent harm to others. His own good, either physical or moral, is not a sufficient warrant.… Over himself, over his own body and mind, the individual is sovereign.”
— John Stuart Mill, On Liberty (1859), ch. 1

(Mill 1863)

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
text:
Soft vs. Hard Paternalism









Soft Paternalism
Hard Paternalism




What it overrides
Choices that are not fully voluntary — made under coercion, ignorance, incapacity
Choices that are fully voluntary, competent, and informed


Example
Detaining a delirious post-op patient who tries to leave the hospital pulling out her IV
Banning the sale of cigarettes to a competent adult who has read every warning label


Mill’s view
Permitted: not a real violation of autonomy, because the person isn’t really choosing
Not permitted: this is overriding sovereignty


Where it gets hard
Determining when a choice is “not voluntary enough”
Almost every legitimate public-health intervention sits in this zone

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
section: The Standard Test for Justified Paternalism
text:
The Standard Test for Justified Paternalism
Three questions, taken together, license a presumption against intervention.

Is the person competent — able to understand and weigh the relevant information?
Is the choice voluntary — free from coercion, manipulation, or material misinformation?
Does the harm fall on the chooser alone, or also on identifiable others?

If the answers are yes, yes, and chooser alone, the liberal answer is: leave the person be. Most of the real work is reasonable disagreement about each question.

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
text:
Prohibition, 1920–1933


The Eighteenth Amendment (1919) prohibited the manufacture, sale, and transportation of “intoxicating liquors” in the US; the Volstead Act gave it teeth. It was repealed in 1933 — the only constitutional amendment ever fully overturned.
In between, alcohol consumption fell briefly and climbed back, illegal manufacture became a major industry, federal prison populations roughly tripled, and the homicide rate hit a peak it would not match for sixty years.



Three lessons that recur in modern drug policy:

Banning a widely-used substance does not erase the demand.
The black market that replaces a legal market is more violent than the legal one it replaced.
Selective enforcement falls hardest on the communities with the least political power to push back.

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
section: The War on Drugs at Fifty
text:
The War on Drugs at Fifty








Indicator
1971 (Nixon declares “war on drugs”)
2023




Federal drug-control budget
~$100 million
~$40 billion


U.S. incarcerated population
~200,000
~1.9 million


Share of state prisoners held for drug offenses
<10%
~14%


Black–White disparity in drug-arrest rates
not systematically tracked
~2.5× higher arrest rate for Black Americans, despite near-identical use rates


U.S. overdose deaths (annual)
~7,000
~107,000



The numbers do not, by themselves, settle the policy question. They do force a specific question: at fifty years and forty billion dollars a year, what does the policy claim to have achieved?

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
section: Harm Reduction as a Third Frame
text:
Harm Reduction as a Third Frame
Harm reduction accepts that some people will continue using substances and tries to minimize the medical and social damage — without making abstinence a precondition for help.


Concrete examples:

Naloxone (Narcan) distribution to opioid users and those around them.
Syringe-services programs reducing HIV and hepatitis C transmission.
Methadone and buprenorphine for opioid use disorder during pregnancy.
Supervised consumption sites (legal in Canada; piloted in NYC since 2021).




The philosophical signature:
Harm reduction does not require resolving whether use is morally wrong. It asks a different question — what intervention, in this person, this week, reduces harm the most? That is soft paternalism in public-health clothes.

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
section: Thought Question — Should the State Ban Tobacco?
text:
Thought Question — Should the State Ban Tobacco?

Tobacco kills roughly 480,000 Americans a year — more than all illegal drugs combined. It is sold legally to anyone over twenty-one. The FDA has authority to cut nicotine to non-addictive levels and has proposed, but not implemented, doing so.
Write or discuss.

Apply the three-question test (competent? voluntary? harm to chooser alone?) to a thirty-year-old smoker.
What does secondhand smoke do to the third question, and what does that commit you to about other secondhand harms?
If you would not ban tobacco, can you coherently support criminal penalties for methamphetamine? Identify the premise you are relying on.

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
text:
Make It Contemporary


Three live policies that turn on these questions:

The FDA’s proposed menthol cigarette ban, delayed in 2024 over concerns about disparate enforcement.
State and federal restrictions on flavored e-cigarettes, aimed at adolescent uptake.
Australia’s prescription-only nicotine model for vaping, in force since 2024.




What each is doing under the hood:

Menthol is about secondhand harms — uptake patterns in communities with less choice.
Vape restrictions are about competence — the choice is made by adolescents whose voluntariness is disputed.
Australia is soft paternalism dressed as a clinical pathway: you can have it, you just have to go through a doctor.

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
text:
After You’ve Discussed






Note


A distinction worth carrying into the pregnancy block.
Deterrence-of-care is the failure mode where an enforcement policy causes the people it claims to protect to avoid the system protection was supposed to flow through.
When tobacco enforcement targets the user, smokers do not stop smoking; they hide it from their doctor. When drug enforcement reaches into pregnancy, women do not stop using; they avoid prenatal care. The next forty minutes is about how that pattern plays out when the patient is pregnant.

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
text:
Relational Autonomy — The Critique
Relational autonomy is the view, most associated with Susan Sherwin, that the liberal model describes an abstraction rather than a real person.
Real autonomy is exercised through relationships, social positions, economic conditions, and bodies that are never fully bounded. Treating autonomy as a property of an isolated chooser misses most of what shapes a real decision.
The view is not anti-autonomy. It is a richer account of what autonomy is.

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
section: Where Relational Autonomy Changes the Clinical Move
text:
Where Relational Autonomy Changes the Clinical Move


The liberal model would ask:

Does the patient understand the information?
Has she given valid consent?
Has she signed?

If yes, the encounter is essentially done. The clinician has discharged the duty.



Relational autonomy adds:

Who is in the room with her, and who is not?
Is the choice she is making available to her because of the conditions of her life, or despite them?
What changes about her choice if the conditions change?

The encounter ends not when the form is signed but when those questions are answered honestly.

(Sherwin 1992)

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
text:
Liberal vs. Relational Autonomy









Liberal Autonomy
Relational Autonomy




The self
Bounded, prior to relationships
Constituted through relationships


The interests
Fixed; the chooser brings them to the choice
Responsive; partly shaped by social conditions


The threat to autonomy
Coercion by an identifiable agent
Background conditions that narrow what choices are even available


The clinical implication
Document the consent and step back
Attend to the conditions under which the consent is being given


Where it shines
Refusal of unwanted treatment
Cases where the choice is shaped by poverty, racism, or domestic violence

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
text:
Why It Matters for Pregnancy
Pregnancy is the paradigm case where the liberal picture strains.

The chooser is bodily continuous, for nine months, with the entity her choice is partly about.
Most US pregnancies happen to women whose economic, social, and medical circumstances do real work in shaping what counts as a “free” choice.
Medicine has historically treated her as the gestational vehicle of a future patient — and her refusals as obstacles to be managed.

Relational autonomy does not tell us to override her. It tells us to take the conditions of her choice more seriously than the liberal model required.

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
text:
The Question, Stated Sharply

May the state criminalize, in a pregnant woman, conduct that — were she not pregnant — would be unprosecutable?

That is the question. Every case in this section turns on it. Notice that it presupposes nothing about the moral status of the fetus; it asks about the enforcement practice and what it does to the people it touches.

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
section: Ferguson v. City of Charleston (2001) — Facts
text:
Ferguson v. City of Charleston (2001) — Facts


In the late 1980s, MUSC in Charleston began a program of covert urine drug screening on selected pregnant patients in its prenatal clinic. Positive results were forwarded to police. The selection criteria — written by MUSC staff and approved by the local solicitor — included markers like “no prenatal care.”
Ten of the eleven women arrested under the program were Black. The eleventh was a white woman whose chart noted that she “lives with her boyfriend, who is a Negro.”



The legal question:
Were these searches Fourth Amendment searches requiring patient consent, or “special-needs” searches that an institution may conduct without consent because their primary purpose is public-health-related rather than law-enforcement-related?
The Supreme Court decided in 2001.

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
text:
Ferguson — Holding






Important


Held, 6–3 (Stevens, J.): When the immediate objective of the searches was to generate evidence for law enforcement, the special-needs exception did not apply. The covert drug-testing program violated the Fourth Amendment.
What the Court did not decide: Whether drug testing tied to treatment referral — with no police involvement — would be permissible. Whether the prosecutions themselves were lawful (a separate state-law question). Whether civil consequences short of arrest (custody removal, civil commitment) raised the same constitutional concern.



(Supreme Court of the United States 2001)

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
text:
Wisconsin Act 292 — The “Cocaine Mom” Law

Wisconsin Act 292 (1997). Permits civil detention of a pregnant woman if a court finds probable cause she has used a controlled substance “to a severe degree” and is unwilling to accept treatment. She has no right to appointed counsel until forty-eight hours in. The fetus, by contrast, is appointed a guardian ad litem at the outset.
The statute remains in force. The most recent Seventh Circuit ruling (2023) sent the case back without resolving the constitutional question.

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
text:
Alabama’s Chemical Endangerment Statute
Alabama’s chemical-endangerment law was enacted in 2006 to punish adults who exposed children to the toxic chemicals of clandestine meth production. Within five years, prosecutors were using it to charge pregnant women whose newborns tested positive for any controlled substance — reading the unborn child as the protected “child.”
The Alabama Supreme Court affirmed this reading in Ex parte Ankrom (2013). Prosecutions accelerated. Between 2006 and 2022, several hundred women — disproportionately poor and rural — were convicted, with sentences from probation to ten years.
The statute is identical in form to the one used against meth-lab operators. Its application to pregnant women was a prosecutorial choice, not a legislative one.

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
section: Tennessee’s Fetal Assault Law — Rise and Fall
text:
Tennessee’s Fetal Assault Law — Rise and Fall


2014. Tennessee enacts Public Chapter 820, making it a Class A misdemeanor for a woman to take a narcotic during pregnancy “if her child is born addicted to or harmed by the narcotic.”
2014–16. Roughly one hundred women are arrested. Concentration is in poor, rural counties. Drug-use rates in pregnancy do not decline. Women report avoiding prenatal care; some give birth at home, unattended, to escape mandatory hospital testing.



2016. The statute is allowed to sunset; the legislature declines to renew it.
The empirical aftermath. Faherty et al. (2019), examining state-level data nationwide, found that states with punitive policies toward substance use in pregnancy had higher rates of neonatal abstinence syndrome — exactly the outcome the policies were sold as preventing.
The deterrence-of-care concept from earlier in the lecture has a body count.

(Faherty et al. 2019)

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
text:
The Scale


Paltrow and Flavin documented 1,396 arrests and forced interventions on pregnant women in the US between 1973 and 2005 — a landmark 2013 study in JHPPL. Pregnancy Justice has continued the tracking; the pace has accelerated post-Dobbs.
Of the documented cases:

71% of the women were low-income.
59% were women of color.
The most common charge was drug-related; the second was refusal of care.

Dorothy Roberts’s Killing the Black Body (1997) is the canonical analysis of why these patterns track race and class.



Read this number carefully.
1,396 is not the number of pregnant women who used drugs. It is the number who were prosecuted, detained, or forced into a medical intervention by the state.

(Paltrow and Flavin 2013; Roberts 1997)

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
text:
Brittney Poolaw, Oklahoma, 2021

In October 2021, a Comanche County jury convicted Brittney Poolaw, twenty-one, of first-degree manslaughter for a pregnancy loss at approximately seventeen weeks. The medical examiner could not identify a cause; meth and amphetamine metabolites were detected in fetal tissue at levels the toxicologist himself testified were not clearly causal. Pregnancy loss at this stage occurs on its own in roughly twenty percent of cases. Poolaw was sentenced to four years.
— Pregnancy Justice case summary; State v. Poolaw, Comanche Co. CF-2020-114

The conviction was affirmed on appeal in 2023.

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
section: ACOG’s Position — And Its Empirical Backing
text:
ACOG’s Position — And Its Empirical Backing


ACOG Committee Opinion 473 / 664:

Substance use in pregnancy is “a medical condition that should be managed in collaboration with the patient.”
Criminal sanctions and forced detention are “not effective in reducing the incidence of alcohol or drug abuse” and are “contrary to the welfare of the mother and the fetus.”
Mandatory testing without consent is rejected. Mandatory reporting to child-protective services is “controversial” and depends on state law.




Faherty et al. (2019), JAMA Network Open:
State-level analysis of 4.5 million births. States with punitive policies (criminalization, civil commitment, or mandatory reporting as abuse) had a 27% higher rate of neonatal abstinence syndrome than states without.
ACOG’s normative claim and the population-level data point the same way. That convergence is uncommon enough to matter.

(American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists 2011, 2016; Faherty et al. 2019)

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
section: Thought Question — A Patient on Methamphetamine
text:
Thought Question — A Patient on Methamphetamine

You are the L&D charge nurse on a Tuesday night. A 32-week-pregnant patient arrives in early labor. Her urine tox is positive for methamphetamine. She has had three prenatal visits, no other complications, and tells you she used “yesterday, maybe the day before.” Your state has mandatory reporting of in-utero drug exposure to CPS; the hospital has discretion about police involvement.
Write or discuss.

What do you tell her about what will happen next? What do you not yet tell her?
Your charge physician asks whether you think CPS should be called now or after delivery. What’s your answer, and what is it grounded in?
Does your answer change if the substance is alcohol? If it is fentanyl? If it is cannabis legal in your state? Be honest about why.

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
text:
Make It Contemporary


Three live trends, 2022–2025:

Pregnancy Justice has documented a sharp post-Dobbs acceleration in arrests for pregnancy outcomes — from roughly fifty per year nationally to more than two hundred in 2023.
At least nine states have introduced fetal-personhood bills since 2022 that would expand the statutory basis for charges like Poolaw’s.
The Alabama Supreme Court’s LePage ruling (2024) declaring frozen embryos to be “extrauterine children” — discussed at length in Part B — is the most aggressive recent extension of the same legal logic.




What unifies these:
Each treats the fetus or embryo as a legal subject with claims that constrain what the pregnant person (or the patient seeking IVF) may do.
The professional medical position has not changed. The legal environment in which it operates has changed sharply.

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
text:
After You’ve Discussed






Note


A distinction the body of the lecture has been circling.
When a pregnant patient and her team disagree, there are three different kinds of disagreement that call for different responses:

About the facts. (“That medication will harm my fetus.” It won’t, or it will less than the disease.) The remedy is persuasion and better information.
About the values. (“Even though the medication is safer, I want to take my chances.”) The remedy is respect for refusal.
About whose interests count. (“Your refusal will harm your fetus, who cannot speak.”) This is where the litigation lives — and where ACOG’s modern position lands: fetal interests exist, but coercion of the patient is almost never the right response to them.

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
section: When the Doctor Says Yes and the Patient Says No
text:
When the Doctor Says Yes and the Patient Says No
Three families of cases recur. They look superficially similar and are ethically and legally different.

Refusal of cesarean. The clinician believes a section is indicated; the patient prefers vaginal delivery, or no delivery at all.
Refusal of transfusion. Most commonly a Jehovah’s Witness with peripartum hemorrhage; the standard model honors the refusal; pregnancy has historically been used to override it.
Refusal of management for ruptured membranes or previa. Bedrest, hospitalization, cerclage — burdens almost entirely on the patient, benefits statistical and indirect.

The foundational case for all three is In re A.C. (1990).

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
text:
In re A.C. (1990) — Angela Carder


Angela Carder was twenty-seven, twenty-six weeks pregnant, and dying of metastatic cancer when she was admitted to George Washington University Hospital in 1987. Her oncologist had treated her since adolescence. The agreed plan was palliation.
Hospital administrators sought a court order for a non-consensual cesarean, on the grounds that the fetus might be viable. The hearing was convened in the hospital corridor. The order was granted. The cesarean was performed. The infant lived two hours. Carder died two days later. The cesarean was listed on her death certificate as a contributing cause.



The case the appellate court eventually decided was not whether the trial judge had been merciful or cruel.
It was whether the framework the judge had used — balancing the patient’s right to refuse against the state’s interest in potential life — was a framework the law would tolerate going forward.
The DC Court of Appeals, sitting en banc, answered that question almost two and a half years later.

(District of Columbia Court of Appeals 1990)

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
text:
The 1990 Appellate Repudiation






Important


Held (DC Court of Appeals, en banc, April 1990): “In virtually all cases the question of what is to be done is to be decided by the patient — the pregnant woman — on behalf of herself and the fetus. The trial court erred in subordinating A.C.’s right of bodily integrity in favor of the state’s interest in potential life.”
The court did not decide what should happen in a hypothetical case where a pregnant patient was incompetent and the fetus highly viable — but it set the bar for that case so high that, in the thirty-five years since, no American appellate court has affirmed a similar order.

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
text:
ACOG Committee Opinion 664

“Pregnancy is not an exception to the principle that a decisionally capable patient has the right to refuse treatment, even treatment needed to maintain life. … Coercive and punitive legal approaches to pregnant women who refuse medical advice fail to recognize that all competent adults are entitled to informed consent and bodily integrity.”

— ACOG, Committee Opinion 664 (2016, reaffirmed 2022) (American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists 2016)
The opinion has the additional pedagogical virtue of being short. Most students can read it in fifteen minutes; it is the cleanest single statement of the modern professional consensus.

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
text:
Burton v. State (Florida, 2009–2010)
If A.C. feels like a 1980s artifact, Samantha Burton shows it is not.
In 2009, Burton was twenty-five weeks pregnant when her membranes ruptured. Her physician at Tallahassee Memorial recommended hospitalization and bedrest. Burton — two other children at home, no childcare, a job she could not afford to lose — asked to be discharged for a second opinion. The hospital obtained a court order by telephone compelling her to remain. She stayed three days against her will; her fetus was delivered stillborn by emergency cesarean.
The Florida District Court of Appeal vacated the order in 2010, holding that even the state’s interest in potential life “cannot override Burton’s right to make her own medical treatment decisions.” (District Court of Appeal of Florida, First District 2010)

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
section: Thought Question — A Refused Cesarean at Thirty-Eight Weeks
text:
Thought Question — A Refused Cesarean at Thirty-Eight Weeks

A thirty-eight-week-pregnant patient with a history of one prior low-transverse cesarean is in labor. Her obstetrician recommends a repeat cesarean on the grounds of an estimated fetal weight of 4,300 grams and a non-reassuring tracing for the last forty minutes. The patient — competent, oriented, and afraid of surgery — refuses, and asks to continue laboring.
You are the labor nurse. The obstetrician has just asked you to “help her understand.”
What is your script for the next ten minutes? What are you not willing to do, and what would you escalate?

title: Lecture 5 — Reproductive Ethics
text:
Marlise Muñoz, Fort Worth, 2013–14


On November 26, 2013, Marlise Muñoz collapsed in her kitchen from what was later determined to be a pulmonary embolism. She was 33 and fourteen weeks pregnant. Paramedics restored cardiac activity en route to John Peter Smith Hospital. By morning, she met criteria for whole-brain death.
Her husband Erick — a paramedic — and her parents requested withdrawal of support, consistent with her previously expressed wishes.
