title: Dax Cowart and the Right to Refuse Life-Saving Treatment: Autonomy, Beneficence, and Paternalism
text:
Who: Donald Herbert “Dax” Cowart (1947–2019), a 25-year-old former Air Force pilot from Henderson, Texas.
What: Burned over roughly two-thirds of his body in a 1973 propane explosion. He repeatedly, and competently, demanded that treatment stop, and was overruled for more than a year.
Where / When: Parkland Memorial Hospital (Dallas), a rehabilitation hospital in Houston, and John Sealy Hospital at the University of Texas Medical Branch (Galveston), 1973–1974.
Why it matters: Cowart’s case became the most-discussed American example of a clash between patient autonomy (a competent person’s right to refuse treatment) and clinical beneficence (the duty to act for the patient’s good). Even after a full recovery and a successful career, he insisted his refusal should have been honored. The argument he started has run for fifty years and is not over.
Pair with: History & the Clinical Relationship

title: Dax Cowart and the Right to Refuse Life-Saving Treatment: Autonomy, Beneficence, and Paternalism
text:
The Case
On the afternoon of July 25, 1973, Dax Cowart and his father, Ray, drove out to look at a piece of rural land in east Texas. Dax — then named Donald, a name he would later change — was twenty-five. He was a former high-school football captain, a rodeo rider, and an Air Force pilot recently back from service. By every account, including his own, he was a young man who took his strong, fast, capable body for granted.
They did not know that an underground propane pipeline near where they parked had been leaking. The gas had pooled, invisible and odorless, in the low ground around the car. When Ray turned the ignition to leave, a spark from the engine ignited it. The explosion threw Dax clear of a wall of fire. He ran through pine woods until he could not run anymore, beating at flames with hands that were already destroyed.
A farmer reached him first. Cowart’s first recorded words, to that stranger, were a request for a weapon: “Get me a gun. Can’t you see I’m a dead man. I’m going to die anyway.” Both men were taken to a small hospital in Kilgore, then transferred together — 140 miles, in the same ambulance — to the burn unit at Parkland Memorial Hospital in Dallas. Ray Cowart died on the way. Dax survived. The damage was severe:

Burns covered about 65% of his body.
The deepest, third-degree burns destroyed the skin of his face, ears, and hands.
He was blinded in both eyes.
Most of his fingers were later amputated.
He was conscious, and he stayed conscious.

What followed was fourteen months of treatment that Cowart would later describe, without exaggeration, as “pure hell.” The medical necessity was real.

Why the treatment was unavoidable. Burn depth is graded by layer:

First-degree burns affect only the outer skin (an ordinary sunburn).
Second-degree burns blister and are intensely painful.
Third-degree (full-thickness) burns destroy all skin layers.

The amount burned is reported as percent of total body surface area (TBSA). At ~65% TBSA, much of it third-degree, Cowart was at the edge of what was survivable in 1973. Survival required debridement — the physical removal of dead tissue, because new skin cannot grow over it and dead tissue breeds lethal infection. This was often done in a Hubbard tank, a steel pool of warm antiseptic water that softened the dead skin so it could be scrubbed and cut away. The procedure works. Before modern pain control, it was also one of the most agonizing routine treatments in medicine.

From the start — at Parkland, and at every stage afterward — Cowart asked the people treating him to stop. Not in passing, and not only when the pain was worst. He asked his doctors, his nurses, and his mother, again and again, to end treatment and let him die. He understood what he was asking for. He understood that without the tankings and skin grafts he would die of infection within weeks. He wanted that outcome over the one being forced on him. He felt, he said, that he was “being skinned alive.”
A psychiatrist, Dr. Robert B. White, was brought in to evaluate him at Galveston. The implied hope was that White would find Cowart too depressed or too overwhelmed to be making a real decision. That would have authorized treating him against his will with a clear conscience. White concluded the opposite. Cowart, he found, was “not in the least incompetent or mentally ill.” It was during this period that White recorded the interview that became the 1974 film Please Let Me Die — a competent man, on camera, lucidly asking to be allowed to die, and being treated anyway (White 1974).
The most revealing turn in the story is one the standard retellings often skip. After about eight months at Parkland, Cowart was moved to a rehabilitation hospital in Houston. There he again refused further treatment — and the rehabilitation physician accepted the refusal. For a moment, the system did what Cowart asked. Then his mother, Ada, his legal next of kin, and his attorney intervened. They had him transferred to the burn unit at John Sealy Hospital in Galveston, where treatment — and the tankings — resumed under the unit’s director, Dr. Duane Larson. The decisive fact of the case is not that no one would listen. Someone did. He was moved away from the person who listened.
He survived, was discharged blind and severely disabled, and was, by his own description, deeply depressed for years. He attempted suicide more than once. Then his life changed. He earned a law degree from Texas Tech University in 1986, married, practiced law, and became one of the most articulate patient-rights advocates in the country. He died on April 28, 2019, at seventy-one, of complications from leukemia and liver cancer.
For the rest of that life he said two things that many listeners could not hold together. He was glad to be alive. And his physicians had been wrong to keep him alive against his will. He did not think these statements contradicted each other. The good outcome, he insisted, was never the question. The question was whose decision it had been — and that question became one of the longest-running arguments in modern bioethics.

title: Dax Cowart and the Right to Refuse Life-Saving Treatment: Autonomy, Beneficence, and Paternalism
text:
The Argument Dax Started
What makes Cowart’s case central to bioethics is not the horror of the burns. It is the quality of the disagreement it produced. Over fifty years, Cowart, his physicians, and a series of philosophers and lawyers built an argument, attacked it, repaired it, and attacked the repair. The pieces below trace that exchange as a connected dialogue. Each move answers the one before.

The argument from autonomy
Cowart’s own position is the cleanest. He insisted the issue was control over his body, not the value of the result: “the right to control your own body is a right you’re born with, not something that you have to ask anyone else for, not the government, not your treating physician, not your next-of-kin” (Cowart and Burt 1998).

The Autonomy Argument

A competent adult has a moral right to refuse any medical treatment.
Dax Cowart was a competent adult who refused treatment.
Therefore, treating Cowart over his objection violated his moral rights.


Premise 1 is now close to settled doctrine. Courts and ethicists broadly accept that bodily self-determination includes the right to decline care. So the argument’s whole weight falls on premise 2 — and that is exactly where his physicians attacked it.

Capacity, competence, and the surrogate. Decision-making capacity is a clinical judgment that a patient can do four things:

Understand the relevant information.
Appreciate how it applies to them.
Reason about the options.
Communicate a choice.

Capacity is decision-specific and time-specific. “Competence” is the parallel legal term. When a patient lacks capacity, a surrogate (often next of kin) decides for them — but a surrogate has no authority to override a patient who still has capacity. Two psychiatrists found Cowart had capacity. His mother’s authorization was nonetheless treated as controlling. That is the legal pressure point premise 2 exposes.



The beneficence reply: pain, shock, and “they almost always change their minds”
The physicians did not deny premise 1. They denied premise 2. Their claim was not that Cowart could not talk coherently. It was that a person in catastrophic pain, in shock, facing a future he could not yet imagine, is not making a settled, autonomous choice. He is crying out from an unbearable present. Dr. Larson, on the film Dax’s Case, put both the conviction and its limit in one breath: “No other burn patient has made it so clear … that they really do desire to die. … This is an ongoing battle that we have to individualize.”

The Beneficence / Temporary-Incapacity Reply

If a patient is in extreme pain and shock, their capacity to refuse treatment is undermined.
Dax Cowart was in extreme pain and shock.
Therefore, Dax Cowart lacked the capacity to refuse treatment.


This is essentially the position the philosopher H. Tristram Engelhardt later defended in the Dax’s Case essays. He called it a genuine conflict “at the roots of health care” between freedom and best interest (Kliever 1989). The reply also has empirical backing that did not exist when commentators first took Cowart’s side:

A 1978 NIH consensus statement held that shock makes early decision-making nearly impossible for burn victims.
In a later study, burn survivors interviewed years afterward said almost uniformly that informed consent was unrealistic at the moment of injury.
They said the capacity to consent developed over time.
None thought withdrawing support would have been right for them (Brewster et al. 2006).


Informed consent vs. informed refusal — and how new it was. Informed consent — disclosure plus free agreement before treatment — was barely a decade old in law in 1973. Canterbury v. Spence, which set the modern “reasonable patient” disclosure standard, had been decided only the year before (United States Court of Appeals, District of Columbia Circuit 1972). The harder idea, informed refusal — a competent patient’s right to decline life-saving care — was almost unrecognized. Many physicians trained in an older paternalistic model treated refusal of life-saving treatment as itself evidence of impaired judgment. That is precisely the reasoning premise 1 of the reply formalizes (see Faden and Beauchamp (1986)).

But the reply has a soft spot. Premise 2 is a statistical generalization — most patients are glad later — used to override this patient. Applying an average to the individual in front of you is a recognizably utilitarian move: maximize good outcomes across the class of burn patients. It invites the objection Cowart spent his life pressing.


Cowart’s counter: a good outcome doesn’t make the choice yours
Cowart’s reply is not “you were wrong about the outcome.” He concedes the outcome. His reply is that a good result does not retroactively transfer the decision to the people who made it.

The Retrospective-Endorsement Objection

The right to make a decision does not depend on the outcome of that decision.
Pointing to a patient’s later gladness is an appeal to the outcome.
Therefore, a patient’s later gladness does not prove that the decision belonged to the medical team.


He sharpened it with an analogy: a surgeon who operates on the wrong patient is not vindicated because the patient happens to recover.
This is where a concept from ethical theory does real work. Cowart’s two claims — I am glad to be alive and I was wronged — are not a contradiction. They are a textbook case of moral residue: even when overriding a duty is, on balance, defensible, the overridden duty does not vanish. Something wrongful remains to be acknowledged. As he said: “I do not feel grateful to anyone for fighting my request to die … no, I’m not glad they forcibly treated me because the pain that I went through was pure hell” (Cowart and Burt 1998). From a Kantian angle, the same point is that treating a competent person merely as a means to a future others judge worthwhile fails to respect him as a person who may author his own life — even badly.


Burt’s relational counter: “I won’t abandon you”
The legal scholar Robert Burt, who debated Cowart publicly for years, accepted almost all of this and still resisted the conclusion that the team should simply have complied. His move changes the frame from a transaction (consent given or withheld) to a relationship.

The Relational Counter

Desperate demands to die are often cries for sustained relationship rather than pure refusals.
If a demand is a cry for relationship, then immediately complying with it is a form of abandonment.
Therefore, immediately honoring a patient’s refusal to live can be a form of abandonment.


This is recognizably an ethics of care position: obligations grow out of relationship and dependency, not only out of rights. It is also where the dialogue gets its most famous phrase. Burt kept asking when comes the end of the day? He conceded that staying cannot be indefinite, because a relationship that never lets the patient’s “no” be final has quietly become the old paternalism in kinder language. Cowart’s answer was blunt: “an hour was an eternity, with the burns and the pain I was going through.” Burt’s honest reply was that he did not always know. That unresolved gap is what the next move tries to close.


The Arnold–Menzel procedure: turning principles into a process
Denis Arnold and Paul Menzel took the Burt–Cowart standoff seriously as an unanswered question: when, exactly, does resistance to a competent refusal have to yield? They proposed a step-by-step procedure to answer it without smuggling paternalism back in. Their guiding question reframes the whole debate away from absolute rights. Not “who holds the trump card?” but “Whose pain is it, anyway?” — a question, they write, about who may decide that agonizing pain now is worth a possible good life later (Arnold and Menzel 1998).
The procedure is, in the vocabulary of principlism, an exercise in specification and balancing. It takes two abstract principles that conflict in the abstract — respect for autonomy and beneficence — and turns them into a concrete sequence a real clinical team can follow at the bedside. The decisive split is whether the patient’s pain is being adequately controlled. Uncontrolled pain both clouds judgment and is itself a harm the patient is being forced to endure. Read the diagram as a single question that forks: first fix the pain or honor a short waiting period. Either way, the patient — not the team and not the family — ends up holding the decision.








G



start

Competent patient refuses
life-sustaining treatment



pain

Is severe pain being
adequately managed?



start->pain





prog

Give an honest (not inflated)
prognosis; acknowledge how hard
it is to believe in recovery



pain->prog


  yes



manage

Set an explicit maximum time
to get pain under control;
defer more to the patient



pain->manage


  no



date

Patient and team set an
agreed decision date;
appoint a patient advocate



prog->date





hold

Refusal may be reasonably
declined for a short period
(about a week)



date->hold





honor

The competent patient
controls the decision;
the refusal is honored



manage->honor


  time elapses,
  pain still uncontrolled



hold->honor


  insistence continues
  & advocate concurs






Figure 1: The Arnold–Menzel “end of the day” procedure. Burt asked when a care team must finally stop resisting a competent patient’s refusal; Arnold and Menzel answer with a process rather than a fixed deadline. The pivotal question (gold diamond) is whether severe pain is controlled. If it is, the team owes the patient an honest prognosis, an agreed decision date, and an advocate, and may decline the refusal only for a short period (about a week). If pain is not controlled, the team may hold only long enough to set and pursue an explicit pain-control deadline. On both paths the endpoint (green) is the same: the competent patient controls the decision. Adapted from Arnold & Menzel (1998).







The argument that hasn’t ended: the empirical turn
Cowart’s case itself never produced a court ruling. The legal architecture grew up around it. Two cases were decisive:

In re Quinlan (1976) recognized that life-sustaining treatment could be withdrawn through a surrogate (Supreme Court of New Jersey 1976).
Cruzan v. Director (1990) treated the refusal of such treatment as a protected liberty, while letting states demand clear evidence of the patient’s wishes (Supreme Court of the United States 1990).

Out of that lineage came living wills, durable powers of attorney, and the federal Patient Self-Determination Act. The law moved Cowart’s way. The bedside did not always follow. In a 2019 remembrance, the bioethicist Greg Pence reported a burn physician saying, of patients who ask to die, “They all want to die, and I just ignore them. I don’t let them give up on themselves” (Parsi and Winslade 2019). Advance directives, when they exist, are quietly overridden a large share of the time. The doctrine settled. The disagreement did not.
If the story stopped with Arnold and Menzel, the lesson would still look tidy: respect autonomy, control pain, hand the patient the decision. The most important recent move complicates exactly that lesson — and it comes not from law but from data. The bioethicist Monica Gerrek argues that building burn ethics almost entirely on Cowart has spread a belief — that burn units are inherently paternalistic — that the wider evidence does not support (Gerrek 2018). She sets beside Dax the case of Andrea Rubin, severely burned in 2014 with comparable injuries. The two cases ran in parallel:

Rubin’s friends, like Dax’s, pleaded that she would not want to live this way.
Her father, like Dax’s mother, consented to treatment.
She was sedated for weeks and could not decide.
After recovery, Rubin did not feel betrayed. She believes she genuinely lacked capacity early on, that the team acted in her interest, and that honoring her friends’ pleas would have been a fatal mistake.

Rubin’s case does not refute Cowart. Two psychiatrists found he had capacity, and hers is also just one story. What it does is reopen premise 2 of the original autonomy argument as a genuinely empirical question that cannot be settled from a single famous case. Capacity in catastrophic injury is harder to read, and more variable across people, than either “burn units are tyrannical” or “patients always come around” allows. Cowart himself, asked how a physician could ever be sure a patient truly wants to die, conceded: “I doubt that there is any way a physician can be absolutely sure.” Fifty years on, that is roughly where the argument rests — not at an answer, but at a sharper question, with Cowart’s autonomy claim intact and the certainty once used against it gone on both sides.

title: Dax Cowart and the Right to Refuse Life-Saving Treatment: Autonomy, Beneficence, and Paternalism
text:
Discussion Questions

Write out The Autonomy Argument and The Beneficence Reply in your own words — premises first, then the conclusion. The two arguments disagree about one thing: whether Cowart was competent to refuse during his treatment. Then say what kind of evidence — clinical, philosophical, or statistical — could actually settle that question.
After he recovered, Cowart said two things: he was glad to be alive, and his rights had been violated. The idea of moral residue says a decision can turn out fine and still leave a moral debt behind. Use that idea to explain why his two statements are not a contradiction. What does the idea tell us about paternalistic decisions that “turned out fine”?
The Beneficence Reply uses a generalization about a group — “most patients in Cowart’s situation are later glad they were treated” — to override one person’s choice. When, if ever, is it fair to apply a true generalization about a group to the specific person in front of you? What principle are you relying on?
You are the nurse assigned to Cowart’s daily tank treatment. He has just told you again that he wants it to stop and wants to die. What do you owe him in that moment that the institution does not? What, concretely, do you do?
Apply the Arnold–Menzel procedure to Andrea Rubin’s case. Does the procedure give the answer Rubin would endorse? If not, what does the mismatch tell you — is the procedure flawed, is Rubin’s case importantly different from Cowart’s, or is the argument Cowart started still open?

title: Dax Cowart and the Right to Refuse Life-Saving Treatment: Autonomy, Beneficence, and Paternalism

title: Sylvain Lesné and the Aβ*56 Affair: Research Misconduct, Replication, and the Amyloid Hypothesis
text:
Who: Sylvain Lesné (b. 1974), neuroscientist at the University of Minnesota; Matthew Schrag (b. ~1984), Vanderbilt neurologist and image sleuth; Karen Ashe, Lesné’s senior collaborator and a founding figure in mouse models of Alzheimer’s disease.
What: A 2006 paper in Nature claimed to have isolated a form of amyloid-beta — “Aβ56” — that caused memory loss in rats. The paper anchored the leading theory of Alzheimer’s for sixteen years. In 2022, a whistleblower published evidence that dozens of Western blot images in Lesné’s papers had been digitally faked. Nature* retracted the paper in 2024.
Where / When: University of Minnesota; Vanderbilt University; published in Science July 2022; retraction June 2024; Lesné resigned February 2025.
Why it matters: The case sits at the crossroads of research misconduct, the replication crisis, and subject protection. If a basic preclinical result is faked, every drug trial built on it enrolls human volunteers under false clinical equipoise — and the field’s billions in public funding rest on a mirage.
Pair with: Research Ethics (Protecting Research Subjects)

title: Sylvain Lesné and the Aβ*56 Affair: Research Misconduct, Replication, and the Amyloid Hypothesis
text:
The Case
In August 2021, Matthew Schrag got a phone call. He was a junior neurologist at Vanderbilt, thirty-seven years old, soft-spoken, with a small sign on his desk reading “Everything is figureoutable.” A colleague wanted to connect him with an attorney. The attorney represented two short-sellers — investors betting that a small biotech, Cassava Sciences, would lose value. Cassava was developing an Alzheimer’s drug called Simufilam. The short-sellers thought the underlying research was fake. Would Schrag, who had already criticized the FDA’s approval of another anti-amyloid drug, agree to examine the published images? He said yes. Over the next months, the attorney paid him about $18,000 (Piller 2022).
What began as a paid side job became something larger. Schrag’s method was unglamorous. He downloaded published figures, zoomed in, and looked for telltale signs that an image had been built in Photoshop rather than captured from a real experiment:

Patches of pixels repeated in places they should not be.
Bands that looked identical when they should be unique.
Edges too clean to come from a working gel.

His targets were Western blots. A Western blot uses electricity to drag protein samples through a gel, separating them by size into stacked bands that are stained and photographed. Two blots from the same true experiment can never look pixel-identical. If they do, someone copied and pasted.

Western blot. A Western blot detects a specific protein in a tissue or fluid sample. The sample runs through a gel that separates proteins by size. The proteins are then moved to a membrane and tagged with antibodies that bind only to the target protein. The result is a photograph of dark bands at certain positions — a visual fingerprint. The exact pattern of background noise and band shape is random, so two real blots should never match.

While studying Cassava, Schrag noticed something far bigger. The same patterns of image trouble appeared in an older and much more famous paper: a 2006 article in Nature, first-authored by Sylvain Lesné. Lesné was then a postdoc at the University of Minnesota, working with the well-known Alzheimer’s scientist Karen Ashe (Lesné et al. 2006). The paper reported the isolation of a specific form of amyloid-beta. The authors called it Aβ56 (pronounced “A-beta star fifty-six”). They claimed that injecting purified Aβ56 into young rats made them fail memory tests they had passed before. In the language of biomedical research, this was a smoking gun. It was a direct claim that a particular amyloid species causes dementia, not merely tracks it.

The amyloid hypothesis. Since the early 1990s, the leading theory of Alzheimer’s has held that sticky protein deposits called amyloid-beta plaques in the brain are a main cause of memory loss. The hypothesis has shaped drug development for thirty years. Hundreds of clinical trials have tested antibodies and small molecules meant to prevent, dissolve, or clear amyloid. Most have failed. The few approved drugs — aducanumab (Aduhelm), lecanemab, donanemab — produce small, disputed benefits and can cause brain swelling or bleeding.

The 2006 paper was cited more than 2,300 times. It launched a generation of follow-up studies. It helped Lesné win his own NIH-funded lab at Minnesota in 2009. And, according to Schrag, dozens of the Western blots in it and in Lesné’s later papers showed signs of digital editing: bands repeated where they should be unique, backgrounds spliced together, edges that looked drawn rather than photographed (Piller 2022).
Schrag was careful. He refused to use the word “fraud” in his public critiques. He could not prove intent. He could not see the original, unedited image files. “I focus on what we can see in the published images, and describe them as red flags, not final conclusions,” he told the journalist Charles Piller. “The data should speak for itself.” But when Science sent his findings to top independent image analysts and senior Alzheimer’s researchers, they agreed with him. The reviewers included:

Elisabeth Bik, a microbiologist and the world’s best-known forensic image consultant.
Donna Wilcock, an Alzheimer’s researcher at the University of Kentucky, who called some of the images “shockingly blatant.”
Thomas Südhof, a Nobel laureate at Stanford.

Bik concluded that the authors “appeared to have composed figures by piecing together parts of photos from different experiments… The obtained experimental results might not have been the desired results, and that data might have been changed to better fit a hypothesis” (Piller 2022).
Piller’s investigation appeared in Science in July 2022 under the title “Blots on a Field?”. It named Schrag publicly for the first time. The fallout came in stages:

Nature issued an expression of concern within days of the article.
In June 2024, the editors and most of the original authors agreed to retract the 2006 paper.
In February 2025, the University of Minnesota concluded its inquiry. It found that Lesné had committed research misconduct, and he resigned his faculty post.

The deeper damage is harder to count. The U.S. National Institutes of Health spent roughly $1.6 billion on amyloid-related Alzheimer’s research in fiscal year 2022 alone — about half the federal Alzheimer’s budget. Südhof’s verdict was blunt: “The immediate, obvious damage is wasted NIH funding and wasted thinking in the field because people are using these results as a starting point for their own experiments” (Piller 2022).
The Aβ*56 affair turned out not to be alone. Over the next three years, Piller and his collaborators documented apparent image manipulation in the work of several other senior figures:

Eliezer Masliah, hired in 2016 to lead the National Institute on Aging’s expanded Alzheimer’s program.
Berislav Zlokovic, a stroke researcher at USC whose work supported a federally funded trial that was later suspended.
Marc Tessier-Lavigne, the president of Stanford University, who resigned in 2023 after a student journalist found altered images in papers from his lab.

Across forty-six leading Alzheimer’s researchers, imaging experts flagged nearly six hundred suspect papers. Those papers had collectively been cited some 80,000 times (Piller 2025).
The question the Aβ*56 affair raises is not only whether Sylvain Lesné faked data. That question is now largely settled. The harder question is what it means for a field, and for the human volunteers downstream of it, when a basic result turns out to have been an “elaborate mirage.”

title: Sylvain Lesné and the Aβ*56 Affair: Research Misconduct, Replication, and the Amyloid Hypothesis
text:
The Argument Aβ*56 Started
The Lesné affair is interesting less for the misconduct itself than for the quality of the disagreement it produced. The disagreement is about what counts as harm, what counts as evidence, and what a medical research enterprise owes the people who enroll in its trials. What follows is a connected exchange. Each move answers the last.

Schrag’s argument: fraud in preclinical research is a research-ethics violation
Schrag’s public framing is careful, but it contains a sharper ethical claim than his prose makes obvious. He worried from the start that volunteers in Cassava’s Simufilam trials “faced risks of side effects with no chance of benefit.” That worry generalizes. If the preclinical foundation of a drug-development program is faked, the trials built on it cannot meet clinical equipoise. Equipoise is the rule that a community of skilled clinicians must be in honest, good-faith doubt about whether the experimental treatment beats the alternative. Equipoise is what makes it acceptable to expose trial subjects to risk. Without it, the trial is not research. It is human experimentation in the older, condemned sense.

Schrag’s Argument

A clinical trial is ethically permissible only if there is genuine community equipoise about whether the experimental intervention will benefit participants.
Equipoise requires that the preclinical evidence supporting the intervention be honestly produced.
Several anti-amyloid drug programs rely, directly or by citation chain, on preclinical results that appear to have been faked.
Therefore, those trials enroll participants under a false equipoise and violate the conditions for ethical research.


The disputed premise is the second one. Does equipoise depend on the honesty of the preclinical evidence in particular, or on the honesty of the broader scientific community at any given moment? If the field still has other good reasons to believe amyloid matters, perhaps a single faked study does not poison the well. That is where the main reply starts.


The principal reply: science is robust to individual fraud
A defender of the amyloid hypothesis can grant most of Schrag’s facts and still resist his conclusion. The hypothesis does not stand or fall with Aβ*56. The link between amyloid and Alzheimer’s was established long before 2006. Three independent lines of evidence pointed at it:

Pathology. Amyloid plaques were identified in Alois Alzheimer’s original 1906 case.
Genetics. The amyloid-precursor-protein gene was tied to inherited Alzheimer’s in 1991.
Down syndrome. People with an extra copy of that gene develop early Alzheimer’s pathology with striking reliability.

Aβ*56 was one proposed mechanism among many. Losing it does not collapse the hypothesis.

The “Robustness” Reply

The ethics of a clinical trial depends on the overall state of evidence supporting the intervention, not the integrity of any single paper.
The amyloid hypothesis is supported by multiple independent lines of evidence (genetics, pathology, imaging) that do not depend on Lesné’s work.
Therefore, even if Lesné’s specific findings are faked, current anti-amyloid trials need not violate equipoise.


The reply’s soft spot is empirical. How much of the field’s confidence in amyloid actually traveled — by citation and by influence on grant decisions — through the Aβ*56 result and the broader network of suspect papers? If the answer is “most of it,” the reply is weak. If “very little,” it is strong. Piller’s team estimates that roughly six hundred questionable Alzheimer’s papers have been cited around 80,000 times (Piller 2025). That is not noise. It also does not, on its own, prove that the field has been bent.


The counter-reply: structural harm and the therapeutic misconception
The counter-reply does not argue that the field has been bent. It argues that the participants have been wronged regardless. Trial volunteers — especially in early-stage Alzheimer’s, where cognitive decline is the condition being treated — are vulnerable subjects. They suffer from what the bioethicist Paul Appelbaum named the therapeutic misconception. This is the tendency of seriously ill research participants to believe that what is being done to them is being chosen for their personal benefit, the way a doctor’s prescription would be.

Therapeutic misconception. The therapeutic misconception is the false but natural belief that a research protocol is a form of personalized care. Patients in trials commonly do three things at once:

They underestimate the chance of being randomized to placebo.
They overestimate the chance of personal benefit.
They fail to register that the trial’s main purpose is general knowledge, not their own treatment.

The misconception is strongest in conditions like Alzheimer’s, where standard care offers little hope.

The structural harm, in this telling, is not that the trials produced bad medical outcomes. Many anti-amyloid trials are honestly run by investigators who had no idea anything was wrong with the preclinical literature. The harm is that the consent the participants gave was undermined upstream. They were told, in effect, that skilled independent researchers had reason to believe the drug might help. That claim turned out, in part, to be false. The wrong is a wrong of disclosure and of respect for persons. It is not erased by the fact that the trialists were themselves deceived. Here is where Kant’s idea of treating persons as ends, never merely as means, earns its keep. A research subject used to test a drug whose preclinical basis was faked has been used.


A reframing move: Stegenga and medical nihilism
A different kind of critic accepts most of the exchange and then shifts the frame. The philosopher of medicine Jacob Stegenga argues, in his book Medical Nihilism, that we should “have low confidence in the effectiveness of medical interventions” in general (Stegenga 2018). The reason is not that researchers cheat. It is that even honest clinical evidence is shaped by structural problems:

Small effect sizes.
Selective publication of positive results.
p-hacking — running analyses until something looks significant.
Surrogate endpoints that may not track real benefit.
Conflicts of interest from industry funding.
Career pressure to find positive results.

On Stegenga’s view, the Lesné case is not an outlier in an otherwise healthy science. It is a dramatic version of the field’s normal pathology.

The Medical Nihilism Argument

The honest evidence base for most medical interventions is distorted by small effect sizes, surrogate endpoints, selective publication, industry funding, and pressure to find positive results.
Once those distortions are properly discounted, the residual evidence shows that most medical interventions deliver small benefits relative to their harms and costs.
Therefore, we should have low confidence in the effectiveness of most medical interventions.


The disputed premise is the second. Skeptics of Stegenga grant that the distortions are real but deny that discounting for them leaves most interventions looking marginal.
The two anti-amyloid drugs to reach the U.S. market are a test case. The first was Biogen’s aducanumab, sold as Aduhelm. The FDA approved it in June 2021 over the near-unanimous objection of its own advisory committee. Three committee members resigned in protest. Key facts:

The approval used the accelerated approval pathway. This pathway lets a drug reach patients based on a surrogate endpoint — here, the clearance of amyloid plaques from PET scans — rather than direct evidence that patients live longer or think more clearly.
The two pivotal trials had been stopped early for futility.
A post hoc re-analysis of a subset of patients given the highest dose produced a slim positive signal. The FDA accepted it.
Biogen priced the drug at $56,000 per patient per year. The Medicare program raised Part B premiums for every beneficiary in the country to cover the projected cost.
By early 2024, Biogen had withdrawn Aduhelm from the market for commercial failure.


Accelerated approval and surrogate endpoints. Accelerated approval is an FDA pathway that lets a drug for a serious illness reach patients based on a surrogate endpoint — a measurable proxy (a blood test, a scan finding, a biomarker) “reasonably likely” to predict real clinical benefit. The maker must complete confirmatory trials after approval. The pathway speeds access in conditions like cancer and HIV. Its risk is that the surrogate sometimes fails to track what patients actually care about: surviving longer, thinking more clearly, walking without help.

Lecanemab (sold as Leqembi) and donanemab (Kisunla) are the successors. The FDA approved lecanemab in January 2023 and donanemab in July 2024. Their trials were not stopped early. Their data are real. The headline numbers:

They slow cognitive decline by about half a point on the eighteen-point Clinical Dementia Rating Sum of Boxes scale over eighteen months. Many neurologists openly doubt whether that difference matters in daily life.
They cause amyloid-related imaging abnormalities (ARIA) — brain swelling or small bleeds visible on MRI — in roughly one in five treated patients, with rare but documented deaths.
Annual cost runs to about $26,000 per patient. That figure does not include infusion, monitoring MRI, or clinic fees, all of which Medicare now covers.

If Stegenga is right, the bioethical question is not only “how do we catch the next Lesné?” It is “what should we do when even our non-fraudulent medicine routinely overpromises?” The amyloid program looks, on this view, like a field that absorbed faked data so smoothly because the rest of its evidence base was, even at its best, weaker than its advocates claimed. Aduhelm did not require fraud. It required an honest field willing to count amyloid clearance as good enough.
Stegenga’s prescription is what he calls gentle medicine. The default should lean toward less intervention. Aggressive treatment should be reserved for cases where the evidence base clears a high bar:

The Gentle Medicine Argument

When confidence in an intervention’s benefit is low and its harms are non-trivial, prescribing it does more to violate non-maleficence than withholding it does to violate beneficence.
For most medical interventions in their current evidence base, confidence in benefit is low and harms are non-trivial.
Therefore, medicine should default to less intervention, reserving aggressive treatment for cases where the evidence is unusually strong.


A defender of standard practice will press on the second premise. “Most” is doing a great deal of work. And the alternative to prescribing a marginal drug is sometimes prescribing nothing — itself a choice with costs. Some patients would have benefited. Family caregivers carry a heavy burden the drug was meant, however slightly, to ease. The argument does not, on its own, tell a memory-clinic nurse what to say to a frightened sixty-eight-year-old. It does suggest the conversation should start somewhere other than the brochure.
This is not a comforting frame. It implies that better misconduct policing — sharper image-forensics software, mandatory raw-data deposits, stronger journal retraction rules — addresses a real problem but not the deepest one. The deepest problem is the gap between what biomedical research can reliably deliver and what patients, regulators, and journalists are induced to expect from it.


The move that hasn’t ended
The institutional fallout has been steady:

June 2024. The original authors of the Aβ*56 paper agreed to retract it.
September 2024. The NIH found that Eliezer Masliah — until that month the leader of the agency’s Alzheimer’s program — had committed research misconduct. He was removed from his post (Piller 2025).
February 2025. The University of Minnesota concluded its inquiry. Lesné resigned.

None of these moves answers the live question.
The live question is what biomedical research should do now that the tools of image faking and the tools of image forensics are both becoming much more powerful. Generative AI can produce Western blots that look real to the eye. Forensic AI can scan an entire journal’s back catalog for duplications in minutes — Elisabeth Bik did her early work by hand. The arms race is real. It is being run on top of an incentive structure — publish-or-perish, novelty-or-irrelevance, single-laboratory secrecy — that the field has not seriously revised since the 1970s.
The parallel to Tuskegee is instructive but limited. Tuskegee produced direct, identifiable victims and a clear set of failures: no consent, no treatment, no public oversight. The Aβ*56 affair produces diffuse, statistical victims:

Alzheimer’s patients who enrolled in trials that should have been redesigned.
Families who paid out of pocket for drugs that should have been studied differently.
A generation of postdocs whose careers were built on a hypothesis that was overinvested in.

There is no single wronged person to apologize to. There is, instead, a field-level question about what the public is owed when it funds science. Not perfect results, but a system honest enough that its mistakes are recoverable. The question is whether we now have one.

title: Sylvain Lesné and the Aβ*56 Affair: Research Misconduct, Replication, and the Amyloid Hypothesis
text:
Discussion Questions

Write out Schrag’s Argument and the Robustness Reply in your own words — premises first, then the conclusion. The two arguments share one premise about what makes a clinical trial ethically OK to run. Identify it. Then say which side has the better read on what that premise requires.
The counter-reply says that patients in anti-amyloid trials were wronged even when their own trials were honestly run — because the science behind those trials had been corrupted upstream by Lesné’s fraud. Is this idea of “being wronged” too broad? If you accept it, where does it stop? If you reject it, why?
Stegenga’s medical nihilism says the Lesné case is just an extreme version of how biomedical research normally fails. Push back on this claim. Name one part of biomedical research that you think does work well, and explain why it survives Stegenga’s critique.
You are a nurse in a memory clinic. A patient with mild cognitive impairment asks you whether she should enroll in a new Alzheimer’s drug trial. She mentions news coverage of the “new Alzheimer’s drugs.” She has not read the Science article on the fraud. What do you say to her? What do you not say?
The 2006 Nature paper was retracted in 2024 — eighteen years after it was published, and only after a whistleblower (paid in part by stock-market short-sellers) brought it to public attention. Design one specific reform — at the journal, the university, the NIH, or the FDA — that would have caught the problem earlier. What trade-off would your reform force on the system?

title: Sylvain Lesné and the Aβ*56 Affair: Research Misconduct, Replication, and the Amyloid Hypothesis

Charles Piller, Doctored: Fraud, Arrogance, and Tragedy in the Quest to Cure Alzheimer’s (Atria, 2025) — book-length investigation.
Charles Piller, “Blots on a Field?”, Science, 21 July 2022 — the original exposé.
Jacob Stegenga, Medical Nihilism (Oxford, 2018), chapters 1–3 — the philosophical reframing.
The PubPeer entries on Lesné’s papers (pubpeer.com) — the actual flagged images, with public commentary.
John Ioannidis, “Why Most Published Research Findings Are False”, PLoS Medicine (2005) — the foundational replication-crisis paper.

title: Adriana Smith: Brain Death, Pregnancy, and the Limits of Fetal-Protection Law
text:
Who: Adriana Smith (1994–2025), a thirty-year-old registered nurse who lived in Atlanta and worked at Emory University Hospital.
What: In February 2025, Smith went to a hospital with severe headaches, was discharged, and the next morning was found by her boyfriend gasping for air. Multiple blood clots in her brain. Declared brain-dead at nine weeks pregnant. Emory University Hospital Midtown kept her on mechanical support for about four months — over her family’s stated objection — citing Georgia’s 2019 abortion statute. On June 13, 2025, her son Chance was delivered by emergency cesarean at roughly twenty-two weeks of gestation, weighing one pound thirteen ounces. Smith was removed from support four days later.
Where / When: Emory University Hospital Midtown, Atlanta, Georgia, February–June 2025.
Why it matters: The case tests three ideas at once:

Conscription of the dead — whether the state may use a dead body for someone else’s benefit.
The role of fetal-personhood law in driving hospital risk-aversion under post-Dobbs statutory ambiguity.
Arthur Caplan’s claim that prolonged somatic gestation in a brain-dead patient is medical experimentation rather than treatment — and so subject to research-ethics standards that no one in the case attempted to meet.

Pair with: Reproductive Ethics (Pregnancy, Autonomy, and Maternal-Fetal Conflict)

title: Adriana Smith: Brain Death, Pregnancy, and the Limits of Fetal-Protection Law
text:
The Case
Adriana Smith was thirty years old, a registered nurse, and nine weeks pregnant. In February 2025 she walked into Northside Hospital in Atlanta complaining of severe headaches. She was given medication and sent home. Sometime overnight, her boyfriend woke to the sound of her gasping for air. He called 911. She was taken to Emory University Hospital, where she worked. A CT scan revealed multiple blood clots in her brain. She deteriorated rapidly. Within hours, she met clinical criteria for whole-brain death.

Whole-brain death. A patient is whole-brain dead when there is irreversible loss of all functions of the entire brain, including the brainstem. Under the Uniform Determination of Death Act, adopted by Georgia and forty-eight other US states, whole-brain death is legal death — equivalent in all clinical, legal, and ethical respects to the cessation of cardiac and respiratory function. A brain-dead patient:

Is not “in a coma.”
Is not “unconscious.”
Is not “on life support” in the sense that the term ordinarily implies.

The patient has died. Mechanical ventilation, vasopressors, and tube feedings can sustain organ perfusion in the body of a person who is no longer alive.

Smith was transferred to Emory University Hospital Midtown, a separate facility within the same system. She was connected to mechanical ventilation. Her mother, April Newkirk, later told the local press what the hospital had said: Georgia law required continued somatic support because the fetus had detectable cardiac activity. The family was not given a choice. The plan, Newkirk said, was to maintain Smith on the ventilator until about thirty-two weeks of gestation. At that point, her son’s odds of survival outside the uterus would be reasonable.
The law the hospital was reading was the Living Infants Fairness and Equality Act, known as the LIFE Act, signed by Governor Brian Kemp in 2019.

The LIFE Act (O.C.G.A. § 16-12-141). Georgia’s 2019 statute prohibits most abortions after detection of “a human heartbeat” — typically around six weeks gestation. The statute defines an “unborn child” as a “member of the species Homo sapiens at any stage of development who is carried in the womb.” Crucially for what follows, the LIFE Act amends Georgia’s definitions of personhood and dependency in ways that extend far beyond the abortion question. A fetus with cardiac activity:

May be claimed as a dependent on Georgia state income tax.
May be the subject of a wrongful-death action.

The legal scholar Mary Ziegler has argued that the LIFE Act is structurally a fetal-personhood statute, with the abortion prohibition as one of its several legal consequences (State of Georgia 2019).

For three months, Smith remained on the ventilator while the fetus continued to develop. Reports later said imaging during this period had detected fetal abnormalities. The family was not, by their account, offered options for stopping. In mid-May, with Smith about twenty-two weeks into the pregnancy, April Newkirk went to the local Atlanta press.
The story was national within forty-eight hours. International within a week. It collected a predictable coalition:

Abortion-rights advocates.
Reproductive-justice organizations.
Civil-liberties lawyers.

It also collected a less predictable one:

Cole Muzio, of the Christian Frontline Policy Council, publicly stated that Georgia’s abortion law did not, in fact, require what Emory was doing.
State Senator Nabilah Islam Parkes, a Democrat, wrote to Republican Attorney General Christopher Carr asking for the state’s official reading of the statute.

The Attorney General’s office responded in writing.

Carr’s letter, May 2025. “There is nothing in the LIFE Act that requires medical professionals to keep a woman on life support after brain death. Removing life support is not an action ‘with the purpose to terminate a pregnancy.’” The letter was widely reported and not contested by Georgia legislative leadership. House Republican leadership separately released a statement saying that the law “is completely irrelevant” to Smith’s case and that “any implication otherwise is just another gross mischaracterization of the intent of this legislation” (Office of the Attorney General of Georgia 2025).

Emory did not change course. The hospital declined to comment on the legal opinion in public. State Senator Ed Setzler, the original sponsor of the LIFE Act, publicly backed Emory’s reading. Bioethicists went on record in agreement with Carr’s office. Three were especially prominent:

Thaddeus Pope at Mitchell Hamline.
Lois Shepherd at the University of Virginia.
Mary Ziegler at UC Davis.

On June 13, 2025, Smith was taken to surgery for an emergency cesarean. Her son, named Chance, was delivered weighing one pound thirteen ounces, classified by the World Health Organization as “extremely preterm.” On June 15, Smith’s family observed what would have been her thirty-first birthday at a private church service. On June 17, four days after Chance was born, Emory removed Smith from mechanical support. A funeral was held later that month.
By the time it ended, what had happened at Emory between February and June was the longest documented somatic gestation in a brain-dead patient in the medical literature. There is essentially one prior case approaching its length — Germany, decades earlier, outcome unreported. The procedure was, in every way, unprecedented:

There were no protocols.
There was no advance directive guidance from the patient.
There was no published institutional policy at Emory.
There was no IRB review of the regimen.

There was a hospital lawyer reading an ambiguous statute. There was a family that was not given an option. The question Smith’s case raises is not whether anyone behaved illegally. The Attorney General said the hospital was not legally compelled. The question is what category of medical action the hospital was performing for four months, and what ethical rules apply to it.

title: Adriana Smith: Brain Death, Pregnancy, and the Limits of Fetal-Protection Law
section: Between the Statute and the Bedside
text:
Between the Statute and the Bedside
To understand how the AG’s letter failed to change Emory’s behavior, three things have to be visible at once:

The legal machinery that runs hospital decision-making in the post-Dobbs environment.
The people whose voices were not in the room when the decisions were made.
The actual nursing labor that the four-month interval consisted of.

The legal machinery. An attorney general’s opinion letter binds no county prosecutor. It creates no insurance defense. A hospital’s malpractice insurers do not underwrite the claim that “the Attorney General said it was fine.” The exposure picture for Emory’s lawyers looked like this:

Individual physicians in Georgia face up to ten years in prison under the LIFE Act if a county prosecutor later decides they aided a prohibited act.
The wrongful-death risk to the institution under the same statute’s personhood reading has no cap.
The Alabama Supreme Court’s 2024 LePage decision — frozen embryos as “extrauterine children” under the wrongful-death statute — is the same legal machinery running in the same direction.

Put those facts together. Fetal-personhood logic plus harsh civil and criminal exposure makes hospital lawyers read every ambiguity against the patient. The contrast with Damla Karsan’s choice in Cox v. Texas is exact and telling. Karsan put her own name on the lawsuit, knowing the criminal risk. She was a single physician acting on her own conscience. The Emory decision was made by an institution’s lawyers acting on its risk model. Same statute, different actor, opposite outcome.
The voices that were not in the room. This is what relational autonomy was built to notice. Susan Sherwin’s question was developed for cases like this one: who is in the room with her, who is not, and what conditions shape the choice that is being offered? April Newkirk and the rest of Smith’s family were offered a single option. It was framed as legally required. It came from people whose institutional incentives ran in only one direction. They were not told two important things:

The Attorney General would, weeks later, publicly reject the hospital’s reading.
Prior precedent — In re A.C. (1990) and the Florida appellate court’s rejection of the order in Burton — settled that the modern professional consensus is to honor refusal “in virtually all cases.”

That precedent presumed a living patient with capacity. Smith breaks the presumption. Under the doctrine, the family inherits the authority that A.C. and Burton protected. They exercised it. The hospital’s counsel overrode it. That override is not the conversation about autonomy that the post-A.C. doctrine had spent thirty-five years building.
The bedside. The four-month interval consisted of intensive nursing care:

Ventilator management.
Tube feedings.
Pressure-injury prevention on a body that does not move.
Antibiotic management of the urinary-tract and respiratory infections that come with prolonged immobility.
Hormonal and electrolyte support of a pregnancy in a body whose endocrine signaling no longer originated from a living brain.

Many of the nurses providing this care were Smith’s own former Emory colleagues. None of the interventions, in the dose and duration applied to her, has an evidence base. The same pattern shows up in ordinary prescription medication in pregnancy. Roughly ninety-five percent of drugs in common use lack adequate pregnancy-specific safety data. The reason: pregnant women were systematically excluded from the trials. The Smith case extends that pattern in a sharpened form. Somatic gestation in a brain-dead patient has no evidence base for three connected reasons:

The practice was never studied.
No IRB ever approved a study.
No protocol was ever written.

Four months of intensive care, in service of a fetus whose imaging raised concerns, using a regimen no one had tested. That is exclusion-as-harm in its most concentrated form. It is also the bridge to Caplan’s argument, which the next section reconstructs.

title: Adriana Smith: Brain Death, Pregnancy, and the Limits of Fetal-Protection Law
text:
The Argument Adriana Smith Started
Smith’s case is unusual in modern bioethics. Most cases at this level have a dominant frame and a single main objection. Smith produces four sharply distinct moves:

A hospital risk-management argument.
A statutory-interpretation reply.
A conscription objection from the philosophy of postmortem pregnancy.
Caplan’s reframing of the whole thing as unconsented human research.

A fifth move is empirical. It asks who absorbs the ambiguity when these four positions disagree.

The Hospital’s Argument
Emory has not published its reasoning. What follows is the most charitable reconstruction of the position the hospital evidently took, defended by State Senator Setzler and others.

The Hospital’s Argument

The Georgia LIFE Act treats a fetus with detectable cardiac activity as a legal person, with rights protected by the state.
Withdrawing mechanical support from a brain-dead pregnant patient with detectable fetal cardiac activity arguably causes the death of that legal person.
A hospital practicing in a jurisdiction with severe criminal penalties for the wrongful death of a person is obligated to interpret ambiguous statutes conservatively to avoid criminal exposure for the institution and its physicians.
Therefore, continued somatic support was the legally and professionally required course of action.


The single disputed premise is the third. This is the institutional risk move that lets hospital lawyers do the work the statute does not. It is the premise the AG’s letter directly contests. It is also the premise that turns out to do most of the real work in post-Dobbs medicine.


The Statutory Reply
The Attorney General’s letter, supported by the legal scholarship that immediately surrounded it, is the cleanest legal answer the case received.

The Statutory Reply (Carr, Pope, Shepherd, Ziegler)

The LIFE Act prohibits abortion, defined as an act undertaken with the purpose of terminating a pregnancy.
Withdrawing mechanical support from a brain-dead patient has no such purpose; the patient is already dead, and the action being taken is the cessation of futile somatic interventions.
Therefore, the LIFE Act does not require, and does not even speak to, the question of what Emory should have done.


The soft spot in this reply is not its legal reasoning, which appears to be correct. It is the reply’s hidden assumption: that the legal answer settles the institutional question. The hospital’s lawyers were not asking what the AG believed the LIFE Act required. They were asking a different question. What could an aggressive prosecutor — or a wrongful-death plaintiff — persuade a Georgia jury that it required? The gap between those two questions is what produced the four-month interval.


Lindemann Nelson and the Conscription of the Dead
Whatever the law said or did not say, what Emory did is the kind of thing the rest of medicine has long forbidden. The philosopher Hilde Lindemann Nelson wrote about postmortem pregnancy in Bioethics in 1994. She gave the principle its sharpest form: we do not, in any other context, conscript the dead (Lindemann Nelson 1994).

The Conscription Argument (Lindemann Nelson)

In every other medical context, we do not use a dead person’s body for the benefit of a third party without explicit prior consent or family authorization — not for organ donation, not for tissue research, not for medical-school training, not for life-extending therapy of another patient.
Adriana Smith was a dead person whose body was used for the benefit of a third party — her fetus — without explicit prior consent or family authorization.
Therefore, what was done to Smith was a form of conscription that the rest of medicine forbids, and that the law has never explicitly authorized.


The point is not that the fetus had no interests. Lindemann Nelson concedes that the fetus may have interests. The point is that the rest of medicine requires consent to use a dead body even when third-party interests are powerful:

Even when organ recipients on transplant waiting lists are dying.
Even when the social benefit of an autopsy or a teaching session is enormous.

Smith’s case is the one place where that requirement collapsed.


Caplan and Experimentation
The fourth move is the most original. For a nursing-student audience, it may be the most useful. Arthur Caplan, writing in Bioethics Today in May 2025 while the case was still unfolding, argued that the framing was wrong from the start (Caplan 2025). The question was not whether Emory was permitted to continue treatment. The question was what category of medical action four-month somatic gestation in a brain-dead patient really is. Caplan’s answer: it is not treatment at all.

The Experimentation Argument (Caplan)

A medical practice with essentially no evidence base, no established protocols, and no published prior outcomes is, in the relevant ethical sense, research rather than care.
Research on human subjects requires informed consent — from the subject directly, or from those legally authorized to give surrogate consent on the subject’s behalf.
Prolonged somatic gestation in a brain-dead patient (one prior case in the literature, no protocols, no IRB review) meets the criteria for research; no such consent was sought or given for Smith.
Therefore, what Emory did was unconsented human experimentation, and the modern research-ethics regime forbids it regardless of what any abortion statute does or does not require.


This move opens onto the research-ethics framework students saw earlier in the course. That framework included the therapeutic misconception — the tendency of patients to mistake research for personalized treatment. In Smith’s case, the misconception ran in the opposite direction. The hospital framed an experimental procedure as ordinary obstetric care. That framing kept the family from ever being asked the question they would have been asked at an IRB: will you participate in this research? The answer, by the family’s account, would have been no. The question was not posed.
The vulnerability framework returns here too. A brain-dead patient is the limit case of vulnerable subject:

Incapable of consent.
Incapable of withdrawing.
Incapable of objecting.

Subjecting such a patient to a four-month experimental regimen without surrogate consent is a research-ethics violation of an order not seen in American medicine since the post-Tuskegee reforms of the 1970s.


The Move That Hasn’t Ended: Race and the Distribution of Ambiguity
The fifth move is empirical. It asks who, in practice, absorbs the cost of statutes whose enforcement is uncertain. The Georgia LIFE Act is, on its face, race-neutral. The outcomes it produces are not.
Adriana Smith was Black. Emory University Hospital Midtown serves a substantially Black patient population. Hospitals in Georgia that operate under the same statute but serve wealthier, whiter patient populations have not, to date, produced a comparable case. The civil-rights organization SisterSong had been the lead plaintiff in earlier litigation against the LIFE Act. They responded to Smith’s case by building an altar called “Trust Black Women” (SisterSong Women of Color Reproductive Justice Collective 2025). The choice of phrase is precise. The case raised exactly the question Dorothy Roberts framed in Killing the Black Body nearly thirty years earlier. The reproductive choices of Black women — and, here, the disposition of a Black woman’s body after death — are the choices American medicine has been most willing to override (Roberts 1997).
Mary Ziegler, in interviews after Carr’s letter became public, drew the structural point. Post-Dobbs medicine routinely involves hospital lawyers reading ambiguous statutes against the most aggressive reading a prosecutor might offer. That reading is not random:

Hospitals most willing to make conservative interpretive moves are the hospitals with the fewest resources to absorb litigation risk — public-payer-heavy hospitals serving low-income communities.
Hospitals serving wealthier, whiter patient populations have more freedom to interpret ambiguity in the patient’s favor.

The same statute therefore produces different outcomes depending on whose body it lands on.
The sharpened question Smith’s case leaves open is not whether any of the four arguments above is right. It is whether the legal architecture that produced the case is one that the rest of American medicine can live with in cases yet to come. That architecture has three parts:

A fetal-personhood statute.
An ambiguous criminal exposure.
A hospital legal department deciding which way to read it.

The case is recent enough that the answer is unsettled. It will not stay unsettled for long.

title: Adriana Smith: Brain Death, Pregnancy, and the Limits of Fetal-Protection Law
text:
Discussion Questions

Write out The Hospital’s Argument and The Statutory Reply in your own words — premises first, then the conclusion. The two arguments share one premise and disagree about another. Identify both. Then choose: is the disagreement between them best described as legal (about what the LIFE Act actually says), institutional (about how a hospital’s lawyers handle legal uncertainty), or political (about which reading any actor in the system has reason to make public)? Defend your choice.
Caplan’s argument turns on one claim: that what Emory did to Smith was research, not care. Push back on that claim. What features have to be present for a medical practice to count as “experimental” in a way that triggers research-ethics rules? Does every new medical intervention without an evidence base count as experimental? If not, what makes Smith’s case different from the rest?
Lindemann Nelson says we do not, in any other context, conscript the dead. Pick one current medical practice that comes close to conscripting the dead — postmortem organ donation under an opt-out system, continued ventilation while a family decides, postmortem sperm retrieval, or another of your choice. Explain how it differs from what was done to Smith. Does the difference confirm Lindemann Nelson’s principle or weaken it?
You are the charge nurse on the night shift in a Georgia ICU. A patient was declared brain-dead this morning at eleven weeks pregnant. The team has gathered to discuss whether to “continue support pending legal review.” The patient’s advance directive declines life-sustaining treatment. Her sister, the legal decision-maker, requests withdrawal. The hospital’s lawyer has not yet returned the team’s call. What do you say in the meeting? Which of the four arguments from the case is doing the work in what you say?
Georgia’s LIFE Act is, on its face, race-neutral. The outcomes it produces — including Smith’s — are not. The chain runs from statute → hospital legal counsel → individual physician decision → patient and family encounter. At which point in that chain does race make the most difference? Propose one specific reform that would address race-driven differences at that point. Then say what your reform would not fix.

title: Adriana Smith: Brain Death, Pregnancy, and the Limits of Fetal-Protection Law
text:
Further Reading

Arthur Caplan, “The Adriana Smith Case Unfolding in Atlanta Raises Many Questions,” Bioethics Today, May 22, 2025. Three pages. Read it.
Hilde Lindemann Nelson, “The Architect and the Bee: Some Reflections on Postmortem Pregnancy,” Bioethics 8:3 (1994), 247–267. The philosophical foundation for the conscription objection.
Dorothy Roberts, Killing the Black Body (1997), especially chapters 4 and 6 on the criminalization of Black women’s reproductive choices.
Mary Ziegler, Personhood: The New Civil War over Reproduction (Yale, 2025) — for the legal-historical context of fetal-personhood statutes.
The Wikipedia article “Adriana Smith pregnancy case” — unusually well-sourced; a serviceable factual reference.

Lindemann Nelson, Hilde. 1994. “The Architect and the Bee: Some Reflections on Postmortem Pregnancy.” Bioethics 8 (3): 247–67. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1467-8519.1994.tb00257.x.
